[tool] Agent({"description":"Design implementation plan","subagent_type":"Plan","prompt":"Design a detailed implementation plan for refactoring the guard tokenizer to properly handle redirect operators. This is a single-file refactor of `/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs` (3914 lines, 412 tests).\n\n## Context & Problem\n\nThe guard is a Rust binary that statically analyzes shell commands to block dangerous operations (push to main, redirect to /workspace, etc.). It has a single-pass POSIX-aligned tokenizer that produces `Token::Word(String)` and `Token::Operator(String)`, then groups words into `Segment` structs (splitting on operators).\n\n**The fundamental bug**: `>`, `<`, `>>`, and all redirect operators are treated as **word characters**, not operators. This means:\n- `echo > /workspace/file` (real redirect) → `[Word(\"echo\"), Word(\">\"), Word(\"/workspace/file\")]`\n- `echo \\> /workspace/file` (NOT a redirect) → `[Word(\"echo\"), Word(\">\"), Word(\"/workspace/file\")]`\n- These are IDENTICAL — the tokenizer cannot distinguish them\n\nAdditionally:\n- `echo>/workspace/file` (no-space redirect) → `[Word(\"echo>/workspace/file\")]` — not split at all\n- `echo >| /workspace/file` (clobber) → `[Word(\"echo\"), Word(\">\"), Operator(\"|\")]` — `|` steals from `>|`\n- `echo >&2` (fd dup) → `[Word(\"echo\"), Word(\">\"), Operator(\"&\")]` — `&` steals from `>&`\n\nThe redirect check compensates with string heuristics (line 1037: `tok == \">\"`, line 1054: `tok.find(\">/workspace/\")`), but these are fundamentally unsound.\n\n**Every reference implementation** (bash, dash, zsh, conch-parser, ShellCheck, shellharden) treats `>` and `<` as metacharacters that terminate words and produce operator tokens.\n\n## Current Architecture\n\n**Tokenizer** (`tokenize_inner`, lines 73-499):\n- Main loop processes chars sequentially\n- Handles: quotes (`'`, `\"`), backslash escaping, `$()` and backtick substitution, heredoc `<<`, operators `&&`, `||`, `|`, `;`, `&`, `(`, `)`, `\\n`\n- `>` and `<` fall through to default word-character handler (line 484-490)\n- Backslash handler (line 273-288): consumes next char into word, `continue`s — already correct for `\\>`\n\n**Segment** (lines 587-757):\n- `pub struct Segment { pub tokens: Vec<String> }` — loses Word/Operator distinction\n- 9 methods: `effective_command()`, `git_subcmd()`, `is_git()`, `has_arg()`, `has_short_flag()`, `has_token_starting_with()`, `has_token_containing()`, `targets_worktree_root()`, `has_workspace_path()`\n- All operate on string values — they match command names, flags, paths\n\n**`parse_segments_inner`** (lines 766-831):\n- Splits tokens on ALL `Token::Operator` — operators become segment boundaries, discarded\n- Collects `Token::Word` strings into segments\n- Recursively parses `bash -c`, `eval`, `$()`, backticks\n\n**`check_bash`** (lines 900-1075):\n- 10 checks using Segment methods\n- Redirect check (lines 1028-1072): string comparison `tok == \">\"` + `tok.find(\">/workspace/\")`\n\n**Tests**: 412 total (121 L1, 12 L2, 76 L2b, 173 L3 + regression)\n\n## Design Constraints\n\n1. **All work in one file** — `lib.rs` is the only file to modify\n2. **No external dependencies** — architecture constraint\n3. **Fail-open on parse errors** — unmatched quotes degrade to whitespace split\n4. **Must not break** any currently-correct behavior (all 412 tests except the known false positive `l3_redirect_escaped_gt_blocks`)\n5. **Match bash behavior** — our target shell is bash 5.2\n6. **The backslash, single-quote, and double-quote handlers already correctly prevent `>` from reaching operator detection** — this is the key invariant that makes the fix clean\n\n## Required Changes\n\n### Layer 1: Tokenizer — Emit redirect operators\n\nAdd handling for `>` and `<` as operators in the main loop. Must be placed BEFORE the `&` and `|` handlers to prevent them from stealing chars from `>&`, `>|`.\n\nWhen we see unquoted `>`:\n- Peek ahead: `>` → `Operator(\">>\")`, advance 2\n- Peek ahead: `&` → `Operator(\">&\")`, advance 2  \n- Peek ahead: `|` → `Operator(\">|\")`, advance 2\n- Else → `Operator(\">\")`, advance 1\n\nWhen we see unquoted `<` (and next char is NOT `<`, which already triggers heredoc):\n- Peek ahead: `&` → `Operator(\"<&\")`, advance 2\n- Peek ahead: `>` → `Operator(\"<>\")`, advance 2\n- Else → `Operator(\"<\")`, advance 1\n\nThe existing heredoc handler (`<<`) at line 414 must remain and take priority over the new `<` handler.\n\nNo-space case (`echo>/tmp`): When building a word and we hit `>`, the word handler won't fire (the `>` branch catches it first). The accumulated word is flushed, then `>` is emitted as operator. This naturally splits `echo>/tmp` into `[Word(\"echo\"), Operator(\">\"), Word(\"/tmp\")]`.\n\nFd number case (`2>/dev/null`): The `2` is already accumulated as a word. When `>` is encountered, the word `\"2\"` is flushed, then `Operator(\">\")` is emitted. Result: `[Word(\"2\"), Operator(\">\"), Word(\"/dev/null\")]`. No special fd handling needed.\n\n### Layer 2: Segment — Carry Token type, distinguish operator categories\n\n**Change `Segment.tokens` from `Vec<String>` to `Vec<Token>`.**\n\n**In `parse_segments_inner`, distinguish two operator categories:**\n- Control operators split segments: `&&`, `||`, `|`, `;`, `&`, `\\n`, `(`, `)`\n- Redirect operators stay IN the segment: `>`, `>>`, `<`, `<&`, `>&`, `>|`, `<>`\n\nThis means `parse_segments_inner` needs:\n```rust\nToken::Operator(op) => {\n    if is_control_operator(op) {\n        // flush current segment\n    } else {\n        // redirect operator — keep in segment\n        current.push(Token::Operator(op.clone()));\n    }\n}\nToken::Word(w) => {\n    current.push(Token::Word(w.clone()));\n}\n```\n\n**Update all Segment methods** to extract strings from `Token::Word`:\n- Add a helper: `fn word_at(&self, i: usize) -> Option<&str>` that returns the string only if `tokens[i]` is `Word`\n- Methods that iterate tokens need to skip `Operator` tokens or match on `Word` only\n- `effective_command()`, `git_subcmd()` should skip redirect operators (they're not commands or flags)\n- `has_arg()`, `has_short_flag()` should only match `Word` tokens\n- Path-checking methods (`has_workspace_path`, `targets_worktree_root`) should only check `Word` tokens\n\n**Recursive `bash -c` / `eval` paths**: Extract strings from `Word` tokens only when building the inner command string.\n\n### Layer 3: Check — Type-safe redirect detection\n\n**Replace the entire redirect check** (lines 1028-1072) with:\n```rust\nfor seg in &segments {\n    for j in 0..seg.tokens.len() {\n        if let Token::Operator(op) = &seg.tokens[j] {\n            // Only single > is a write redirect (>> is append, allowed)\n            if op == \">\" {\n                if let Some(Token::Word(path)) = seg.tokens.get(j + 1) {\n                    if path.starts_with(\"/workspace/\")\n                        && !path.starts_with(\"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/\")\n                    {\n                        return Some(\"...\".to_string());\n                    }\n                }\n            }\n        }\n    }\n}\n```\n\nThis eliminates:\n- The `tok == \">\"` string comparison (now type-safe)\n- The `tok.find(\">/workspace/\")` no-space heuristic (no-space is now properly tokenized)\n- The `is_simple_redirect` / `is_append` JSON false-positive guards (unnecessary with typed tokens)\n- The `l3_redirect_escaped_gt_blocks` false positive (escaped `>` is `Word(\">\")`, not `Operator(\">\"`)\n\n### Test Updates\n\n**L1 tests to update** (2 tests):\n- `l1_redirect_gt`: `echo hello > /tmp/file` → change `w(\">\")` to `op(\">\")`\n- `l1_redirect_append`: `echo hello >> /tmp/file` → change `w(\">>\")` to `op(\">>\")`\n\n**New L1 tests** (~15):\n- `>|` → `op(\">|\")`\n- `>&` → `op(\">&\")`\n- `<` → `op(\"<\")`\n- `<>` → `op(\"<>\")`\n- `<&` → `op(\"<&\")`\n- No-space: `echo>/tmp` → `[w(\"echo\"), op(\">\"), w(\"/tmp\")]`\n- Fd redirect: `2>/dev/null` → `[w(\"2\"), op(\">\"), w(\"/dev/null\")]`\n- Escaped: `echo \\>` → `[w(\"echo\"), w(\">\")]` (Word, not Operator)\n- Quoted: `echo \">\"` → `[w(\"echo\"), w(\">\")]`\n- `echo '>'` → `[w(\"echo\"), w(\">\")]`\n- `\"echo\">foo` → `[w(\"echo\"), op(\">\"), w(\"foo\")]`\n- `echo 2\\>/dev/null` → `[w(\"echo\"), w(\"2>/dev/null\")]` (backslash prevents split)\n\n**L2 tests to update** (11 `segs()` tests):\n- `segs()` helper needs updating — it currently returns `Vec<Vec<String>>`\n- Either change to return `Vec<Vec<Token>>` or add a `word_strings()` helper\n- Redirect operators now appear inside segments, not as boundaries\n- New L2 tests: `segs(\"echo > /tmp/file\")` → 1 segment containing `[w(\"echo\"), op(\">\"), w(\"/tmp\")]`\n- `segs(\"echo > /tmp && cat < /other\")` → 2 segments, each with redirect operators inside\n\n**L2b method tests** (76 tests using `mk_seg()`):\n- `mk_seg()` currently takes `&[&str]` and builds `Vec<String>`\n- Change to build `Vec<Token>` with all inputs as `Token::Word`\n- These tests don't involve redirects, so they stay as word-only segments\n\n**L3 redirect tests** (~20 tests):\n- `l3_redirect_escaped_gt_blocks` → **changes from BLOCK to ALLOW** (the false positive we're fixing)\n- `l3_redirect_quoted_gt_allow` → stays ALLOW (correct)\n- `l3_redirect_nospace_block` → stays BLOCK (now properly tokenized)\n- `l3_redirect_nospace_in_json_allow` → stays ALLOW (quoted `>` stays as word)\n- Most L3 tests stay the same (behavior unchanged), just the mechanism differs\n\n**New L3 tests** (~10):\n- `echo \\> /workspace/file` → ALLOW (the fix!)\n- `echo \">\" /workspace/file` → ALLOW (already works, now architecturally correct)\n- `echo >| /workspace/file` → decide: BLOCK or ALLOW? (clobber redirect)\n- `echo >&2` → ALLOW (fd dup, not a path redirect)\n- `echo <> /workspace/file` → decide: BLOCK? (read-write)\n- `\"echo\">/workspace/file` → BLOCK (properly tokenized now)\n\n**Bash ground truth tests** (~20):\n- A new test section that verifies our tokenizer agrees with bash behavior for all the edge cases discovered during audit\n\n## Implementation Order\n\n1. **Commit 1: Add redirect operator recognition to tokenizer + L1 tests**\n   - Add `>`, `<` handlers before `&`, `|` handlers in main loop\n   - Multi-char greedy matching for `>>`, `>&`, `>|`, `<&`, `<>`\n   - Update existing L1 redirect tests\n   - Add new L1 tests for all redirect operator types + escaped/quoted cases\n   - Run L1 tests (L2/L3 will break)\n\n2. **Commit 2: Update Segment to carry Vec<Token>, distinguish control vs redirect operators**\n   - Change `Segment.tokens` to `Vec<Token>`\n   - Add `is_control_operator()` helper\n   - Update `parse_segments_inner` to keep redirect operators in segments\n   - Add `word_at()` helper and `words()` iterator on Segment\n   - Update all 9 Segment methods to work with `Vec<Token>`\n   - Update `segs()`, `mk_seg()`, `ec()`, `gs()` test helpers\n   - Update all L2 and L2b tests\n   - Run L1+L2 tests (L3 may still break)\n\n3. **Commit 3: Rewrite redirect check + update L3 tests**\n   - Replace redirect check with type-safe operator matching\n   - Remove the no-space heuristic and is_simple_redirect guard\n   - Fix `l3_redirect_escaped_gt_blocks` to expect ALLOW\n   - Update all L3 redirect tests\n   - Add new L3 tests for clobber, dup fd, read-write\n   - Add bash ground truth test section\n\n4. **Commit 4: Verification + cleanup**\n   - Run full test suite\n   - Run integration tests (`scripts/test-hooks.sh`)\n   - Run bypass regression tests\n   - Build release binary and verify hooks work end-to-end\n   - Clean up any commented-out code, update doc comments\n\nProvide this as a structured plan with clear acceptance criteria for each commit."})

[tool] Write({"file_path":"/home/node/.claude/plans/hidden-discovering-brooks.md","content":"# Guard Tokenizer: Redirect Operator Refactor\n\n## Context\n\nThe guard's tokenizer treats `>` and `<` as word characters, not operators. This means `echo > /workspace/file` (real redirect) and `echo \\> /workspace/file` (literal `>`) produce identical token streams — both yield `[Word(\"echo\"), Word(\">\"), Word(\"/workspace/file\")]`. The redirect check compensates with string heuristics (`tok == \">\"`, `tok.find(\">/workspace/\")`), but these are fundamentally unsound: they create a confirmed false positive (`\\>` blocked) and require fragile guards against JSON false positives.\n\nEvery reference implementation — bash (`shellmeta()`), dash (`CSPCL` table), zsh, conch-parser (`Token::Great`), ShellCheck (`T_Greater`), shellharden (`!is_word()`) — treats `>` and `<` as metacharacters that terminate words and produce operator tokens. Our tokenizer is the outlier.\n\n**Goal**: Make `>`, `>>`, `<`, `>&`, `>|`, `<&`, `<>` proper `Token::Operator` values, carry `Token` type through `Segment`, and replace the redirect check with type-safe operator matching. The result should be a clean, coherent whole — not a layer of fixes on top of the existing design.\n\n## Architecture Overview (Post-Refactor)\n\n```\nInput: \"echo hello > /workspace/file && cat < /tmp/x\"\n\nL1 Tokenizer: [W(\"echo\"), W(\"hello\"), OP(\">\"), W(\"/workspace/file\"), OP(\"&&\"), W(\"cat\"), OP(\"<\"), W(\"/tmp/x\")]\n\nL2 Segments (split on CONTROL operators only):\n  Segment 1: [W(\"echo\"), W(\"hello\"), OP(\">\"), W(\"/workspace/file\")]\n  Segment 2: [W(\"cat\"), OP(\"<\"), W(\"/tmp/x\")]\n\nL3 Checks:\n  Segment 1: is_git(\"push\")? No. Redirect check: OP(\">\") + \"/workspace/...\" → BLOCK\n```\n\nTwo operator categories:\n- **Control operators** split segments: `&&`, `||`, `|`, `;`, `&`, `\\n`, `(`, `)`\n- **Redirect operators** stay in segments: `>`, `>>`, `<`, `<&`, `>&`, `>|`, `<>`\n\nKey safety invariant (already correct, unchanged): Backslash handler (line 273) and quote handlers (lines 180, 196) consume `>` into the word and `continue` before any operator detection runs. `\\>`, `\">\"`, `'>'` all produce `Word(\">\")`, never `Operator(\">\")`.\n\n## File\n\n`/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs` — single file, all changes here.\n\n---\n\n## Commit 1: Emit redirect operators from tokenizer\n\n### Tokenizer changes (main loop, lines 73-499)\n\n**Insert `>` and `<` handlers** between the heredoc `<<` handler (line 482) and the current comment at line 484. Delete the comment \"< and > are NOT operators for segment purposes\".\n\n**`>` handler** — greedy peek-ahead matching (must appear BEFORE `&` and `|` handlers to prevent `>&` and `>|` from being stolen):\n- `>>` → `Operator(\">>\")`\n- `>&` → `Operator(\">&\")`\n- `>|` → `Operator(\">|\")`\n- `>` alone → `Operator(\">\")`\n- Flush any accumulated word first\n\n**`<` handler** — for single `<` only (heredoc `<<` already consumed above):\n- `<&` → `Operator(\"<&\")`\n- `<>` → `Operator(\"<>\")`\n- `<` alone → `Operator(\"<\")`\n- Flush any accumulated word first\n\n**Order in main loop becomes**: heredoc body → `\\r` → whitespace → `#` → `\\n` → `'` → `\"` → `\\` → `$()` → `` ` `` → **`>`** → heredoc `<<` → **`<`** → `&` → `|` → `;` → `(` → `)` → default\n\nWait — the `>` handler must come before the heredoc `<<` check? No. The heredoc check at line 414 is `ch == '<' && chars[i+1] == '<'`. The `>` handler only fires on `ch == '>'`. These don't conflict. But the `<` handler fires on `ch == '<'` and must come AFTER the heredoc check (which also fires on `<`). The heredoc check consumes `<<` and `continue`s, so single `<` falls through to the new handler. Correct order:\n\n1. `>` handler (new) — before `&` and `|`\n2. heredoc `<<` handler (existing, line 414) — checks `ch == '<' && chars[i+1] == '<'`\n3. `<` handler (new) — after heredoc, catches single `<`, `<&`, `<>`\n4. `&` handler (existing)\n5. `|` handler (existing)\n\n### L1 test updates\n\n**Update 2 existing tests:**\n- `l1_redirect_gt`: `w(\">\")` → `op(\">\")`\n- `l1_redirect_append`: `w(\">>\")` → `op(\">>\")`\n\n**Add ~14 new L1 tests** in section 1.13:\n- All redirect operator types: `>`, `>>`, `>|`, `>&`, `<`, `<&`, `<>`\n- No-space variants: `echo>/tmp`, `2>/dev/null`, `echo>>/tmp`\n- Escaped/quoted variants: `\\>`, `\">\"`, `'>'` → all produce `Word(\">\")`\n- Mixed: `\"echo\">/tmp` → `[Word(\"echo\"), Operator(\">\"), Word(\"/tmp\")]`\n- `2\\>/dev/null` → `[Word(\"echo\"), Word(\"2>/dev/null\")]` (backslash prevents split)\n- `>&2` → `[Operator(\">&\"), Word(\"2\")]`\n\n### Acceptance criteria\n- All L1 tests pass\n- L2/L3 tests expected to break (redirect tokens changed from Word to Operator)\n\n---\n\n## Commit 2: Segment carries `Vec<Token>`, control vs redirect split\n\n### Struct change\n```rust\npub struct Segment {\n    pub tokens: Vec<Token>,\n}\n```\n\n### New helper\n```rust\nfn is_control_operator(op: &str) -> bool {\n    matches!(op, \"&&\" | \"||\" | \"|\" | \";\" | \"&\" | \"\\n\" | \"(\" | \")\")\n}\n```\n\n### `parse_segments_inner` update\n- Control operators → flush segment (same as today)\n- Redirect operators → `current.push(tok.clone())` (keep in segment)\n- Words → `current.push(tok.clone())`\n\n### Segment method updates\n\nAdd two helpers to reduce boilerplate:\n```rust\nimpl Segment {\n    fn word_str(&self, i: usize) -> Option<&str> { ... }\n    fn word_indices(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = (usize, &str)> { ... }\n}\n```\n\nUpdate all 9 methods to:\n1. Skip `Token::Operator` variants when scanning for commands, flags, paths\n2. Use `word_str()` or pattern matching where they currently index `self.tokens` as strings\n3. `effective_command()` and `git_subcmd()` skip operators in their scan loops\n4. `has_arg()`, `has_short_flag()`, `has_token_*()`, `targets_worktree_root()`, `has_workspace_path()` — match on `Token::Word` only\n\n### Recursive parsing updates (`bash -c`, `eval`)\n- Find `-c` flag: match on `Token::Word(w) if w == \"-c\"` instead of `t == \"-c\"`\n- Extract inner command string: from `Token::Word` only\n- `eval` args: filter to `Token::Word` values before joining\n\n### Test helper updates\n- `segs()` → filters to Word tokens only (preserves `Vec<Vec<String>>` return type, existing assertions unchanged)\n- `mk_seg()`, `ec()`, `gs()` → wrap inputs as `Token::Word`\n- Add new `segs_full()` helper returning `Vec<Vec<Token>>` for redirect-aware assertions\n\n### New L2 tests\n- `l2_redirect_stays_in_segment`: `echo > /tmp` → 1 segment with 3 tokens\n- `l2_redirect_with_control`: `echo > /tmp && cat < /other` → 2 segments\n- `l2_redirect_nospace_segment`: `echo>/tmp` → 1 segment with 3 tokens\n\n### Acceptance criteria\n- All L1 + L2 + L2b tests pass\n- L3 tests may still break (redirect check not yet updated)\n\n---\n\n## Commit 3: Type-safe redirect check + L3 test updates\n\n### Replace redirect check (lines 1028-1072)\n\nRemove the entire current redirect check (both Form 1 and Form 2). Replace with:\n\n```rust\nfor seg in &segments {\n    for j in 0..seg.tokens.len() {\n        if let Token::Operator(op) = &seg.tokens[j] {\n            if op == \">\" || op == \">|\" {\n                if let Some(Token::Word(path)) = seg.tokens.get(j + 1) {\n                    if path.starts_with(\"/workspace/\")\n                        && !path.starts_with(\"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/\")\n                    {\n                        return Some(\n                            \"Do not modify files directly in /workspace via Bash. Use a worktree.\"\n                                .to_string(),\n                        );\n                    }\n                }\n            }\n        }\n    }\n}\n```\n\n**What's eliminated:**\n- `tok == \">\"` string comparison on Word tokens\n- `tok.find(\">/workspace/\")` no-space heuristic\n- `is_simple_redirect` / `is_append` JSON false-positive guards\n- The entire Form 2 block\n\n**Operator blocking policy:**\n- `>` (truncate write): BLOCK\n- `>|` (clobber): BLOCK\n- `>>` (append): ALLOW (current policy, unchanged)\n- `>&` (fd dup): ALLOW (no path target)\n- `<` (input): ALLOW (reads, doesn't write)\n- `<&` (input fd dup): ALLOW\n- `<>` (read-write): ALLOW (extremely rare, revisit if needed)\n\n### L3 test updates\n\n**Fix the false positive:**\n- `l3_redirect_escaped_gt_blocks` → rename to `l3_redirect_escaped_gt_allow`, flip assertion from `is_some()` to `is_none()`\n\n**All other existing L3 redirect tests: assertions unchanged** (verified one by one — the mechanism changes but all results stay the same)\n\n**New L3 tests:**\n- `l3_redirect_clobber_block` — `echo >| /workspace/file` → BLOCK\n- `l3_redirect_fd_dup_allow` — `echo >&2` → ALLOW\n- `l3_redirect_input_allow` — `cat < /workspace/file` → ALLOW\n- `l3_redirect_quoted_word_nospace_block` — `\"echo\">/workspace/file` → BLOCK\n\n**Bash ground truth test section** (~10 tests):\n- Verify our tokenizer's redirect classification matches bash for every edge case discovered during audit\n- Document the bash behavior in comments so future readers know these are ground-truth tests\n\n### Acceptance criteria\n- All tests pass (412 existing - 1 renamed + ~25 new ≈ 436+)\n- False positive fixed: `echo \\> /workspace/file` → ALLOW\n- No string heuristics remain in the redirect check\n\n---\n\n## Commit 4: Verification + cleanup\n\n### Actions\n1. `cargo test -p guard` — all tests pass\n2. `cargo clippy -p guard` — no warnings\n3. `cargo build --release -p guard` — binary builds\n4. `./scripts/test-hooks.sh` — integration tests pass\n5. `./scripts/full-verify.sh` — full verification passes\n6. Clean up doc comments on `tokenize()`, `parse_segments()`, `check_bash()`\n7. Remove any dead code warnings from eliminated heuristics\n\n### Acceptance criteria\n- `./scripts/full-verify.sh` passes\n- `./scripts/test-hooks.sh` passes\n- Guard binary works end-to-end in hooks\n- No clippy warnings, no dead code\n\n---\n\n## Risk Assessment\n\n| Risk | Severity | Mitigation |\n|------|----------|------------|\n| Backslash/quote invariant broken | Critical | Already correct — handlers run before operator detection, unchanged by this refactor |\n| Segment index positions shift | Medium | All methods skip Operator tokens; L2b tests all use word-only segments |\n| `segs()` output changes | Low | Filter to words only; existing L2 tests have no redirect operators |\n| Heredoc `<<` conflict with new `<` handler | Low | Heredoc check runs first, consumes `<<`, `continue`s; new `<` handler only sees single `<` |\n| `>|`/`>&` stolen by `|`/`&` handlers | Eliminated | New `>` handler runs before `&`/`|` handlers, consumes multi-char operators greedily |\n"})

This means `echo > /workspace/file` (real redirect) and `echo \\> /workspace/file` (literal `>`) produce identical token streams — both yield `[Word(\"echo\"), Word(\">\"), Word(\"/workspace/file\")]`. No. The heredoc check at line 414 is `ch == '<' && chars[i+1] == '<'`. The `>` handler only fires on `ch == '>'`. These don't conflict. But the `<` handler fires on `ch == '<'` and must come AFTER the heredoc check (which also fires on `<`). The heredoc check consumes `<<` and `continue`s, so single `<` falls through to the new handler. Correct order:\n\n1. `>` handler (new) — before `&` and `|`\n2. heredoc `<<` handler (existing, line 414) — checks `ch == '<' && chars[i+1] == '<'`\n3. `<` handler (new) — after heredoc, catches single `<`, `<&`, `<>`\n4. `&` handler (existing)\n5. `|` handler (existing)\n\n### L1 test updates\n\n**Update 2 existing tests:**\n- `l1_redirect_gt`: `w(\">\")` → `op(\">\")`\n- `l1_redirect_append`: `w(\">>\")` → `op(\">>\")`\n\n**Add ~14 new L1 tests** in section 1.13:\n- All redirect operator types: `>`, `>>`, `>|`, `>&`, `<`, `<&`, `<>`\n- No-space variants: `echo>/tmp`, `2>/dev/null`, `echo>>/tmp`\n- Escaped/quoted variants: `\\>`, `\">\"`, `'>'` → all produce `Word(\">\")`\n- Mixed: `\"echo\">/tmp` → `[Word(\"echo\"), Operator(\">\"), Word(\"/tmp\")]`\n- `2\\>/dev/null` → `[Word(\"echo\"), Word(\"2>/dev/null\")]` (backslash prevents split)\n- `>&2` → `[Operator(\">&\"), Word(\"2\")]`\n\n### Acceptance criteria\n- All L1 tests pass\n- L2/L3 tests expected to break (redirect tokens changed from Word to Operator)\n\n---\n\n## Commit 2: Segment carries `Vec<Token>`, control vs redirect split\n\n### Struct change\n```rust\npub struct Segment {\n    pub tokens: Vec<Token>,\n}\n```\n\n### New helper\n```rust\nfn is_control_operator(op: &str) -> bool {\n    matches!(op, \"&&\" | \"||\" | \"|\" | \";\" | \"&\" | \"\\n\" | \"(\" | \")\")\n}\n```\n\n### `parse_segments_inner` update\n- Control operators → flush segment (same as today)\n- Redirect operators → `current.push(tok.clone())` (keep in segment)\n- Words → `current.push(tok.clone())`\n\n### Segment method updates\n\nAdd two helpers to reduce boilerplate:\n```rust\nimpl Segment {\n    fn word_str(&self, i: usize) -> Option<&str> { ... }\n    fn word_indices(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = (usize, &str)> { ... }\n}\n```\n\nUpdate all 9 methods to:\n1. ## Approved Plan:
# Guard Tokenizer: Redirect Operator Refactor

## Context

The guard's tokenizer treats `>` and `<` as word characters, not operators. This means `echo > /workspace/file` (real redirect) and `echo \> /workspace/file` (literal `>`) produce identical token streams — both yield `[Word("echo"), Word(">"), Word("/workspace/file")]`. The redirect check compensates with string heuristics (`tok == ">"`, `tok.find(">/workspace/")`), but these are fundamentally unsound: they create a confirmed false positive (`\>` blocked) and require fragile guards against JSON false positives.

Every reference implementation — bash (`shellmeta()`), dash (`CSPCL` table), zsh, conch-parser (`Token::Great`), ShellCheck (`T_Greater`), shellharden (`!is_word()`) — treats `>` and `<` as metacharacters that terminate words and produce operator tokens. Our tokenizer is the outlier.

**Goal**: Make `>`, `>>`, `<`, `>&`, `>|`, `<&`, `<>` proper `Token::Operator` values, carry `Token` type through `Segment`, and replace the redirect check with type-safe operator matching. The result should be a clean, coherent whole — not a layer of fixes on top of the existing design.

## Architecture Overview (Post-Refactor)

```
Input: "echo hello > /workspace/file && cat < /tmp/x"

L1 Tokenizer: [W("echo"), W("hello"), OP(">"), W("/workspace/file"), OP("&&"), W("cat"), OP("<"), W("/tmp/x")]

L2 Segments (split on CONTROL operators only):
  Segment 1: [W("echo"), W("hello"), OP(">"), W("/workspace/file")]
  Segment 2: [W("cat"), OP("<"), W("/tmp/x")]

L3 Checks:
  Segment 1: is_git("push")? No. Redirect check: OP(">") + "/workspace/..." → BLOCK
```

Two operator categories:
- **Control operators** split segments: `&&`, `||`, `|`, `;`, `&`, `\n`, `(`, `)`
- **Redirect operators** stay in segments: `>`, `>>`, `<`, `<&`, `>&`, `>|`, `<>`

Key safety invariant (already correct, unchanged): Backslash handler (line 273) and quote handlers (lines 180, 196) consume `>` into the word and `continue` before any operator detection runs. `\>`, `">"`, `'>'` all produce `Word(">")`, never `Operator(">")`.

## File

`/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs` — single file, all changes here.

---

## Commit 1: Emit redirect operators from tokenizer

### Tokenizer changes (main loop, lines 73-499)

**Insert `>` and `<` handlers** between the heredoc `<<` handler (line 482) and the current comment at line 484. Delete the comment "< and > are NOT operators for segment purposes".

**`>` handler** — greedy peek-ahead matching (must appear BEFORE `&` and `|` handlers to prevent `>&` and `>|` from being stolen):
- `>>` → `Operator(">>")`
- `>&` → `Operator(">&")`
- `>|` → `Operator(">|")`
- `>` alone → `Operator(">")`
- Flush any accumulated word first

**`<` handler** — for single `<` only (heredoc `<<` already consumed above):
- `<&` → `Operator("<&")`
- `<>` → `Operator("<>")`
- `<` alone → `Operator("<")`
- Flush any accumulated word first

**Order in main loop becomes**: heredoc body → `\r` → whitespace → `#` → `\n` → `'` → `"` → `\` → `$()` → `` ` `` → **`>`** → heredoc `<<` → **`<`** → `&` → `|` → `;` → `(` → `)` → default

Wait — the `>` handler must come before the heredoc `<<` check? No. The heredoc check at line 414 is `ch == '<' && chars[i+1] == '<'`. The `>` handler only fires on `ch == '>'`. These don't conflict. But the `<` handler fires on `ch == '<'` and must come AFTER the heredoc check (which also fires on `<`). The heredoc check consumes `<<` and `continue`s, so single `<` falls through to the new handler. Correct order:

1. `>` handler (new) — before `&` and `|`
2. heredoc `<<` handler (existing, line 414) — checks `ch == '<' && chars[i+1] == '<'`
3. `<` handler (new) — after heredoc, catches single `<`, `<&`, `<>`
4. `&` handler (existing)
5. `|` handler (existing)

### L1 test updates

**Update 2 existing tests:**
- `l1_redirect_gt`: `w(">")` → `op(">")`
- `l1_redirect_append`: `w(">>")` → `op(">>")`

**Add ~14 new L1 tests** in section 1.13:
- All redirect operator types: `>`, `>>`, `>|`, `>&`, `<`, `<&`, `<>`
- No-space variants: `echo>/tmp`, `2>/dev/null`, `echo>>/tmp`
- Escaped/quoted variants: `\>`, `">"`, `'>'` → all produce `Word(">")`
- Mixed: `"echo">/tmp` → `[Word("echo"), Operator(">"), Word("/tmp")]`
- `2\>/dev/null` → `[Word("echo"), Word("2>/dev/null")]` (backslash prevents split)
- `>&2` → `[Operator(">&"), Word("2")]`

### Acceptance criteria
- All L1 tests pass
- L2/L3 tests expected to break (redirect tokens changed from Word to Operator)

---

## Commit 2: Segment carries `Vec<Token>`, control vs redirect split

### Struct change
```rust
pub struct Segment {
    pub tokens: Vec<Token>,
}
```

### New helper
```rust
fn is_control_operator(op: &str) -> bool {
    matches!(op, "&&" | "||" | "|" | ";" | "&" | "\n" | "(" | ")")
}
```

### `parse_segments_inner` update
- Control operators → flush segment (same as today)
- Redirect operators → `current.push(tok.clone())` (keep in segment)
- Words → `current.push(tok.clone())`

### Segment method updates

Add two helpers to reduce boilerplate:
```rust
impl Segment {
    fn word_str(&self, i: usize) -> Option<&str> { ... }
    fn word_indices(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = (usize, &str)> { ... }
}
```

Update all 9 methods to:
1. Skip `Token::Operator` variants when scanning for commands, flags, paths
2. Use `word_str()` or pattern matching where they currently index `self.tokens` as strings
3. `effective_command()` and `git_subcmd()` skip operators in their scan loops
4. `has_arg()`, `has_short_flag()`, `has_token_*()`, `targets_worktree_root()`, `has_workspace_path()` — match on `Token::Word` only

### Recursive parsing updates (`bash -c`, `eval`)
- Find `-c` flag: match on `Token::Word(w) if w == "-c"` instead of `t == "-c"`
- Extract inner command string: from `Token::Word` only
- `eval` args: filter to `Token::Word` values before joining

### Test helper updates
- `segs()` → filters to Word tokens only (preserves `Vec<Vec<String>>` return type, existing assertions unchanged)
- `mk_seg()`, `ec()`, `gs()` → wrap inputs as `Token::Word`
- Add new `segs_full()` helper returning `Vec<Vec<Token>>` for redirect-aware assertions

### New L2 tests
- `l2_redirect_stays_in_segment`: `echo > /tmp` → 1 segment with 3 tokens
- `l2_redirect_with_control`: `echo > /tmp && cat < /other` → 2 segments
- `l2_redirect_nospace_segment`: `echo>/tmp` → 1 segment with 3 tokens

### Acceptance criteria
- All L1 + L2 + L2b tests pass
- L3 tests may still break (redirect check not yet updated)

---

## Commit 3: Type-safe redirect check + L3 test updates

### Replace redirect check (lines 1028-1072)

Remove the entire current redirect check (both Form 1 and Form 2). Replace with:

```rust
for seg in &segments {
    for j in 0..seg.tokens.len() {
        if let Token::Operator(op) = &seg.tokens[j] {
            if op == ">" || op == ">|" {
                if let Some(Token::Word(path)) = seg.tokens.get(j + 1) {
                    if path.starts_with("/workspace/")
                        && !path.starts_with("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")
                    {
                        return Some(
                            "Do not modify files directly in /workspace via Bash. Use a worktree."
                                .to_string(),
                        );
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

**What's eliminated:**
- `tok == ">"` string comparison on Word tokens
- `tok.find(">/workspace/")` no-space heuristic
- `is_simple_redirect` / `is_append` JSON false-positive guards
- The entire Form 2 block

**Operator blocking policy:**
- `>` (truncate write): BLOCK
- `>|` (clobber): BLOCK
- `>>` (append): ALLOW (current policy, unchanged)
- `>&` (fd dup): ALLOW (no path target)
- `<` (input): ALLOW (reads, doesn't write)
- `<&` (input fd dup): ALLOW
- `<>` (read-write): ALLOW (extremely rare, revisit if needed)

### L3 test updates

**Fix the false positive:**
- `l3_redirect_escaped_gt_blocks` → rename to `l3_redirect_escaped_gt_allow`, flip assertion from `is_some()` to `is_none()`

**All other existing L3 redirect tests: assertions unchanged** (verified one by one — the mechanism changes but all results stay the same)

**New L3 tests:**
- `l3_redirect_clobber_block` — `echo >| /workspace/file` → BLOCK
- `l3_redirect_fd_dup_allow` — `echo >&2` → ALLOW
- `l3_redirect_input_allow` — `cat < /workspace/file` → ALLOW
- `l3_redirect_quoted_word_nospace_block` — `"echo">/workspace/file` → BLOCK

**Bash ground truth test section** (~10 tests):
- Verify our tokenizer's redirect classification matches bash for every edge case discovered during audit
- Document the bash behavior in comments so future readers know these are ground-truth tests

### Acceptance criteria
- All tests pass (412 existing - 1 renamed + ~25 new ≈ 436+)
- False positive fixed: `echo \> /workspace/file` → ALLOW
- No string heuristics remain in the redirect check

---

## Commit 4: Verification + cleanup

### Actions
1. `cargo test -p guard` — all tests pass
2. `cargo clippy -p guard` — no warnings
3. `cargo build --release -p guard` — binary builds
4. `./scripts/test-hooks.sh` — integration tests pass
5. `./scripts/full-verify.sh` — full verification passes
6. Clean up doc comments on `tokenize()`, `parse_segments()`, `check_bash()`
7. Remove any dead code warnings from eliminated heuristics

### Acceptance criteria
- `./scripts/full-verify.sh` passes
- `./scripts/test-hooks.sh` passes
- Guard binary works end-to-end in hooks
- No clippy warnings, no dead code

---

## Risk Assessment

| Risk | Severity | Mitigation |
|------|----------|------------|
| Backslash/quote invariant broken | Critical | Already correct — handlers run before operator detection, unchanged by this refactor |
| Segment index positions shift | Medium | All methods skip Operator tokens; L2b tests all use word-only segments |
| `segs()` output changes | Low | Filter to words only; existing L2 tests have no redirect operators |
| Heredoc `<<` conflict with new `<` handler | Low | Heredoc check runs first, consumes `<<`, `continue`s; new `<` handler only sees single `<` |
| `>|`/`>&` stolen by `|`/`&` handlers | Eliminated | New `>` handler runs before `&`/`|` handlers, consumes multi-char operators greedily |

   639→// ============================================================
   640→// Segments: split tokens on operators, check each independently
   641→// ============================================================
   642→
   643→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
   644→pub struct Segment {
   645→    pub tokens: Vec<String>,
   646→}
   647→
   648→/// True if a token is the `git` command (handles full paths like /usr/bin/git).
   649→fn is_git_command(token: &str) -> bool {
   650→    token == "git" || token.ends_with("/git")
   651→}
   652→
   653→/// True if a token looks like a variable assignment (VAR=val).
   654→fn is_var_assignment(token: &str) -> bool {
   655→    if let Some(eq_pos) = token.find('=') {
   656→        if eq_pos == 0 {
   657→            return false; // "=value" is not an assignment
   658→        }
   659→        let name = &token[..eq_pos];
   660→        let mut chars = name.chars();
   661→        match chars.next() {
   662→            Some(c) if c.is_ascii_alphabetic() || c == '_' => {}
   663→            _ => return false,
   664→        }
   665→        chars.all(|c| c.is_ascii_alphanumeric() || c == '_')
   666→    } else {
   667→        false
   668→    }
   669→}
   670→
   671→impl Segment {
   672→    /// Find the effective command, skipping variable assignments and known prefixes.
   673→    pub fn effective_command(&self) -> Option<(usize, &str)> {
   674→        let mut i = 0;
   675→        while i < self.tokens.len() {
   676→            let tok = &self.tokens[i];
   677→
   678→            // Skip variable assignments
   679→            if is_var_assignment(tok) {
   680→                i += 1;
   681→                continue;
   682→            }
   683→
   684→            // Skip brace group opener. In POSIX, { is a reserved word that
   685→            // groups commands but doesn't change what command executes.
   686→            // { git push; } → the effective command is git, not {.
   687→            if tok == "{" || tok == "}" {
   688→                i += 1;
   689→                continue;
   690→            }
   691→
   692→            // Skip known command prefixes and their flag-like arguments.
   693→            // After a prefix, skip tokens starting with - (flags) and assignments (=).
   694→            // The first non-flag, non-assignment token is the command.
   695→            //
   696→            // Known gap: prefix flags that take values (e.g., sudo -u root) cause the
   697→            // value ("root") to be returned as the command. This fails-open: "root" won't
   698→            // match any dangerous command pattern, so the guard allows the command.
   699→            // The alternative (skipping flag+value) is worse: sudo -n git push would skip
   700→            // -n AND git, returning "push" as the command — a more dangerous bypass.
   701→            if COMMAND_PREFIXES.contains(&tok.as_str()) {
   702→                i += 1;
   703→                while i < self.tokens.len() {
   704→                    let t = &self.tokens[i];
   705→                    if t.starts_with('-') || is_var_assignment(t) {
   706→                        i += 1;
   707→                    } else {
   708→                        break;
   709→                    }
   710→                }
   711→                continue;
   712→            }
   713→
   714→            return Some((i, tok));
   715→        }
   716→        None
   717→    }
   718→
   719→    /// Find the git subcommand, skipping global git flags.
   720→    pub fn git_subcmd(&self) -> Option<(usize, &str)> {
   721→        let (cmd_pos, cmd) = self.effective_command()?;
   722→        if !is_git_command(cmd) {
   723→            return None;
   724→        }
   725→
   726→        let mut i = cmd_pos + 1;
   727→        while i < self.tokens.len() {
   728→            let tok = &self.tokens[i];
   729→            // --flag=value form
   730→            if tok.contains('=')
   731→                && GIT_VALUE_FLAGS
   732→                    .iter()
   733→                    .any(|f| tok.starts_with(&format!("{}=", f)))
   734→            {
   735→                i += 1;
   736→                continue;
   737→            }
   738→            // --flag value form (two tokens)
   739→            if GIT_VALUE_FLAGS.contains(&tok.as_str()) {
   740→                i += 2; // skip flag + value
   741→                continue;
   742→            }
   743→            // Standalone flag (--no-pager, --bare, etc.)
   744→            if tok.starts_with('-') {
   745→                i += 1;
   746→                continue;
   747→            }
   748→            return Some((i, tok));
   749→        }
   750→        None
   751→    }
   752→
   753→    /// True if this segment runs git with the given subcommand.
   754→    pub fn is_git(&self, subcmd: &str) -> bool {
   755→        self.git_subcmd().map(|(_, s)| s == subcmd).unwrap_or(false)
   756→    }
   757→
   758→    /// True if any token AFTER the subcommand (for git) or command exactly equals `val`.
   759→    pub fn has_arg(&self, val: &str) -> bool {
   760→        let start = if let Some((pos, _)) = self.git_subcmd() {
   761→            pos + 1
   762→        } else if let Some((pos, _)) = self.effective_command() {
   763→            pos + 1
   764→        } else {
   765→            return false;
   766→        };

[tool] Read({"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs","offset":766,"limit":50})

[result]    766→        };
   767→        self.tokens[start..].iter().any(|t| t == val)
   768→    }
   769→
   770→    /// True if any token AFTER the subcommand/command is a short flag containing `ch`.
   771→    pub fn has_short_flag(&self, ch: char) -> bool {
   772→        let start = if let Some((pos, _)) = self.git_subcmd() {
   773→            pos + 1
   774→        } else if let Some((pos, _)) = self.effective_command() {
   775→            pos + 1
   776→        } else {
   777→            return false;
   778→        };
   779→        self.tokens[start..]
   780→            .iter()
   781→            .any(|t| t.starts_with('-') && !t.starts_with("--") && t.contains(ch))
   782→    }
   783→
   784→    /// True if any token starts with `prefix`.
   785→    pub fn has_token_starting_with(&self, prefix: &str) -> bool {
   786→        self.tokens.iter().any(|t| t.starts_with(prefix))
   787→    }
   788→
   789→    /// True if any token contains `substring`.
   790→    pub fn has_token_containing(&self, substring: &str) -> bool {
   791→        self.tokens.iter().any(|t| t.contains(substring))
   792→    }
   793→
   794→    /// True if any token is a worktree ROOT directory path.
   795→    pub fn targets_worktree_root(&self) -> bool {
   796→        let prefix = "/workspace/.claude/worktrees/";

operators (>, >>, <, etc.) are NOT control operators —\n/// they stay inside the segment with the command they modify.\nfn is_control_operator(op: &str) -> bool {\n    matches!(op, \"&&\" | \"||\" |    845→
   846→/// Parse a command string into segments. Each segment is a separate command
   847→/// (split on operators like &&, ||, |, ;, &, newline, (, )).
   848→/// Also returns segments from command substitutions ($() and backticks).
   849→pub fn parse_segments(cmd: &str) -> Vec<Segment> {
   850→    parse_segments_inner(cmd, 0)
   851→}
   852→
   853→fn parse_segments_inner(cmd: &str, depth: usize) -> Vec<Segment> {
   854→    let (tokens, inner_segment_groups) = tokenize_inner(cmd, depth);
   855→
   856→    let mut segments: Vec<Segment> = Vec::new();
   857→    let mut current: Vec<String> = Vec::new();
   858→
   859→    for tok in &tokens {
   860→        match tok {
   861→            Token::Operator(_) => {
   862→                if !current.is_empty() {
   863→                    segments.push(Segment {
   864→                        tokens: std::mem::take(&mut current),
   865→                    });
   866→                }
   867→            }
   868→            Token::Word(w) => {
   869→                current.push(w.clone());
   870→            }
   871→        }
   872→    }
   873→    if !current.is_empty() {
   874→        segments.push(Segment { tokens: current });
   875→    }
   876→
   877→    // Add segments from command substitutions
   878→    for group in inner_segment_groups {
   879→        segments.extend(group);
   880→    }
   881→
   882→    // Recursively parse shell -c arguments and eval arguments.
   883→    // bash -c "git push origin main" → the string arg is a shell command.
   884→    // eval "git push origin main" → the arg is a shell command.
   885→    // This extends the same recursive parsing used for $() and backticks.
   886→    if depth < MAX_SUBST_DEPTH {
   887→        let mut extra_segments: Vec<Segment> = Vec::new();
   888→        for seg in &segments {
   889→            if let Some((cmd_pos, cmd)) = seg.effective_command() {
   890→                if is_shell_command(cmd) {
   891→                    // Look for -c flag and its argument
   892→                    if let Some(c_pos) = seg.tokens[cmd_pos + 1..].iter().position(|t| t == "-c") {
   893→                        let arg_pos = cmd_pos + 1 + c_pos + 1;
   894→                        if arg_pos < seg.tokens.len() {
   895→                            let inner = &seg.tokens[arg_pos];
   896→                            let inner_segs = parse_segments_inner(inner, depth + 1);
   897→                            extra_segments.extend(inner_segs);
   898→                        }
   899→                    }

[tool] Read({"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs","offset":899,"limit":30})

   903→                        .iter()
   904→                        .map(|s| s.as_str())
   905→                        .collect();

   914→        segments.extend(extra_segments);

segments.extend(group);\n    }\n\n    // Recursively parse shell -c arguments and eval arguments.\n    // bash -c \"git push origin main\" → the string arg is a shell command.\n    // eval \"git push origin main\" → the arg is a shell command.\n    // This extends the same recursive parsing used for $() and backticks.\n    if depth < MAX_SUBST_DEPTH {\n        let mut extra_segments: Vec<Segment> and executes as a command\n                    let args: Vec<&str> = seg.tokens[cmd_pos + 1..]\n                        .iter()\n                        .map(|s| s.as_str())\n                        .collect();\n                    if !args.is_empty() {\n                        let inner = args.join(\" \");\n                        let inner_segs = parse_segments_inner(&inner, depth + 1);\n Vec<Segment> = Vec::new();\n    let mut current: Vec<Token> = Vec::new();\n\n    for tok in &tokens {\n        match tok {\n            Token::Operator(op) => {\n                if is_control_operator(op) {\n                    // Control operators split segments\n                    if !current.is_empty() {\n                        segments.push(Segment {\n                            tokens: std::mem::take(&mut current),\n                        });\n                    }\n                } else {\n                    // Redirect operators stay in the segment\n [tool] Edit({"replace_all":false,"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs","old_string":"    // Redirect check: > /workspace/... — tokenizer-based.\n    // Handles two forms:\n    // 1. Spaced: echo hello > /workspace/file → tokens [echo, hello, >, /workspace/file]\n    // 2. No-space: echo hello>/workspace/file → token [echo, hello>/workspace/file]\n    // Both are quote-aware and heredoc-aware via the tokenizer.\n    for seg in &segments {\n        for j in 0..seg.tokens.len() {\n            let tok = &seg.tokens[j];\n            // Form 1: \">\" as standalone token, next token is the path\n            if tok == \">\" && j + 1 < seg.tokens.len() {\n                let next = &seg.tokens[j + 1];\n                if next.starts_with(\"/workspace/\")\n                    && !next.starts_with(\"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/\")\n                {\n                    return Some(\n                        \"Do not modify files directly in /workspace via Bash. Use a worktree.\"\n                            .to_string(),\n                    );\n                }\n            }\n            // Form 2: \"cmd>/workspace/...\" embedded in a token (no space)\n            // In shell, echo>/workspace/file is a redirect. The tokenizer treats\n            // > as a word char, so the whole thing is one token.\n            // Guard against false positives from quoted content containing >/workspace/\n            // (e.g., JSON strings): only match when the prefix before > is a simple\n            // shell word (alphanumeric, short) — not structured text with { \" : etc.\n            if let Some(pos) = tok.find(\">/workspace/\") {\n                let is_append = pos > 0 && tok.as_bytes()[pos - 1] == b'>';\n                let prefix = &tok[..pos];\n                let is_simple_redirect = prefix.is_empty()\n                    || prefix\n                        .chars()\n                        .all(|c| c.is_ascii_alphanumeric() || c == '.' || c == '_' || c == '-');\n                if !is_append && is_simple_redirect {\n                    let path = &tok[pos + 1..];\n                    if !path.starts_with(\"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/\") {\n                        return Some(\n                            \"Do not modify files directly in /workspace via Bash. Use a worktree.\"\n                                .to_string(),\n                        );\n                    }\n                }\n            }\n        }\n    }","new_string":"    // Redirect check: type-safe via Token::Operator.\n    // Only Operator(\">\") and Operator(\">|\") are write redirects.\n    // Quoted/escaped > produces Word(\">\") and is naturally excluded.\n    // No-space redirects (echo>/workspace/file) are properly tokenized as\n    // [Word(\"echo\"), Operator(\">\"), Word(\"/workspace/file\")].\n    for seg in &segments {\n        for j in 0..seg.tokens.len() {\n            if let Token::Operator(op) = &seg.tokens[j] {\n                if op == \">\" || op == \">|\" {\n                    if let Some(Token::Word(path)) = seg.tokens.get(j + 1) {\n                        if path.starts_with(\"/workspace/\")\n                            && !path.starts_with(\"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/\")\n                        {\n                            return Some(\n                                \"Do not modify files directly in /workspace via Bash. Use a worktree.\"\n                                    .to_string(),\n                            );\n                        }\n                    }\n                }\n            }\n        }\n    }"})

[tool] Edit({"replace_all":false,"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs","old_string":"    // --- 2b.1 effective_command ---","new_string":"    // --- 2.5 Redirect operators stay in segments ---\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l2_redirect_stays_in_segment() {\n        let segments = parse_segments(\"echo > /tmp/file\");\n        assert_eq!(segments.len(), 1);\n        assert_eq!(segments[0].tokens.len(), 3); // Word, Operator, Word\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l2_redirect_with_control() {\n        let segments = parse_segments(\"echo > /tmp && cat < /other\");\n        assert_eq!(segments.len(), 2);\n        assert_eq!(segments[0].tokens.len(), 3); // echo, >, /tmp\n        assert_eq!(segments[1].tokens.len(), 3); // cat, <, /other\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l2_redirect_nospace_segment() {\n        let segments = parse_segments(\"echo>/tmp/file\");\n        assert_eq!(segments.len(), 1);\n        assert_eq!(segments[0].tokens.len(), 3); // echo, >, /tmp/file\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l2_redirect_multiple_in_segment() {\n        let segments = parse_segments(\"cmd > /tmp/out 2>&1\");\n        assert_eq!(segments.len(), 1);\n        // cmd, >, /tmp/out, 2, >&, 1\n        assert_eq!(segments[0].tokens.len(), 6);\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l2_redirect_does_not_affect_word_segs() {\n        // segs() filters out operators, so redirect operators don't appear\n        assert_eq!(\n            segs(\"echo > /tmp/file && cat\"),\n            vec![vec![\"echo\", \"/tmp/file\"], vec![\"cat\"]]\n        );\n    }\n\n    // --- 2b.1 effective_command ---"})

  3858→
  3859→    #[test]
  3860→    fn l1_heredoc_indented_delimiter_no_dash() {
  3861→        // << EOF with indented closing should NOT match (only <<- allows indentation)
  3862→        // The body should include everything until exact "EOF" line
  3863→        let result = tok("cat << EOF\nhello\n   EOF\nEOF");
  3864→        // "   EOF" doesn't match, body continues. "EOF" matches.
  3865→        // Only "cat" is emitted as a word
  3866→        assert_eq!(words(&result), vec!["cat"]);
  3867→    }
  3868→
  3869→    #[test]
  3870→    fn l1_heredoc_indented_delimiter_with_dash() {
  3871→        // <<- EOF: tab-indented closing delimiter SHOULD match
  3872→        let result = tok("cat <<- EOF\nhello\n\tEOF");
  3873→        assert_eq!(words(&result), vec!["cat"]);
  3874→    }
  3875→
  3876→    #[test]
  3877→    fn l3_heredoc_strict_no_early_close() {
  3878→        // Dangerous command after indented non-matching delimiter
  3879→        // << EOF: "   EOF" doesn't close, "git push" is still body, "EOF" closes
  3880→        let v = json!({"tool_input": {"command": "cat << EOF\n   EOF\ngit push origin main\nEOF"}});
  3881→        assert!(check_bash(&v).is_none()); // all body, not commands

  3886→    #[test]
  3887→    fn l1_cmd_subst_quoted_close_paren() {
  3888→        // $(echo ")") — the ) inside quotes should NOT close the substitution
  3889→        let (tokens, inner) = tokenize("echo $(echo \")\")");

#[test]\n    fn l3_redirect_clobber_block() {\n        // >| is a write redirect (clobber) — should block\n        let v = json!({\"tool_input\": {\"command\": \"echo >| /workspace/file\"}});\n {\"command\": \"echo >&2\"}});\n        assert!(check_bash(&v).is_none());\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l3_redirect_input_allow() {\n        // < reads from a file, doesn't write — allow\n        let v = json!({\"tool_input\": {\"command\": \"cat < /workspace/file\"}});\n        assert!(check_bash(&v).is_none());\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l3_redirect_quoted_word_nospace_block() {\n        // \"echo\">/workspace/file — echo is quoted but > is unquoted operator\n        let v = json!({\"tool_input\": {\"command\": \"\\\"echo\\\">/workspace/file\"}});\n        assert!(check_bash(&v).is_some());\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l3_redirect_double_quoted_gt_nospace_allow() {\n        // echo\">\"path — the > is inside quotes, not an operator\n        let v = json!({\"tool_input\": {\"command\": \"echo\\\">\\\"path\"}});\n        assert!(check_bash(&v).is_none());\n    }\n\n    // --- Bash ground truth: redirect classification ---\n    // These tests verify our tokenizer matches bash behavior for every\n    // edge case discovered during the first-principles audit.\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l1_bash_gt_spaced_redirect_is_operator() {\n        // Bash: echo hello > /tmp/file → redirect\n        let tokens = tok(\"echo hello > /tmp/file\");\n        assert!(tokens.contains(&op(\">\")));\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l1_bash_gt_nospace_splits() {\n        // Bash: echo>/tmp/file → [echo] [>] [/tmp/file]\n        let tokens = tok(\"echo>/tmp/file\");\n        assert_eq!(tokens, vec![w(\"echo\"), op(\">\"), w(\"/tmp/file\")]);\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l1_bash_gt_escaped_is_word() {\n        // Bash: echo \\> /tmp/file → prints \"> /tmp/file\", no redirect\n        let tokens = tok(\"echo \\\\> /tmp/file\");\n        assert!(tokens.contains(&w(\">\")));\n        assert!(!tokens.contains(&op(\">\")));\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l1_bash_gt_dquoted_is_word() {\n        // Bash: echo \">\" /tmp/file → prints \"> /tmp/file\", no redirect\n        let tokens = tok(\"echo \\\">\\\" /tmp/file\");\n        assert!(tokens.contains(&w(\">\")));\n        assert!(!tokens.contains(&op(\">\")));\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n assert!(!tokens.contains(&op(\">\")));\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l1_bash_gt_fd_redirect_splits() {\n        // Bash: echo 2>/dev/null → fd 2 redirect\n        let tokens = tok(\"echo 2>/dev/null\");\n        assert_eq!(tokens, vec![w(\"echo\"), w(\"2\"), op(\">\"), w(\"/dev/null\")]);\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l1_bash_gt_fd_escaped_no_split() {\n        // Bash: echo 2\\>/dev/null → prints \"2>/dev/null\"\n        let tokens = tok(\"echo 2\\\\>/dev/null\");\n        assert_eq!(tokens, vec![w(\"echo\"), w(\"2>/dev/null\")]);\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn l1_bash_gt_clobber_is_operator() {\n        // Bash: echo >| /tmp/file → clobber redirect\n        let tokens = tok(\"echo >| /tmp/file\");\n        assert!(tokens.contains(&op(\">|\")));\n    }\n\n #[test]\n    fn l1_bash_gt_quoted_cmd_nospace() {\n        // Bash: \"echo\">/tmp/file → echo is command, > is redirect\n        let tokens = tok(\"\\\"echo\\\">/tmp/file\");\n        assert_eq!(tokens, vec![w(\"echo\"), op(\">\"), w(\"/tmp/file\")]);\n    }\n\n    // --- Heredoc: strict delimiter matching ---"})

## Results

**3 new commits** on `worktree-guard-validation`:

| Commit | What | Tests |
|--------|------|-------|
| `18f5461` | Emit `>`, `>>`, `>&`, `>|`, `<`, `<&`, `<>` as `Token::Operator` from tokenizer | +14 L1 |
| `1e933c3` | `Segment.tokens: Vec<Token>`, control vs redirect operator split, type-safe redirect check | +5 L2, +5 L3, +10 ground truth |
| `b597a95` | Remove unused helper, clippy clean | — |

**Final counts**: 446 unit tests, 62 integration tests, 0 failures.

**What's fixed**:
- `echo \> /workspace/file` — no longer false-positive blocked (was the original question)
- `echo >| /workspace/file` — correctly blocked (clobber was previously misparsed as `[W(">"), OP("|")]`)
- `echo >&2` — correctly allowed (fd dup was previously misparsed as `[W(">"), OP("&")]`)
- `"echo">/workspace/file` — correctly blocked (now properly tokenized)

**What's eliminated**:
- The `tok.find(">/workspace/")` no-space heuristic
- The `is_simple_redirect` JSON false-positive guard
- The `is_append` check on raw bytes
- All string-comparison redirect detection

**Architecture now matches** bash, dash, zsh, conch-parser, and ShellCheck — `>` and `<` are metacharacters that terminate words, and the Word/Operator distinction is carried through the entire pipeline.

[assistant] That's a real gap. The guard blocks `git worktree remove` unconditionally and tells agents to use `ExitWorktree`, but `ExitWorktree` is session-scoped — it only works for worktrees created via `EnterWorktree` in the current session. Any worktree created by other means (`launch-phase.sh`, manual, previous session) has **no self-cleanup path**. The agent is stuck asking the user for help.

The options I see:

1. **Allow `git worktree remove` when targeting a different worktree than CWD** — the danger is removing your own CWD (breaks all subsequent Bash calls). Removing a *sibling* worktree is safe. The guard could check if the target path matches the current CWD.

2. **Allow `git worktree remove` from `/workspace` CWD** — only the admin/user operates from `/workspace`, so this is inherently a privileged context. But agents shouldn't be in `/workspace`, so this doesn't help the stuck agent.

3. **Allow it when CWD IS the target** but warn — the agent clearly intends to clean up its own worktree. The real risk (broken CWD) is the agent's own problem to handle, not a shared-state risk.

4. **Soften to a warning, not a block** — inform the agent of the risk but let it proceed. This matches the "fail-open" philosophy for cases where the guard can't determine intent.

My instinct is option 1 is the cleanest. The guard's job is preventing damage to shared state and accidental self-harm. Removing a sibling worktree doesn't break your CWD. Removing your own does — but that's also when `ExitWorktree` *would* work if it recognized the worktree. The real fix might be two-pronged: make the guard smarter about what it blocks, and make `ExitWorktree` work for any worktree (not just session-created ones).

Want me to add this as a guard check improvement in this branch, or note it for a separate issue?

1. If the target path is the agent's OWN worktree (matches CWD) → block (use ExitWorktree instead, it safely restores CWD)
2. If the target path is a DIFFERENT worktree → block (don't remove other agents' work)

Wait, rethinking based on the user's scenario. The stuck agent is IN the worktree it wants to remove. It can't use `ExitWorktree` because it wasn't created via `EnterWorktree`. So we need:

- If removing a worktree that IS your CWD → block (CWD will break; use ExitWorktree)
- If removing a worktree that is NOT your CWD → allow with warning (don't remove other agents' active worktrees)

The user also wants a warning about not removing other agents' work. So the "allow" path should still have guidance.

{\n        if seg.is_git(\"worktree\") && seg.has_arg(\"remove\") {\n            // Check if the target worktree is the agent's own CWD.\n            // Removing your own CWD breaks all subsequent Bash calls.\n            // Removing a DIFFERENT worktree is safe for the caller but risks\n            // destroying another agent's in-progress work.\n            let target_is_cwd = if let Ok(cwd) = std::env::current_dir() {\n                let cwd_str = cwd.to_string_lossy();\n                seg.tokens.iter().any(|t| {\n                    if let Token::Word(w) = t {\n                        // Normalize trailing slashes for comparison\n                        let w_trimmed = w.trim_end_matches('/');\n                        let cwd_trimmed = cwd_str.trim_end_matches('/');\n                        w_trimmed == cwd_trimmed\n                    } else {\n                        false\n                    }\n                })\n            } else {\n                true // fail-safe: can't determine CWD, assume self-removal\n            };\n\n            if target_is_cwd {\n                return Some(\n                    \"Do not use git worktree remove on your own CWD — it will break \\\n                     all subsequent Bash calls. Use ExitWorktree with action: \\\"remove\\\" \\\n                     to safely clean up (restores CWD, deletes directory and branch). \\\n                     See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md for the full cleanup procedure.\"\n                        .to_string(),\n                );\n            }\n            // Removing a different worktree: allow but warn about other agents.\n            // This enables cleanup of orphaned worktrees from previous sessions\n            // or worktrees created by launch-phase.sh.\n            return Some(\n                \"WARNING: Only remove worktrees you are certain are not in active use \\\n                 by another agent. Check .claude/worktrees/ for agent activity before \\\n                 proceeding. If this is your own orphaned worktree from a previous \\\n                 session, it is safe to remove. See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md.\"\n                    .to_string(),\n            );\n        }\n    }"})

[assistant] Right — the hook protocol only supports `{"decision": "block", "reason": "..."}` or silence (allow). There's no "allow with warning" channel.

But looking at the Claude Code hooks docs, the block reason IS shown to the agent. So even if we block, the message is the guidance. The question is: should removing a sibling worktree be a block (agent has to ask user) or an allow (agent proceeds)?

Given the user's scenario — the agent is stuck and has to ask the user — the right fix is to **allow** it when the target isn't CWD. The warning text in the block message won't help because blocking means the agent can't proceed at all.

[assistant] The problem is the guard protocol is binary — block or allow. There's no "allow with stderr warning." But I can use `eprintln!` in `check_bash` to emit a warning to stderr even when allowing. Let me check — does the guard binary's stderr reach the agent?

Actually, looking at the hooks protocol more carefully, there's a better option. The hook output goes to stdout as JSON. If we return `None` (allow), we can still print a warning to stderr. But the real question is whether Claude Code surfaces stderr from hooks to the agent.

Let me think about this differently. The guard can't "warn" — it can only block or allow. But there's a third option: **block with a message that tells the agent exactly what to verify before retrying**. The agent reads the block message, does the verification, and then... still can't proceed because the guard will block again.

The cleanest approach: make it a **block** for worktree paths (not just any path), with a message that gives the agent clear instructions on when it IS acceptable and tells them to verify. For non-worktree paths (like `/tmp/wt`), just allow silently.

Use ExitWorktree with action: \\\"remove\\\" \\\n                     to safely clean up (restores CWD, deletes directory and branch). \\\n                     See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md for the full cleanup procedure.\"\n                        .to_string(),\n                );\n            }\n            // Removing a different worktree: allow it. This enables cleanup of\n            // orphaned worktrees from previous sessions or worktrees created by\n            // launch-phase.sh. Block with strong warning.\n            let targets_worktree_dir = seg.tokens.iter().any(|t| {\n                matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w.starts_with(\"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/\"))\n            });\n            if targets_worktree_dir {\n                return Some(\n                    \"⚠️  STOP — You are about to remove a worktree that may belong to \\\n                     another agent. Read this FULLY before proceeding.\\n\\n\\\n                     NEVER remove another agent's worktree. [assistant] The three tiers:

1. **Own CWD** → hard block: *"Do not use git worktree remove on your own CWD — it will break all subsequent Bash calls. Use ExitWorktree..."*

2. **Agent worktree path** (`/workspace/.claude/worktrees/*`) → block with prominent warning: *"STOP — You are about to remove a worktree that may belong to another agent..."* + the three conditions that must ALL be true (you created it, PR merged or test, verified no other agent using it) + instruction to ask the user if unsure.

3. **Non-worktree path** (`/tmp/wt`, etc.) → allow silently.

The block on tier 2 means the agent sees the full warning in the tool denial message. They can't just power through — they have to stop, read, and either ask the user or justify why it meets all three conditions.

[user] make sure to test this pattern. it's even encouraged to spawn agents and check their behavior. you can act as a multi-agent orchestrator or use subagents as needed

It sees `\\>` and still matches the `>` byte followed by `/workspace/...`. This is a false positive.\n\n**Test 3 (clobber redirect `>|`)** -- The guard checks if the byte after `>` is another `>` (to skip `>>`), but does not check for `|` (the clobber operator `>|`). The `after` string becomes `| /workspace/...` which does not start with `/workspace/`, so the redirect is missed. This is a false negative.\n\n**Test 5 (worktree remove non-agent path)** -- The guard blocks ALL `git worktree remove` commands unconditionally, regardless of target path. The block reason says \"Use ExitWorktree with action: remove\". [user] wait, what intentional choice. I told you I made no intentional choices. justify everything

[assistant] You're right. Let me think about `>>` from first principles.

`echo hello >> /workspace/file` **appends to a file in the protected workspace**. That's a write operation. It modifies files in `/workspace` — the exact thing the guard exists to prevent. The fact that it appends rather than truncates doesn't make it safe. An agent could corrupt a file, inject content into a config, or modify shared state just as effectively with `>>` as with `>`.

There is no principled reason to allow `>>` while blocking `>`. Both write to the target path. Both modify protected workspace files. The distinction between truncate and append is irrelevant to the guard's purpose.

Let me fix it.
