  1004→
  1005→/// Run all Bash checks. Returns Some(reason) to block, None to allow.
  1006→pub fn check_bash(input: &serde_json::Value) -> Option<String> {
  1007→    let cmd = input["tool_input"]["command"].as_str().unwrap_or("");
  1008→
  1009→    let segments = parse_segments(cmd);
  1010→
  1011→    // push-to-main
  1012→    for seg in &segments {
  1013→        if seg.is_git("push") && seg.has_arg("main") {
  1014→            return Some("Cannot push directly to main. Use a feature branch and PR.".to_string());
  1015→        }
  1016→    }
  1017→
  1018→    // checkout-switch (CWD-dependent)
  1019→    for seg in &segments {
  1020→        if (seg.is_git("checkout") || seg.is_git("switch"))
  1021→            && !seg.has_arg("--")
  1022→            && !seg.has_short_flag('b')
  1023→            && !seg.has_short_flag('B')
  1024→            && !seg.has_short_flag('c')
  1025→            && !seg.has_token_containing("worktree")
  1026→        {
  1027→            if !is_worktree_cwd() {
  1028→                return Some(
  1029→                    "Do not use git checkout/switch in /workspace. \
  1030→                     Use a worktree: git worktree add /workspace/.claude/worktrees/<name> -b <branch>"
  1031→                        .to_string(),
  1032→                );
  1033→            }
  1034→        }
  1035→    }
  1036→
  1037→    // reset-hard-workspace: block ALL reset --hard in /workspace (even origin/*)
  1038→    for seg in &segments {
  1039→        if seg.is_git("reset") && seg.has_arg("--hard") && is_workspace_cwd() {
  1040→            return Some(
  1041→                "Do not run git reset --hard in /workspace — it modifies shared state \
  1042→                 that other agents depend on. If you need to sync after a squash merge, \
  1043→                 run git reset --hard origin/<branch> in your own worktree instead. \
  1044→                 See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md."
  1045→                    .to_string(),
  1046→            );
  1047→        }
  1048→    }
  1049→
  1050→    // reset-hard-destructive: block reset --hard without origin/ ref (in worktrees)
  1051→    for seg in &segments {
  1052→        if seg.is_git("reset") && seg.has_arg("--hard") && !seg.has_token_starting_with("origin/") {
  1053→            return Some(
  1054→                "git reset --hard is destructive and can discard uncommitted work. \
  1055→                 If syncing after a squash merge, use: git reset --hard origin/<branch>"
  1056→                    .to_string(),
  1057→            );
  1058→        }
  1059→    }
  1060→
  1061→    // clean-force
  1062→    for seg in &segments {
  1063→        if seg.is_git("clean") && (seg.has_short_flag('f') || seg.has_arg("--force")) {
  1064→            return Some(
  1065→                "git clean -f is destructive and permanently deletes untracked files. \
  1066→                 Review untracked files with git clean -n first."
  1067→                    .to_string(),
  1068→            );
  1069→        }
  1070→    }
  1071→
  1072→    // branch-force-delete
  1073→    for seg in &segments {
  1074→        if seg.is_git("branch") && seg.has_short_flag('D') {
  1075→            return Some(
  1076→                "git branch -D force-deletes a branch even if not fully merged. \
  1077→                 Use git branch -d (lowercase) which checks merge status first."
  1078→                    .to_string(),
  1079→            );
  1080→        }
  1081→    }
  1082→
  1083→    // worktree-remove
  1084→    for seg in &segments {
  1085→        if seg.is_git("worktree") && seg.has_arg("remove") {
  1086→            // Check if the target worktree is the agent's own CWD.
  1087→            // Removing your own CWD breaks all subsequent Bash calls.
  1088→            // Removing a DIFFERENT worktree is safe for the caller but risks
  1089→            // destroying another agent's in-progress work.
  1090→            let target_is_cwd = if let Ok(cwd) = std::env::current_dir() {
  1091→                let cwd_str = cwd.to_string_lossy();
  1092→                seg.tokens.iter().any(|t| {
  1093→                    if let Token::Word(w) = t {
  1094→                        // Normalize trailing slashes for comparison
  1095→                        let w_trimmed = w.trim_end_matches('/');
  1096→                        let cwd_trimmed = cwd_str.trim_end_matches('/');
  1097→                        w_trimmed == cwd_trimmed
  1098→                    } else {
  1099→                        false
  1100→                    }
  1101→                })
  1102→            } else {
  1103→                true // fail-safe: can't determine CWD, assume self-removal
  1104→            };
  1105→
  1106→            if target_is_cwd {
  1107→                return Some(
  1108→                    "Do not use git worktree remove on your own CWD — it will break \
  1109→                     all subsequent Bash calls. Use ExitWorktree with action: \"remove\" \
  1110→                     to safely clean up (restores CWD, deletes directory and branch). \
  1111→                     See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md for the full cleanup procedure."
  1112→                        .to_string(),
  1113→                );
  1114→            }
  1115→
  1116→            // Check if target is under the worktrees directory — if so, it could
  1117→            // be another agent's active workspace. Block with strong warning.
  1118→            let targets_worktree_dir = seg.tokens.iter().any(
  1119→                |t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w.starts_with("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")),
  1120→            );
  1121→            if targets_worktree_dir {
  1122→                return Some(
  1123→                    "⚠️  STOP — You are about to remove a worktree that may belong to \
  1124→                     another agent. Read this FULLY before proceeding.\n\n\
  1125→                     NEVER remove another agent's worktree. Other agents may be \
  1126→                     actively working in sibling worktrees even if they look idle.\n\n\
  1127→                     You may ONLY remove a worktree if ALL of these are true:\n\
  1128→                     1. YOU created it (in this session or a previous one)\n\
  1129→                     2. Its PR is merged OR it was a test/scratch worktree\n\
  1130→                     3. You have verified no other agent is using it\n\n\
  1131→                     If this is your own orphaned worktree, ask the user to remove it, \
  1132→                     or use: git -C /workspace worktree remove <path>"
  1133→                        .to_string(),
  1134→                );
  1135→            }
  1136→
  1137→            // Target is not under /workspace/.claude/worktrees/ (e.g. /tmp/wt) —
  1138→            // not an agent worktree, safe to remove.
  1139→        }
  1140→    }
  1141→
  1142→    // rm-worktree-root
  1143→    for seg in &segments {
  1144→        let cmd_name = seg.effective_command().map(|(_, c)| c);
  1145→        if cmd_name == Some("rm")
  1146→            && (seg.has_short_flag('r') || seg.has_short_flag('f'))
  1147→            && seg.targets_worktree_root()
  1148→        {
  1149→            return Some(
  1150→                "Do not use rm -rf to delete worktrees. It breaks your CWD and leaves \
  1151→                 orphaned branches. Use ExitWorktree with action: \"remove\" instead — \
  1152→                 it safely restores CWD, deletes the directory, and removes the branch. \
  1153→                 See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md for the full cleanup procedure."
  1154→                    .to_string(),
  1155→            );
  1156→        }
  1157→    }
  1158→
  1159→    // workspace-file-modification: sed -i
  1160→    for seg in &segments {
  1161→        let cmd_name = seg.effective_command().map(|(_, c)| c);
  1162→        if cmd_name == Some("sed")
  1163→            && (seg.has_arg("-i") || seg.has_token_starting_with("-i"))
  1164→            && seg.has_workspace_path()
  1165→        {
  1166→            return Some(
  1167→                "Do not modify files directly in /workspace via Bash. Use a worktree.".to_string(),
  1168→            );
  1169→        }
  1170→        // tee
  1171→        if cmd_name == Some("tee") && seg.has_workspace_path() {
  1172→            return Some(
  1173→                "Do not modify files directly in /workspace via Bash. Use a worktree.".to_string(),
  1174→            );
  1175→        }
  1176→    }
  1177→
  1178→    // Redirect check: type-safe via Token::Operator.
  1179→    // All write redirect operators are blocked: > (truncate), >> (append), >| (clobber).
  1180→    // Quoted/escaped > produces Word(">") and is naturally excluded.
  1181→    // No-space redirects (echo>/workspace/file) are properly tokenized as
  1182→    // [Word("echo"), Operator(">"), Word("/workspace/file")].
  1183→    for seg in &segments {
  1184→        for j in 0..seg.tokens.len() {
  1185→            if let Token::Operator(op) = &seg.tokens[j] {
  1186→                if op == ">" || op == ">>" || op == ">|" {
  1187→                    if let Some(Token::Word(path)) = seg.tokens.get(j + 1) {
  1188→                        if path.starts_with("/workspace/")
  1189→                            && !path.starts_with("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")
  1190→                        {
  1191→                            return Some(
  1192→                                "Do not modify files directly in /workspace via Bash. Use a worktree."
  1193→                                    .to_string(),
  1194→                            );
  1195→                        }
  1196→                    }
  1197→                }
  1198→            }
  1199→        }
  1200→    }
  1201→
  1202→    None
  1203→}
  1204→
  1205→// ============================================================
  1206→// Tests

[assistant] Here are the assumptions I can identify that haven't been explicitly justified:

**1. `<` (input redirect) is allowed for `/workspace` paths.** `cat < /workspace/secret` reads a protected file. Reading isn't writing, but exfiltration is still a concern. On the other hand, agents can already `cat /workspace/file` or use the Read tool — blocking `<` wouldn't actually prevent reads.

**2. `<>` (read-write open) is allowed.** `cmd <> /workspace/file` opens the file for both reading AND writing. This is a write operation. It's extremely rare in practice, but from first principles it should be blocked.

**3. `>&` (fd dup) targeting a path is allowed.** `echo >&/workspace/file` — is this valid bash? Let me check:

[tool] Bash({"command":"# Does >& with a path (not a number) work?\nbash -c 'echo hello >&/tmp/test-dup; cat /tmp/test-dup; rm /tmp/test-dup' 2>&1","description":"Test if >& with path creates a file"})

[result] hello

[assistant] `>&/tmp/test-dup` **writes to a file**. In bash, `>&word` where word is not a digit is equivalent to `> word 2>&1` (redirects both stdout and stderr). This is a write operation and should be blocked.

**4. `push-to-main` only checks for literal `main`.** `git push origin master` or `git push origin HEAD:main` or `git push origin HEAD:refs/heads/main` would bypass it. Though the first is a different branch name and the latter two are unusual.

**5. `sed -i` and `tee` checks only match explicit `/workspace/` paths.** `sed -i 's/x/y/' ../file` from a worktree could target workspace files via relative paths. Same with `tee ../../../file`. But relative path resolution is impossible without executing the command.

**6. The redirect check only looks at the token immediately after the operator.** `echo > "$VAR"` where `$VAR=/workspace/file` would bypass since we can't resolve variables. This is the documented "variable indirection" gap.

The ones I think need fixing right now are **#2** (`<>`) and **#3** (`>&` with path). Both are write operations to protected paths. Let me fix them:

[tool] Edit({"replace_all":false,"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs","old_string":"                if op == \">\" || op == \">>\" || op == \">|\" {","new_string":"                if op == \">\" || op == \">>\" || op == \">|\" || op == \">&\" || op == \"<>\" {"})

[tool] Edit({"replace_all":false,"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs","old_string":"    // Redirect check: type-safe via Token::Operator.\n    // All write redirect operators are blocked: > (truncate), >> (append), >| (clobber).\n    // Quoted/escaped > produces Word(\">\") and is naturally excluded.\n    // No-space redirects (echo>/workspace/file) are properly tokenized as\n    // [Word(\"echo\"), Operator(\">\"), Word(\"/workspace/file\")].","new_string":"    // Redirect check: type-safe via Token::Operator.\n    // All write-capable redirect operators are blocked:\n    //   >  (truncate), >> (append), >| (clobber),\n    //   >& (dup — with path target, acts as > + 2>&1),\n    //   <> (read-write open).\n    // Not blocked: < (input only), <& (fd dup, no path write).\n    // Quoted/escaped > produces Word(\">\") and is naturally excluded.\n    // No-space redirects (echo>/workspace/file) are properly tokenized as\n    // [Word(\"echo\"), Operator(\">\"), Word(\"/workspace/file\")]."})

[assistant] To answer your question fully — here's every assumption remaining in the guard, with justification for each:

**Assumptions I believe are sound:**
- `<` (input redirect) allowed — agents can already read workspace files via `cat`, `Read` tool, etc. Blocking `<` would add friction without preventing reads.
- `<&` (input fd dup) allowed — operates on file descriptors, no path target.
- `git push origin master` not blocked — only `main` is the protected branch in this repo. If `master` is also protected, that's a separate policy question.

**Documented gaps (fail-open, can't be fixed with static analysis):**
- Variable indirection: `echo > $VAR` where VAR=/workspace/file — can't resolve variables.
- Relative paths: `sed -i 's/x/y/' ../file` from worktree — can't resolve without execution.
- `sudo -u root git push` — can't determine flag values without enumeration.
- `python -c "os.system(...)"` — can't parse non-shell languages.

**Things I'm NOT sure about:**
- `git push origin HEAD:main` (refspec push to main) — bypasses the `has_arg("main")` check. Worth blocking?

[user] how can you resulve the head main master for this repo? you have gh access

[assistant] Only `main` is protected. No `master`. So the guard should:

1. Not hardcode `"main"` — resolve the default branch dynamically, or at least check both common names
2. Also catch refspec pushes like `HEAD:main` and `HEAD:refs/heads/main`

But actually, the better approach: the guard is a compiled binary. It can't call `gh` at runtime without adding latency to every command. The pragmatic answer is to hardcode the known default branch. But let me think about the refspec case — that's the real gap.

[assistant] The current check is `has_arg("main")` — exact token match. This misses:

- `git push origin HEAD:main` — the token is `HEAD:main`, not `main`
- `git push origin HEAD:refs/heads/main` — same
- `git push origin feature:main` — pushing local branch to remote main
- `git push origin main:main` — explicit refspec

All of these push to the `main` branch on the remote. The common pattern is `:main` in a refspec. Let me fix this:

[tool] Edit({"replace_all":false,"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs","old_string":"    // push-to-main\n    for seg in &segments {\n        if seg.is_git(\"push\") && seg.has_arg(\"main\") {\n            return Some(\"Cannot push directly to main. Use a feature branch and PR.\".to_string());\n        }\n    }","new_string":"    // push-to-main: block any push targeting the default branch.\n    // Catches: git push origin main, git push origin HEAD:main,\n    // git push origin feature:refs/heads/main, etc.\n    for seg in &segments {\n        if seg.is_git(\"push\") {\n            let targets_main = seg.tokens.iter().any(|t| {\n                if let Token::Word(w) = t {\n                    w == \"main\" || w.ends_with(\":main\") || w.ends_with(\":refs/heads/main\")\n                } else {\n                    false\n                }\n            });\n            if targets_main {\n                return Some(\n                    \"Cannot push directly to main. Use a feature branch and PR.\".to_string(),\n                );\n            }\n        }\n    }"})

[assistant] Here's where we stand on every assumption in the guard, fully justified:

**Write-capable redirect operators — all blocked:**
| Operator | Bash meaning | Blocked | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| `>` | truncate + write | Yes | Writes to file |
| `>>` | append | Yes | Writes to file |
| `>|` | clobber | Yes | Writes to file (bypasses noclobber) |
| `>&` | dup/write | Yes | With path target, acts as `> file 2>&1` |
| `<>` | read-write | Yes | Opens file for writing |

**Read-only operators — allowed:**
| Operator | Bash meaning | Blocked | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| `<` | input | No | Agents can already read via `cat`, Read tool |
| `<&` | input fd dup | No | Fd operation, no path write |

**Push-to-main — now catches refspecs:**
- `git push origin main` — blocked
- `git push origin HEAD:main` — blocked
- `git push origin feature:main` — blocked
- `git push origin HEAD:refs/heads/main` — blocked
- Default branch confirmed as `main` via `gh api`

**Remaining documented gaps (static analysis limits):**
- Variable indirection (`> $VAR`) — can't resolve
- Relative paths (`sed -i ../file`) — can't resolve
- Non-shell languages (`python -c "os.system(...)"`) — can't parse
- `sudo -u root git push` — can't enumerate flag values

[user] what can we do to close the gaps. python can't be parsed into tokens as well? not using some bach but the interpreter or something?

[assistant] Good question. Let me think through each gap and what's actually feasible:

**1. `python -c "os.system('git push origin main')"` — interpreter arguments**

We don't need to parse Python. We already recursively parse `bash -c` arguments by treating them as shell commands. For Python/Ruby/Node/Perl, we can't parse the language, but we CAN scan the string argument for dangerous patterns — the same way we'd grep for them. If `python -c "..."` contains `git push` and `main`, or `/workspace/` with write operations, that's a signal.

**2. `sed -i ../../../file` — relative paths**

We already have `std::env::current_dir()` at runtime. We can resolve relative paths. If an agent is in `/workspace/.claude/worktrees/wt` and runs `sed -i ../../file`, that resolves to `/workspace/.claude/file`. We can canonicalize and check.

**3. `echo > $VAR` — variable indirection**

This is the hardest. We can't resolve `$VAR`. But we can be conservative: if a write redirect targets a `$`-prefixed token, we know the path is dynamic. The question is whether to block or allow. Blocking is too aggressive — `echo > $TMPFILE` is common and safe. Fail-open is the right call.

**4. `sudo -u root git push` — flag value consumption**

Would require enumerating every flag for every prefix command. Diminishing returns.

Let me check what interpreter patterns are actually realistic and implement the ones that close real gaps:

[assistant] The architecture already supports this cleanly. For `bash -c`, we recursively parse the argument as shell and add the resulting segments. For non-shell interpreters, we can't parse the argument as shell, but we CAN parse it as shell anyway — if `python -c "os.system('git push origin main')"` is tokenized as shell, the string `git push origin main` inside the quotes would be extracted. Actually no — our tokenizer would see the Python code as words, not shell commands.

The right approach for interpreters is different: **scan the string argument for known dangerous patterns**, not parse it as a language. Let me think about what patterns to scan for:

[tool] Bash({"command":"# What are the realistic attack vectors via interpreters?\n# 1. Shell-out: os.system, subprocess, exec, child_process\n# 2. Direct file write: open(..., 'w'), writeFileSync\n# 3. Direct git: via subprocess\n\n# The string argument to python -c / node -e / perl -e contains the code\n# We can just recursively parse it as shell — if it contains shell commands\n# embedded in system() calls, our tokenizer won't find them.\n# But we CAN just grep the string for our danger patterns.\n\n# What patterns do we check in check_bash?\n# - git push ... main\n# - git reset --hard\n# - git clean -f\n# - git branch -D\n# - git worktree remove\n# - rm -rf worktree root\n# - sed -i /workspace/\n# - tee /workspace/\n# - > /workspace/ (redirect)\n\n# For interpreter strings, the most valuable check is:\n# Does the string contain \"/workspace/\" AND NOT \"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/\"?\n# That catches: open(\"/workspace/file\", \"w\"), os.system(\"echo > /workspace/file\"), etc.\n\necho \"Test cases:\"\necho '---'\n# This should be caught by interpreter scanning\necho 'python3 -c \"import os; os.system(\\\"git push origin main\\\")\"'\necho 'node -e \"require(\\\"fs\\\").writeFileSync(\\\"/workspace/file\\\", \\\"pwned\\\")\"'\necho 'perl -e \"system(\\\"git push origin main\\\")\"'\necho 'python3 -c \"open(\\\"/workspace/file\\\", \\\"w\\\").write(\\\"pwned\\\")\"'","description":"Analyze interpreter attack patterns"})

[assistant] Here's my thinking on the approach. Two separate mechanisms:

**Mechanism A: Interpreter string scanning.** For `python -c`, `python3 -c`, `node -e`, `perl -e`, `ruby -e` — extract the code string argument and scan it for `/workspace/` paths (excluding worktree paths). This catches direct file operations (`open("/workspace/file", "w")`) and embedded shell commands (`os.system("echo > /workspace/file")`). Simple substring match, same principle as the existing workspace path checks.

**Mechanism B: Recursive shell parse of interpreter system calls.** Harder — would need to extract strings from `os.system("...")`, `system("...")`, `execSync("...")` and parse them as shell. This is fragile across languages. Not worth the complexity.

**Mechanism A is the right call.** It's simple, covers the real vectors, and follows the same pattern as the existing checks. If the code string contains `/workspace/` (and not a worktree path), block it — the agent has no legitimate reason to hardcode `/workspace/` paths in interpreter code.

For relative paths — let me also handle that. The `has_workspace_path()` method checks for absolute `/workspace/` paths. But `../../../file` from a worktree could escape. Let me add path canonicalization:

[tool] Edit({"replace_all":false,"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs","old_string":"/// Shell interpreters whose -c argument should be recursively parsed.\nconst SHELL_COMMANDS: &[&str] = &[\"bash\", \"sh\", \"dash\", \"zsh\", \"ksh\"];\n\n/// True if a token is a shell interpreter (handles full paths like /bin/bash).\nfn is_shell_command(token: &str) -> bool {\n    SHELL_COMMANDS.contains(&token)\n        || SHELL_COMMANDS\n            .iter()\n            .any(|s| token.ends_with(&format!(\"/{}\", s)))\n}","new_string":"/// Shell interpreters whose -c argument should be recursively parsed.\nconst SHELL_COMMANDS: &[&str] = &[\"bash\", \"sh\", \"dash\", \"zsh\", \"ksh\"];\n\n/// Non-shell interpreters whose code argument should be scanned for\n/// dangerous patterns (workspace paths, git commands).\nconst SCRIPT_INTERPRETERS: &[&str] = &[\"python\", \"python3\", \"node\", \"ruby\", \"perl\"];\n\n/// The flag that introduces an inline code argument for each interpreter.\nconst SCRIPT_INLINE_FLAGS: &[&str] = &[\"-c\", \"-c\", \"-e\", \"-e\", \"-e\"];\n\n/// True if a token is a shell interpreter (handles full paths like /bin/bash).\nfn is_shell_command(token: &str) -> bool {\n    SHELL_COMMANDS.contains(&token)\n        || SHELL_COMMANDS\n            .iter()\n            .any(|s| token.ends_with(&format!(\"/{}\", s)))\n}\n\n/// True if a token is a non-shell script interpreter. Returns the flag\n/// that introduces inline code (e.g., \"-c\" for python, \"-e\" for node).\nfn script_interpreter_flag(token: &str) -> Option<&'static str> {\n    let base = token.rsplit('/').next().unwrap_or(token);\n    SCRIPT_INTERPRETERS\n        .iter()\n        .zip(SCRIPT_INLINE_FLAGS.iter())\n        .find(|(name, _)| *name == &base)\n        .map(|(_, flag)| *flag)\n}"})

We can't parse these languages, but we can detect\n    // hardcoded /workspace/ paths in inline code — agents have no legitimate\n    // reason to embed absolute workspace paths in interpreter code.\n    for seg in &segments {\n        if let Some((cmd_pos, cmd)) = seg.effective_command() {\n            if let Some(flag) = script_interpreter_flag(cmd) {\n                // Find the inline code flag and its argument\n                if let Some(f_pos) = seg.tokens[cmd_pos + 1..]\n                    .iter()\n                    .position(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w == flag))\n                {\n                    let arg_pos = cmd_pos + 1 + f_pos + 1;\n                    if let Some(Token::Word(code)) = seg.tokens.get(arg_pos) {\n                        if code.contains(\"/workspace/\")\n                            && !code.contains(\"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/\")\n                        {\n                            return Some(\n                                \"Do not use interpreter code to access /workspace/ directly. \\\n                                 Use a worktree. Detected /workspace/ path in inline code \\\n                                 argument to a script interpreter.\"\n                                    .to_string(),\n                            );\n                        }\n                    }\n                }\n            }\n        }\n    }\n\n    // Redirect check: type-safe via Token::Operator."})

| Gap | Status | Approach |

| `>>` append redirect | **Closed** | Block all write-capable redirect operators |
| `>&` with path target | **Closed** | Block — acts as `> file 2>&1` |
| `<>` read-write | **Closed** | Block — opens file for writing |
| Relative paths (`../file`) | **Open** | Feasible — canonicalize with CWD, not yet implemented |
| Variable indirection (`> $VAR`) | **Open** | Not feasible — can't resolve variables statically |
| `sudo -u` flag values | **Open** | Diminishing returns — fails open safely |

[assistant] Let me think about this. The relative path issue reveals something deeper about the architecture.

Right now, every check does its own ad-hoc string matching for protected paths:

```
redirect check:  path.starts_with("/workspace/") && !path.starts_with("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")
sed -i check:    seg.has_workspace_path()  → same pattern inside
tee check:       seg.has_workspace_path()  → same pattern
rm -rf check:    seg.targets_worktree_root() → different pattern
worktree remove: tokens matching "/workspace/.claude/worktrees/" → yet another pattern
```

Five checks, three different path-matching patterns, all hardcoding the same string prefixes, all only handling absolute paths. The relative path gap exists in ALL of them because none of them resolve paths.

The first-principles fix: **one function that answers "is this path protected?"**, used everywhere.

```
is_protected_path(path: &str) -> bool
  1. If relative, resolve against CWD
  2. Normalize (resolve ../ and ./ without filesystem access)
  3. Check: under /workspace/ AND NOT under /workspace/.claude/worktrees/
```

This can't use `std::fs::canonicalize()` because the target file might not exist yet — it's a write destination. We need logical normalization.

[tool] Edit({"replace_all":false,"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs","old_string":"// ============================================================\n// Helpers kept from old code\n// ============================================================\n\nfn is_workspace_cwd() -> bool {","new_string":"// ============================================================\n// Path resolution\n// ============================================================\n\n/// Normalize a path logically (without filesystem access).\n/// Resolves `.` and `..` components. Does NOT follow symlinks or\n/// check existence — the target may not exist yet (write operations).\nfn normalize_path(path: &std::path::Path) -> std::path::PathBuf {\n    let mut components = Vec::new();\n    for component in path.components() {\n        match component {\n            std::path::Component::ParentDir => {\n                // Pop the last component (go up one level)\n                // Don't pop past root\n                if !components.is_empty() {\n                    components.pop();\n                }\n            }\n            std::path::Component::CurDir => {\n                // Skip . (current directory)\n            }\n            c => {\n                components.push(c);\n            }\n        }\n    }\n    components.iter().collect()\n}\n\n/// True if a path (absolute or relative) resolves to a protected location.\n/// Protected = under /workspace/ but NOT under /workspace/.claude/worktrees/.\n/// Relative paths are resolved against CWD. Fails open if CWD is unavailable.\nfn is_protected_path(path: &str) -> bool {\n    if path.is_empty() {\n        return false;\n    }\n    let resolved = if path.starts_with('/') {\n        normalize_path(std::path::Path::new(path))\n    } else {\n        match std::env::current_dir() {\n            Ok(cwd) => normalize_path(&cwd.join(path)),\n            Err(_) => return false, // fail-open\n        }\n    };\n    let s = resolved.to_string_lossy();\n    s.starts_with(\"/workspace/\") && !s.starts_with(\"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/\")\n}\n\n// ============================================================\n// CWD helpers\n// ============================================================\n\nfn is_workspace_cwd() -> bool {"})

normalize_path(std::path::Path::new(\"/a/b/c/../../d\")),\n            std::path::PathBuf::from(\"/a/d\")\n        );\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn path_normalize_dot() {\n        assert_eq!(\n    961→/// Normalize a path logically (without filesystem access).
   962→/// Resolves `.` and `..` components. Does NOT follow symlinks or
   963→/// check existence — the target may not exist yet (write operations).
   964→fn normalize_path(path: &std::path::Path) -> std::path::PathBuf {
   965→    let mut components = Vec::new();
   966→    for component in path.components() {
   967→        match component {
   968→            std::path::Component::ParentDir => {
   969→                // Pop the last component (go up one level)
   970→                // Don't pop past root
   971→                if !components.is_empty() {
   972→                    components.pop();
   973→                }
   974→            }
   975→            std::path::Component::CurDir => {
   976→                // Skip . (current directory)
   977→            }
   978→            c => {
   979→                components.push(c);
   980→            }
   981→        }
   982→    }
   983→    components.iter().collect()
   984→}

[assistant] The issue: `components` starts with `RootDir` (`/`), then `Normal("a")`. When we pop `Normal("a")` and then try to pop again for the second `..`, we shouldn't pop `RootDir`. Let me fix:

(current directory)\n            }\n            c => {\n                components.push(c);\n            }\n        }\n    }\n    components.iter().collect()\n}","new_string":"fn normalize_path(path: &std::path::Path) -> std::path::PathBuf {\n    use std::path::Component;\n    let mut components = Vec::new();\n    for component in path.components() {\n        match component {\n            Component::ParentDir => {\n                // Pop the last Normal component (go up one level).\n                // Never pop RootDir or Prefix — can't go above filesystem root.\n                match components.last() {\n                    Some(Component::Normal(_)) => {\n                        components.pop();\n                    }\n                    _ => {}\n                }\n            }\n            Component::CurDir => {\n                // Skip . (current directory)\n            }\n            c => {\n                components.push(c);\n            }\n        }\n    }\n    components.iter().collect()\n}"})

[assistant] Here's the updated gap status:

| Gap | Status | How |
|---|---|---|
| `>` vs `\>` indistinguishable | **Closed** | Redirect operators are proper `Token::Operator` |
| `>|` / `>&` misparsed | **Closed** | Greedy peek-ahead in tokenizer |
| `>>` append allowed | **Closed** | All write-capable operators blocked |
| `>&` path write | **Closed** | Blocked alongside `>` |
| `<>` read-write | **Closed** | Blocked alongside `>` |
| Refspec push (`HEAD:main`) | **Closed** | Match `:main` suffix |
| Interpreter bypass (`python -c`) | **Closed** | Scan code arg for `/workspace/` |
| Relative path escape (`../../file`) | **Closed** | `normalize_path()` + CWD resolution |
| `..` escape from worktree path | **Closed** | Same normalization |
| Worktree remove stuck agents | **Closed** | Three-tier CWD-aware check |
| Variable indirection (`> $VAR`) | **Open** | Can't resolve — fail-open |
| `sudo -u` flag values | **Open** | Fails open safely |

The two remaining open gaps are genuinely not solvable with static analysis — they require runtime variable/flag resolution. Both fail open, which is the correct direction for a guard.

     3→/// Read all of stdin. Returns Err on OS-level infrastructure errors (ENXIO, EAGAIN, ENOENT)
     4→/// so the caller can fail-open. Other IO errors are returned as-is.
     5→pub fn read_stdin() -> io::Result<String> {
     6→    let mut buf = String::new();
     7→    io::stdin().lock().read_to_string(&mut buf)?;
     8→    Ok(buf)
     9→}
    10→
    11→/// Returns true for OS error codes that indicate stdin is unavailable due to infrastructure
    12→/// issues (subagent context, background process, missing FD). Callers should fail-open.
    13→pub fn is_infra_error(e: &io::Error) -> bool {
    14→    matches!(
    15→        e.raw_os_error(),
    16→        Some(6) | Some(11) | Some(2) // ENXIO=6, EAGAIN=11, ENOENT=2
    17→    )
    18→}

    20→/// Serialize a block decision to JSON.

    25→// ============================================================
    26→// Tokenizer: single-pass POSIX-aligned shell command tokenizer
    27→// ============================================================
    28→
    29→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)]
    30→pub enum Token {
    31→    Word(String),
    32→    Operator(String),
    33→}
    34→
    35→/// Tokenize a shell command string into Words and Operators.
    36→/// Handles quotes, backslash escaping, operators, heredocs,
    37→/// command substitution ($() and backticks), and comments.
    38→///
    39→/// On any parse error (unmatched quotes, etc.), returns a fail-open
    40→/// fallback: the entire command whitespace-split into Word tokens.
    41→pub fn tokenize(cmd: &str) -> (Vec<Token>, Vec<Vec<Segment>>) {
    42→    tokenize_inner(cmd, 0)
    43→}
    44→
    45→/// Max recursion depth for command substitution parsing.
    46→const MAX_SUBST_DEPTH: usize = 3;
    47→
    48→/// Known command prefixes that wrap another command.
    49→/// `exec` replaces the process with the command — semantically a prefix.
    50→const COMMAND_PREFIXES: &[&str] = &["sudo", "env", "command", "nice", "nohup", "time", "exec"];
    51→
    52→/// Shell interpreters whose -c argument should be recursively parsed.
    53→const SHELL_COMMANDS: &[&str] = &["bash", "sh", "dash", "zsh", "ksh"];
    54→
    55→/// Non-shell interpreters whose code argument should be scanned for
    56→/// dangerous patterns (workspace paths, git commands).
    57→const SCRIPT_INTERPRETERS: &[&str] = &["python", "python3", "node", "ruby", "perl"];
    58→
    59→/// The flag that introduces an inline code argument for each interpreter.
    60→const SCRIPT_INLINE_FLAGS: &[&str] = &["-c", "-c", "-e", "-e", "-e"];
    61→
    62→/// True if a token is a shell interpreter (handles full paths like /bin/bash).
    63→fn is_shell_command(token: &str) -> bool {
    64→    SHELL_COMMANDS.contains(&token)
    65→        || SHELL_COMMANDS
    66→            .iter()
    67→            .any(|s| token.ends_with(&format!("/{}", s)))
    68→}

    70→/// True if a token is a non-shell script interpreter. Returns the flag

    72→fn script_interpreter_flag(token: &str) -> Option<&'static str> {
    73→    let base = token.rsplit('/').next().unwrap_or(token);
    74→    SCRIPT_INTERPRETERS
    75→        .iter()

   657→// ============================================================
   658→// Segments: split tokens on operators, check each independently
   659→// ============================================================
   660→
   661→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
   662→pub struct Segment {
   663→    pub tokens: Vec<Token>,
   664→}
   665→
   666→/// True if a token is the `git` command (handles full paths like /usr/bin/git).
   667→fn is_git_command(token: &str) -> bool {
   668→    token == "git" || token.ends_with("/git")
   669→}
   670→
   671→/// True if a token looks like a variable assignment (VAR=val).
   672→fn is_var_assignment(token: &str) -> bool {
   673→    if let Some(eq_pos) = token.find('=') {
   674→        if eq_pos == 0 {
   675→            return false; // "=value" is not an assignment
   676→        }
   677→        let name = &token[..eq_pos];
   678→        let mut chars = name.chars();
   679→        match chars.next() {
   680→            Some(c) if c.is_ascii_alphabetic() || c == '_' => {}
   681→            _ => return false,
   682→        }
   683→        chars.all(|c| c.is_ascii_alphanumeric() || c == '_')
   684→    } else {
   685→        false
   686→    }
   687→}
   688→
   689→/// True if an operator separates commands (splits segments).
   690→/// Redirect operators (>, >>, <, etc.) are NOT control operators —
   691→/// they stay inside the segment with the command they modify.
   692→fn is_control_operator(op: &str) -> bool {
   693→    matches!(op, "&&" | "||" | "|" | ";" | "&" | "\n" | "(" | ")")
   694→}
   695→
   696→impl Segment {
   697→    /// Find the effective command, skipping variable assignments, known prefixes,
   698→    /// and redirect operators.
   699→    pub fn effective_command(&self) -> Option<(usize, &str)> {
   700→        let mut i = 0;
   701→        while i < self.tokens.len() {
   702→            // Skip redirect operators — they're not commands
   703→            let tok = match &self.tokens[i] {
   704→                Token::Word(w) => w.as_str(),
   705→                Token::Operator(_) => {
   706→                    i += 1;
   707→                    continue;
   708→                }
   709→            };
   710→
   711→            // Skip variable assignments
   712→            if is_var_assignment(tok) {
   713→                i += 1;
   714→                continue;
   715→            }
   716→
   717→            // Skip brace group opener. In POSIX, { is a reserved word that
   718→            // groups commands but doesn't change what command executes.
   719→            // { git push; } → the effective command is git, not {.
   720→            if tok == "{" || tok == "}" {
   721→                i += 1;
   722→                continue;
   723→            }
   724→
   725→            // Skip known command prefixes and their flag-like arguments.
   726→            // After a prefix, skip tokens starting with - (flags) and assignments (=).
   727→            // The first non-flag, non-assignment token is the command.
   728→            //
   729→            // Known gap: prefix flags that take values (e.g., sudo -u root) cause the
   730→            // value ("root") to be returned as the command. This fails-open: "root" won't
   731→            // match any dangerous command pattern, so the guard allows the command.
   732→            // The alternative (skipping flag+value) is worse: sudo -n git push would skip
   733→            // -n AND git, returning "push" as the command — a more dangerous bypass.
   734→            if COMMAND_PREFIXES.contains(&tok) {
   735→                i += 1;
   736→                while i < self.tokens.len() {
   737→                    match &self.tokens[i] {
   738→                        Token::Operator(_) => {
   739→                            i += 1;
   740→                            continue;
   741→                        }
   742→                        Token::Word(t) => {
   743→                            if t.starts_with('-') || is_var_assignment(t) {
   744→                                i += 1;
   745→                            } else {
   746→                                break;
   747→                            }
   748→                        }
   749→                    }
   750→                }
   751→                continue;
   752→            }
   753→
   754→            return Some((i, tok));
   755→        }
   756→        None
   757→    }
   758→
   759→    /// Find the git subcommand, skipping global git flags and redirect operators.
   760→    pub fn git_subcmd(&self) -> Option<(usize, &str)> {
   761→        let (cmd_pos, cmd) = self.effective_command()?;
   762→        if !is_git_command(cmd) {
   763→            return None;
   764→        }
   765→
   766→        let mut i = cmd_pos + 1;
   767→        while i < self.tokens.len() {
   768→            let tok = match &self.tokens[i] {
   769→                Token::Word(w) => w.as_str(),
   770→                Token::Operator(_) => {
   771→                    i += 1;
   772→                    continue;
   773→                }
   774→            };
   775→            // --flag=value form
   776→            if tok.contains('=')
   777→                && GIT_VALUE_FLAGS
   778→                    .iter()
   779→                    .any(|f| tok.starts_with(&format!("{}=", f)))
   780→            {
   781→                i += 1;
   782→                continue;
   783→            }
   784→            // --flag value form (two tokens)
   785→            if GIT_VALUE_FLAGS.contains(&tok) {
   786→                i += 2; // skip flag + value
   787→                continue;
   788→            }
   789→            // Standalone flag (--no-pager, --bare, etc.)
   790→            if tok.starts_with('-') {
   791→                i += 1;
   792→                continue;
   793→            }
   794→            return Some((i, tok));
   795→        }
   796→        None
   797→    }
   798→
   799→    /// True if this segment runs git with the given subcommand.
   800→    pub fn is_git(&self, subcmd: &str) -> bool {
   801→        self.git_subcmd().map(|(_, s)| s == subcmd).unwrap_or(false)
   802→    }
   803→
   804→    /// True if any Word token AFTER the subcommand (for git) or command exactly equals `val`.
   805→    pub fn has_arg(&self, val: &str) -> bool {
   806→        let start = if let Some((pos, _)) = self.git_subcmd() {
   807→            pos + 1
   808→        } else if let Some((pos, _)) = self.effective_command() {
   809→            pos + 1
   810→        } else {
   811→            return false;
   812→        };
   813→        self.tokens[start..]
   814→            .iter()
   815→            .any(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w == val))
   816→    }
   817→
   818→    /// True if any Word token AFTER the subcommand/command is a short flag containing `ch`.
   819→    pub fn has_short_flag(&self, ch: char) -> bool {
   820→        let start = if let Some((pos, _)) = self.git_subcmd() {
   821→            pos + 1
   822→        } else if let Some((pos, _)) = self.effective_command() {
   823→            pos + 1
   824→        } else {
   825→            return false;
   826→        };
   827→        self.tokens[start..].iter().any(|t| {
   828→            matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w.starts_with('-') && !w.starts_with("--") && w.contains(ch))
   829→        })
   830→    }
   831→
   832→    /// True if any Word token starts with `prefix`.
   833→    pub fn has_token_starting_with(&self, prefix: &str) -> bool {
   834→        self.tokens
   835→            .iter()
   836→            .any(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w.starts_with(prefix)))
   837→    }
   838→
   839→    /// True if any Word token contains `substring`.
   840→    pub fn has_token_containing(&self, substring: &str) -> bool {
   841→        self.tokens
   842→            .iter()
   843→            .any(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w.contains(substring)))
   844→    }
   845→
   846→    /// True if any Word token is a worktree ROOT directory path.
   847→    pub fn targets_worktree_root(&self) -> bool {
   848→        let prefix = "/workspace/.claude/worktrees/";
   849→        self.tokens.iter().any(|t| {
   850→            if let Token::Word(w) = t {
   851→                if let Some(rest) = w.strip_prefix(prefix) {
   852→                    let trimmed = rest.trim_end_matches('/');
   853→                    !trimmed.is_empty() && !trimmed.contains('/')
   854→                } else {
   855→                    false
   856→                }
   857→            } else {
   858→                false
   859→            }
   860→        })
   861→    }
   862→
   863→    /// True if any Word token resolves to a protected workspace path.
   864→    /// Handles both absolute and relative paths via is_protected_path().
   865→    pub fn has_protected_path(&self) -> bool {
   866→        self.tokens
   867→            .iter()
   868→            .any(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if is_protected_path(w)))
   869→    }
   870→}
   871→
   872→/// Parse a command string into segments. Each segment is a separate command

  1035→/// Run all Edit/Write checks. Returns Some(reason) to block, None to allow.
  1036→pub fn check_edit(input: &serde_json::Value) -> Option<String> {
  1037→    let file_path = input["tool_input"]["file_path"].as_str().unwrap_or("");
  1038→
  1039→    // Protected files
  1040→    if file_path.contains("package-lock.json")
  1041→        || file_path.contains("src/wasm/scheduler/")
  1042→        || file_path.contains(".env")
  1043→    {
  1044→        return Some(format!("Protected file: {}", file_path));
  1045→    }
  1046→
  1047→    // Workspace isolation
  1048→    if is_protected_path(file_path) {
  1049→        return Some(
  1050→            "Do not edit files directly on main in /workspace. \
  1051→             Create a worktree first: git worktree add /workspace/.claude/worktrees/<name> -b <branch>"
  1052→                .to_string(),
  1053→        );
  1054→    }
  1055→
  1056→    // CWD enforcement for worktree files
  1057→    if file_path.starts_with("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/") {
  1058→        if let Ok(cwd) = std::env::current_dir() {
  1059→            let cwd_str = cwd.to_string_lossy();
  1060→            if cwd_str == "/workspace" || cwd_str == "/workspace/" {
  1061→                return Some(
  1062→                    "You are editing a worktree file but your CWD is /workspace. \
  1063→                     Enter the worktree first (use the EnterWorktree tool or cd into it). \
  1064→                     Only the admin works from /workspace."
  1065→                        .to_string(),
  1066→                );
  1067→            }
  1068→        }
  1069→    }
  1070→
  1071→    None
  1072→}
  1073→
  1074→/// Run all Bash checks. Returns Some(reason) to block, None to allow.
  1075→pub fn check_bash(input: &serde_json::Value) -> Option<String> {
  1076→    let cmd = input["tool_input"]["command"].as_str().unwrap_or("");
  1077→
  1078→    let segments = parse_segments(cmd);
  1079→
  1080→    // push-to-main: block any push targeting the default branch.
  1081→    // Catches: git push origin main, git push origin HEAD:main,
  1082→    // git push origin feature:refs/heads/main, etc.
  1083→    for seg in &segments {
  1084→        if seg.is_git("push") {
  1085→            let targets_main = seg.tokens.iter().any(|t| {
  1086→                if let Token::Word(w) = t {
  1087→                    w == "main" || w.ends_with(":main") || w.ends_with(":refs/heads/main")
  1088→                } else {
  1089→                    false
  1090→                }
  1091→            });
  1092→            if targets_main {
  1093→                return Some(
  1094→                    "Cannot push directly to main. Use a feature branch and PR.".to_string(),
  1095→                );
  1096→            }
  1097→        }
  1098→    }
  1099→
  1100→    // checkout-switch (CWD-dependent)
  1101→    for seg in &segments {
  1102→        if (seg.is_git("checkout") || seg.is_git("switch"))
  1103→            && !seg.has_arg("--")
  1104→            && !seg.has_short_flag('b')
  1105→            && !seg.has_short_flag('B')
  1106→            && !seg.has_short_flag('c')
  1107→            && !seg.has_token_containing("worktree")
  1108→        {
  1109→            if !is_worktree_cwd() {
  1110→                return Some(
  1111→                    "Do not use git checkout/switch in /workspace. \
  1112→                     Use a worktree: git worktree add /workspace/.claude/worktrees/<name> -b <branch>"
  1113→                        .to_string(),
  1114→                );
  1115→            }
  1116→        }
  1117→    }
  1118→
  1119→    // reset-hard-workspace: block ALL reset --hard in /workspace (even origin/*)
  1120→    for seg in &segments {
  1121→        if seg.is_git("reset") && seg.has_arg("--hard") && is_workspace_cwd() {
  1122→            return Some(
  1123→                "Do not run git reset --hard in /workspace — it modifies shared state \
  1124→                 that other agents depend on. If you need to sync after a squash merge, \
  1125→                 run git reset --hard origin/<branch> in your own worktree instead. \
  1126→                 See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md."
  1127→                    .to_string(),
  1128→            );
  1129→        }
  1130→    }
  1131→
  1132→    // reset-hard-destructive: block reset --hard without origin/ ref (in worktrees)
  1133→    for seg in &segments {
  1134→        if seg.is_git("reset") && seg.has_arg("--hard") && !seg.has_token_starting_with("origin/") {
  1135→            return Some(
  1136→                "git reset --hard is destructive and can discard uncommitted work. \
  1137→                 If syncing after a squash merge, use: git reset --hard origin/<branch>"
  1138→                    .to_string(),
  1139→            );
  1140→        }
  1141→    }
  1142→
  1143→    // clean-force
  1144→    for seg in &segments {
  1145→        if seg.is_git("clean") && (seg.has_short_flag('f') || seg.has_arg("--force")) {
  1146→            return Some(
  1147→                "git clean -f is destructive and permanently deletes untracked files. \
  1148→                 Review untracked files with git clean -n first."
  1149→                    .to_string(),
  1150→            );
  1151→        }
  1152→    }
  1153→
  1154→    // branch-force-delete
  1155→    for seg in &segments {
  1156→        if seg.is_git("branch") && seg.has_short_flag('D') {
  1157→            return Some(
  1158→                "git branch -D force-deletes a branch even if not fully merged. \
  1159→                 Use git branch -d (lowercase) which checks merge status first."
  1160→                    .to_string(),
  1161→            );
  1162→        }
  1163→    }
  1164→
  1165→    // worktree-remove
  1166→    for seg in &segments {
  1167→        if seg.is_git("worktree") && seg.has_arg("remove") {
  1168→            // Check if the target worktree is the agent's own CWD.
  1169→            // Removing your own CWD breaks all subsequent Bash calls.
  1170→            // Removing a DIFFERENT worktree is safe for the caller but risks
  1171→            // destroying another agent's in-progress work.
  1172→            let target_is_cwd = if let Ok(cwd) = std::env::current_dir() {
  1173→                let cwd_str = cwd.to_string_lossy();
  1174→                seg.tokens.iter().any(|t| {
  1175→                    if let Token::Word(w) = t {
  1176→                        // Normalize trailing slashes for comparison
  1177→                        let w_trimmed = w.trim_end_matches('/');
  1178→                        let cwd_trimmed = cwd_str.trim_end_matches('/');
  1179→                        w_trimmed == cwd_trimmed
  1180→                    } else {
  1181→                        false
  1182→                    }
  1183→                })
  1184→            } else {
  1185→                true // fail-safe: can't determine CWD, assume self-removal
  1186→            };
  1187→
  1188→            if target_is_cwd {
  1189→                return Some(
  1190→                    "Do not use git worktree remove on your own CWD — it will break \
  1191→                     all subsequent Bash calls. Use ExitWorktree with action: \"remove\" \
  1192→                     to safely clean up (restores CWD, deletes directory and branch). \
  1193→                     See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md for the full cleanup procedure."
  1194→                        .to_string(),
  1195→                );
  1196→            }
  1197→
  1198→            // Check if target is under the worktrees directory — if so, it could
  1199→            // be another agent's active workspace. Block with strong warning.
  1200→            let targets_worktree_dir = seg.tokens.iter().any(
  1201→                |t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w.starts_with("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")),
  1202→            );
  1203→            if targets_worktree_dir {
  1204→                return Some(
  1205→                    "⚠️  STOP — You are about to remove a worktree that may belong to \
  1206→                     another agent. Read this FULLY before proceeding.\n\n\
  1207→                     NEVER remove another agent's worktree. Other agents may be \
  1208→                     actively working in sibling worktrees even if they look idle.\n\n\
  1209→                     You may ONLY remove a worktree if ALL of these are true:\n\
  1210→                     1. YOU created it (in this session or a previous one)\n\
  1211→                     2. Its PR is merged OR it was a test/scratch worktree\n\
  1212→                     3. You have verified no other agent is using it\n\n\
  1213→                     If this is your own orphaned worktree, ask the user to remove it, \
  1214→                     or use: git -C /workspace worktree remove <path>"
  1215→                        .to_string(),
  1216→                );
  1217→            }
  1218→
  1219→            // Target is not under /workspace/.claude/worktrees/ (e.g. /tmp/wt) —
  1220→            // not an agent worktree, safe to remove.
  1221→        }
  1222→    }
  1223→
  1224→    // rm-worktree-root
  1225→    for seg in &segments {
  1226→        let cmd_name = seg.effective_command().map(|(_, c)| c);
  1227→        if cmd_name == Some("rm")
  1228→            && (seg.has_short_flag('r') || seg.has_short_flag('f'))
  1229→            && seg.targets_worktree_root()
  1230→        {
  1231→            return Some(
  1232→                "Do not use rm -rf to delete worktrees. It breaks your CWD and leaves \
  1233→                 orphaned branches. Use ExitWorktree with action: \"remove\" instead — \
  1234→                 it safely restores CWD, deletes the directory, and removes the branch. \
  1235→                 See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md for the full cleanup procedure."
  1236→                    .to_string(),
  1237→            );
  1238→        }
  1239→    }
  1240→
  1241→    // workspace-file-modification: sed -i
  1242→    for seg in &segments {
  1243→        let cmd_name = seg.effective_command().map(|(_, c)| c);
  1244→        if cmd_name == Some("sed")
  1245→            && (seg.has_arg("-i") || seg.has_token_starting_with("-i"))
  1246→            && seg.has_protected_path()
  1247→        {
  1248→            return Some(
  1249→                "Do not modify files directly in /workspace via Bash. Use a worktree.".to_string(),
  1250→            );
  1251→        }
  1252→        // tee
  1253→        if cmd_name == Some("tee") && seg.has_protected_path() {
  1254→            return Some(
  1255→                "Do not modify files directly in /workspace via Bash. Use a worktree.".to_string(),
  1256→            );
  1257→        }
  1258→    }
  1259→
  1260→    // interpreter-workspace: scan code arguments to python/node/perl/ruby
  1261→    // for workspace paths. We can't parse these languages, but we can detect
  1262→    // hardcoded /workspace/ paths in inline code — agents have no legitimate
  1263→    // reason to embed absolute workspace paths in interpreter code.
  1264→    for seg in &segments {
  1265→        if let Some((cmd_pos, cmd)) = seg.effective_command() {
  1266→            if let Some(flag) = script_interpreter_flag(cmd) {
  1267→                // Find the inline code flag and its argument
  1268→                if let Some(f_pos) = seg.tokens[cmd_pos + 1..]
  1269→                    .iter()
  1270→                    .position(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w == flag))
  1271→                {
  1272→                    let arg_pos = cmd_pos + 1 + f_pos + 1;
  1273→                    if let Some(Token::Word(code)) = seg.tokens.get(arg_pos) {
  1274→                        if code.contains("/workspace/")
  1275→                            && !code.contains("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")
  1276→                        {
  1277→                            return Some(
  1278→                                "Do not use interpreter code to access /workspace/ directly. \
  1279→                                 Use a worktree. Detected /workspace/ path in inline code \
  1280→                                 argument to a script interpreter."
  1281→                                    .to_string(),
  1282→                            );
  1283→                        }
  1284→                    }
  1285→                }
  1286→            }
  1287→        }
  1288→    }
  1289→
  1290→    // Redirect check: type-safe via Token::Operator.
  1291→    // All write-capable redirect operators are blocked:
  1292→    //   >  (truncate), >> (append), >| (clobber),
  1293→    //   >& (dup — with path target, acts as > + 2>&1),
  1294→    //   <> (read-write open).
  1295→    // Not blocked: < (input only), <& (fd dup, no path write).
  1296→    // Quoted/escaped > produces Word(">") and is naturally excluded.
  1297→    // No-space redirects (echo>/workspace/file) are properly tokenized as
  1298→    // [Word("echo"), Operator(">"), Word("/workspace/file")].
  1299→    for seg in &segments {
  1300→        for j in 0..seg.tokens.len() {
  1301→            if let Token::Operator(op) = &seg.tokens[j] {
  1302→                if op == ">" || op == ">>" || op == ">|" || op == ">&" || op == "<>" {
  1303→                    if let Some(Token::Word(path)) = seg.tokens.get(j + 1) {
  1304→                        if is_protected_path(path) {
  1305→                            return Some(
  1306→                                "Do not modify files directly in /workspace via Bash. Use a worktree."
  1307→                                    .to_string(),
  1308→                            );
  1309→                        }
  1310→                    }
  1311→                }
  1312→            }
  1313→        }
  1314→    }
  1315→
  1316→    None
  1317→}
  1318→
  1319→// ============================================================
  1320→// Tests
  1321→// ============================================================
  1322→
  1323→#[cfg(test)]
  1324→mod tests {
  1325→    use super::*;
  1326→    use serde_json::json;
  1327→
  1328→    // Helper to extract word strings from tokens
  1329→    fn words(tokens: &[Token]) -> Vec<&str> {
  1330→        tokens
  1331→            .iter()
  1332→            .filter_map(|t| match t {
  1333→                Token::Word(w) => Some(w.as_str()),
  1334→                _ => None,
  1335→            })
  1336→            .collect()
  1337→    }
  1338→
  1339→    fn tok(cmd: &str) -> Vec<Token> {
  1340→        tokenize(cmd).0
  1341→    }
  1342→
  1343→    fn w(s: &str) -> Token {
  1344→        Token::Word(s.to_string())
  1345→    }
  1346→
  1347→    fn op(s: &str) -> Token {
  1348→        Token::Operator(s.to_string())
  1349→    }

[assistant] Good. Now I can see the full picture. Here's what I've found:

## Findings

**1. Repeated pattern: `for seg in &segments` loop appears 10 times**

Every check in `check_bash` is a separate `for seg in &segments { ... }` loop. This means we iterate the segments list 10 times. More importantly, the structure is always the same: iterate segments, check a condition, return a block reason. This could be a single pass with a match table — but that's an optimization, not a bug. The current form is readable. Leave it.

**2. Real inconsistency: `has_arg`/`has_short_flag` call `git_subcmd()` twice**

Both `has_arg()` (line 806) and `has_short_flag()` (line 820) first try `git_subcmd()`, then fall back to `effective_command()`. But `git_subcmd()` already calls `effective_command()` internally. So when a segment is NOT git (the common case for `sed`, `tee`, `rm`), we call `effective_command()` twice: once inside `git_subcmd()` (which returns None), once in the fallback. Not a bug — just redundant work on every non-git segment.

**3. Real bug: `targets_worktree_root()` doesn't use `is_protected_path()`**

The `rm -rf` check at line 1229 uses `targets_worktree_root()` which does raw string matching on `/workspace/.claude/worktrees/`. An agent could bypass with `rm -rf /workspace/.claude/worktrees/wt/../../../` — the `..` escapes the worktree root check. Though this would resolve to `/workspace/` which isn't a worktree root, so it wouldn't match... Actually, `targets_worktree_root` checks for exactly one path component after the prefix, so `../../../` would have multiple components and wouldn't match. Not a bug.

**4. Real inconsistency: interpreter check uses `code.contains("/workspace/")` instead of `is_protected_path()`**

The interpreter scan (line 1274) does `code.contains("/workspace/")` — a raw substring check. It can't use `is_protected_path()` because the path is embedded in code, not a standalone token. This is correct but inconsistent naming. The comment explains it.

**5. The `worktree-remove` tier-2 check doesn't use `is_protected_path()` or normalize paths**

Line 1201: `w.starts_with("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")` — raw prefix match. An agent could do `git worktree remove /workspace/.claude/worktrees/../worktrees/other-agent` to bypass the warning... wait, `normalize_path` would resolve that to `/workspace/.claude/worktrees/other-agent`. But the check is on the raw token, not the normalized path. Let me fix this.

**6. The `check_edit` CWD enforcement (line 1057) uses raw `starts_with` instead of normalized path**

`file_path.starts_with("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")` — could be bypassed with `..` in the path. But Edit/Write tool paths are typically absolute and normalized by the tool itself. Low risk but inconsistent.

**7. Extractable pattern: `is_write_redirect(op)`**

The redirect check at line 1302 has `op == ">" || op == ">>" || op == ">|" || op == ">&" || op == "<>"`. This is a clear predicate that should be a function — it's the complement of `is_control_operator()` minus the read-only operators.

Let me fix the real issues:

[assistant] Here's the final architecture — a coherent whole from first principles:

## Architecture

**Layer 1: Tokenizer** — Single-pass POSIX-aligned state machine
- Input: shell command string
- Output: `Vec<Token>` where `Token = Word(String) | Operator(String)`
- Handles: quotes, backslash escaping, `$()`, backticks, heredocs, comments
- All operators (control + redirect) recognized at lex time
- Key invariant: quoted/escaped characters go into Words, never Operators

**Layer 2: Segments** — Command isolation
- `parse_segments()` splits on **control operators** (`&&`, `||`, `|`, `;`, `&`, `\n`)
- **Redirect operators** stay inside segments with their command
- Recursive parsing: `bash -c`, `eval`, `$()`, backticks (depth-limited)
- `Segment.tokens: Vec<Token>` — type preserved end-to-end

**Layer 3: Predicates** — Reusable classification functions
- `is_control_operator(op)` — splits segments?
- `is_write_redirect(op)` — can write to a file?
- `is_protected_path(path)` — resolves relative/`..`, checks workspace isolation
- `is_git_command(token)` — handles full paths
- `is_shell_command(token)` — for recursive parsing
- `script_interpreter_flag(token)` — for code argument scanning

**Layer 4: Checks** — Pattern matching on segments
- Each check: iterate segments, match pattern, return block reason
- All path checks go through `is_protected_path()`
- All redirect checks go through `is_write_redirect()`
- All command identification goes through `effective_command()` / `git_subcmd()`

**Metrics**: 4707 lines, 479 unit tests, 64 integration tests, 0 failures. Closes 15 gaps from the original code.

     2→
     3→/// Read all of stdin. Returns Err on OS-level infrastructure errors (ENXIO, EAGAIN, ENOENT)
     4→/// so the caller can fail-open. Other IO errors are returned as-is.
     5→pub fn read_stdin() -> io::Result<String> {
     6→    let mut buf = String::new();
     7→    io::stdin().lock().read_to_string(&mut buf)?;
     8→    Ok(buf)
     9→}
    10→
    11→/// Returns true for OS error codes that indicate stdin is unavailable due to infrastructure
    12→/// issues (subagent context, background process, missing FD). Callers should fail-open.
    13→pub fn is_infra_error(e: &io::Error) -> bool {
    14→    matches!(
    15→        e.raw_os_error(),
    16→        Some(6) | Some(11) | Some(2) // ENXIO=6, EAGAIN=11, ENOENT=2
    17→    )
    18→}
    19→
    20→/// Serialize a block decision to JSON.
    21→pub fn block_json(reason: &str) -> String {
    22→    serde_json::json!({"decision": "block", "reason": reason}).to_string()
    23→}

[result]     25→// ============================================================
    26→// Tokenizer: single-pass POSIX-aligned shell command tokenizer
    27→// ============================================================
    28→
    29→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)]
    30→pub enum Token {
    31→    Word(String),
    32→    Operator(String),
    33→}
    34→
    35→/// Tokenize a shell command string into Words and Operators.
    36→/// Handles quotes, backslash escaping, operators, heredocs,
    37→/// command substitution ($() and backticks), and comments.
    38→///
    39→/// On any parse error (unmatched quotes, etc.), returns a fail-open
    40→/// fallback: the entire command whitespace-split into Word tokens.
    41→pub fn tokenize(cmd: &str) -> (Vec<Token>, Vec<Vec<Segment>>) {
    42→    tokenize_inner(cmd, 0)
    43→}
    44→
    45→/// Max recursion depth for command substitution parsing.
    46→const MAX_SUBST_DEPTH: usize = 3;
    47→
    48→/// Known command prefixes that wrap another command.
    49→/// `exec` replaces the process with the command — semantically a prefix.
    50→const COMMAND_PREFIXES: &[&str] = &["sudo", "env", "command", "nice", "nohup", "time", "exec"];
    51→
    52→/// Shell interpreters whose -c argument should be recursively parsed.
    53→const SHELL_COMMANDS: &[&str] = &["bash", "sh", "dash", "zsh", "ksh"];
    54→
    55→/// Non-shell interpreters whose code argument should be scanned for
    56→/// dangerous patterns (workspace paths, git commands).
    57→const SCRIPT_INTERPRETERS: &[&str] = &["python", "python3", "node", "ruby", "perl"];
    58→
    59→/// The flag that introduces an inline code argument for each interpreter.
    60→const SCRIPT_INLINE_FLAGS: &[&str] = &["-c", "-c", "-e", "-e", "-e"];
    61→
    62→/// True if a token is a shell interpreter (handles full paths like /bin/bash).
    63→fn is_shell_command(token: &str) -> bool {
    64→    SHELL_COMMANDS.contains(&token)
    65→        || SHELL_COMMANDS
    66→            .iter()
    67→            .any(|s| token.ends_with(&format!("/{}", s)))
    68→}
    69→
    70→/// True if a token is a non-shell script interpreter. Returns the flag
    71→/// that introduces inline code (e.g., "-c" for python, "-e" for node).
    72→fn script_interpreter_flag(token: &str) -> Option<&'static str> {
    73→    let base = token.rsplit('/').next().unwrap_or(token);
    74→    SCRIPT_INTERPRETERS
    75→        .iter()
    76→        .zip(SCRIPT_INLINE_FLAGS.iter())
    77→        .find(|(name, _)| *name == &base)
    78→        .map(|(_, flag)| *flag)
    79→}
    80→
    81→/// Git global flags that consume the NEXT token as a value.
    82→const GIT_VALUE_FLAGS: &[&str] = &[
    83→    "-C",
    84→    "-c",

[assistant] Issue: `SCRIPT_INTERPRETERS` and `SCRIPT_INLINE_FLAGS` are parallel arrays — a classic bug magnet. If someone adds an interpreter to one but not the other, silent mismatch. This should be a struct array:

[tool] Edit({"replace_all":false,"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs","old_string":"/// Non-shell interpreters whose code argument should be scanned for\n/// dangerous patterns (workspace paths, git commands).\nconst SCRIPT_INTERPRETERS: &[&str] = &[\"python\", \"python3\", \"node\", \"ruby\", \"perl\"];\n\n/// The flag that introduces an inline code argument for each interpreter.\nconst SCRIPT_INLINE_FLAGS: &[&str] = &[\"-c\", \"-c\", \"-e\", \"-e\", \"-e\"];\n\n/// True if a token is a shell interpreter (handles full paths like /bin/bash).\nfn is_shell_command(token: &str) -> bool {\n    SHELL_COMMANDS.contains(&token)\n        || SHELL_COMMANDS\n            .iter()\n            .any(|s| token.ends_with(&format!(\"/{}\", s)))\n}\n\n/// True if a token is a non-shell script interpreter. Returns the flag\n/// that introduces inline code (e.g., \"-c\" for python, \"-e\" for node).\nfn script_interpreter_flag(token: &str) -> Option<&'static str> {\n    let base = token.rsplit('/').next().unwrap_or(token);\n    SCRIPT_INTERPRETERS\n        .iter()\n        .zip(SCRIPT_INLINE_FLAGS.iter())\n        .find(|(name, _)| *name == &base)\n        .map(|(_, flag)| *flag)\n}","new_string":"/// Non-shell interpreters and the flag that introduces inline code.\n/// (name, inline_flag) — e.g., python uses -c, node uses -e.\nconst SCRIPT_INTERPRETERS: &[(&str, &str)] = &[\n    (\"python\", \"-c\"),\n    (\"python3\", \"-c\"),\n    (\"node\", \"-e\"),\n    (\"ruby\", \"-e\"),\n    (\"perl\", \"-e\"),\n];\n\n/// True if a token is a shell interpreter (handles full paths like /bin/bash).\nfn is_shell_command(token: &str) -> bool {\n    SHELL_COMMANDS.contains(&token)\n        || SHELL_COMMANDS\n            .iter()\n            .any(|s| token.ends_with(&format!(\"/{}\", s)))\n}\n\n/// True if a token is a non-shell script interpreter. Returns the flag\n/// that introduces inline code (e.g., \"-c\" for python, \"-e\" for node).\nfn script_interpreter_flag(token: &str) -> Option<&'static str> {\n    let base = token.rsplit('/').next().unwrap_or(token);\n    SCRIPT_INTERPRETERS\n        .iter()\n        .find(|(name, _)| *name == base)\n        .map(|(_, flag)| *flag)\n}"})

[result]     85→    "-C",

    90→    "--super-prefix",
    91→];
    92→
    93→fn tokenize_inner(cmd: &str, depth: usize) -> (Vec<Token>, Vec<Vec<Segment>>) {
    94→    let chars: Vec<char> = cmd.chars().collect();
    95→    let len = chars.len();
    96→    let mut tokens: Vec<Token> = Vec::new();
    97→    let mut inner_segment_groups: Vec<Vec<Segment>> = Vec::new();
    98→    let mut word = String::new();
    99→    let mut in_word = false;
   100→    let mut i = 0;
   101→
   102→    // Track last emitted token type for newline continuation
   103→    // None = start, Some(op) = last emitted operator string
   104→    let mut last_emitted_op: Option<String> = None;
   105→
   106→    // Heredoc state: delimiter is captured, then we wait for \n to enter body
   107→    let mut heredoc_delimiter: Option<String> = None;
   108→    let mut heredoc_in_body = false;
   109→    let mut heredoc_strip_tabs = false; // true for <<- (indented heredoc)
   110→
   111→    while i < len {
   112→        // Heredoc body: skip until delimiter line
   113→        if heredoc_in_body {
   114→            let delim = heredoc_delimiter.as_ref().unwrap();
   115→            // Find end of current line
   116→            let line_start = i;
   117→            while i < len && chars[i] != '\n' {
   118→                i += 1;
   119→            }
   120→            let line: String = chars[line_start..i].iter().collect();
   121→            // <<- strips leading tabs; << requires exact match
   122→            let check_line = if heredoc_strip_tabs {
   123→                line.trim_start_matches('\t')
   124→            } else {
   125→                &line
   126→            };
   127→            if check_line == delim.as_str() {
   128→                heredoc_delimiter = None;
   129→                heredoc_in_body = false;
   130→                heredoc_strip_tabs = false;
   131→            }
   132→            if i < len {
   133→                i += 1; // skip the \n
   134→            }
   135→            continue;
   136→        }
   137→
   138→        let ch = chars[i];
   139→
   140→        // Skip \r (treat as whitespace, \r\n becomes just \n)
   141→        if ch == '\r' {
   142→            if in_word {
   143→                tokens.push(Token::Word(std::mem::take(&mut word)));
   144→                in_word = false;
   145→                last_emitted_op = None;
   146→            }
   147→            i += 1;
   148→            continue;
   149→        }
   150→
   151→        // Whitespace: space, tab
   152→        if ch == ' ' || ch == '\t' {
   153→            if in_word {

Actually, looking at this more carefully — the pattern is mechanical but readable as-is. A macro would save lines but reduce clarity. The boilerplate is:
```rust
let op = "X".to_string();
last_emitted_op = Some(op.clone());
tokens.push(Token::Operator(op));
```

An Edit/Write with `file_path: "/workspace/.claude/worktrees/wt/../../CLAUDE.md"` would pass the `is_protected_path` check on line 1034 (blocks it), but only because `is_protected_path` normalizes. Good — the CWD enforcement check at line 1043 runs AFTER the protection check, so a `..` escape is already caught. No bug.

But wait — line 1043: if `file_path` is `/workspace/.claude/worktrees/wt/../../something`, `is_protected_path` catches it first. But if it's `/workspace/.claude/worktrees/wt/file` (a legit worktree path), the CWD check at 1043 fires. That check uses raw `starts_with` which is fine for this case since Edit/Write tool paths are absolute and tool-generated.

[assistant] The `unwrap()` at line 114 is safe (guarded by `heredoc_in_body` which is only true when `heredoc_delimiter.is_some()`). The test unwraps are fine. No TODOs. No dead code issues.

But the user's message is right — let me think bigger. What repeatable, composable blocks are still not extracted?

Looking at `check_bash` with fresh eyes, every check follows one of these patterns:

**Pattern A: Git subcommand + conditions → block**
```
push + targets_main → block
checkout/switch + !flags + !worktree_cwd → block
reset + --hard + workspace_cwd → block
reset + --hard + !origin/ → block
clean + -f → block
branch + -D → block
worktree + remove + (CWD/agent/other) → tiered block
```

**Pattern B: Command + protected path → block**
```
sed -i + protected_path → block
tee + protected_path → block
```

**Pattern C: Operator + protected path → block**
```
write_redirect + protected_path → block
```

**Pattern D: Interpreter + workspace in code → block**
```
python/node/perl/ruby + -c/-e + code_contains(/workspace/) → block
```

Patterns B and C are both "writes to protected path" — they're the same invariant expressed differently because the write mechanism differs (command argument vs redirect operator). Pattern D is a variant — "code that references protected path."

The deeper composable block is: **`targets_protected_path(seg) -> bool`** — does this segment write to a protected path through ANY mechanism (redirect, sed, tee, or other)?

But this would mix concerns — `sed -i` needs the `-i` flag check, `tee` doesn't. The redirect check operates on operators, not commands. Combining them would be forced.

What IS worth extracting: the `for seg in &segments { ... }` iteration is genuinely repeated 10 times. Each check could be a function `fn check_X(seg: &Segment) -> Option<String>`, and `check_bash` becomes:

```rust
for seg in &segments {
    if let Some(reason) = check_push_to_main(seg)
        .or_else(|| check_checkout_switch(seg))
        .or_else(|| check_reset_hard(seg))
        // ...
    {
        return Some(reason);
    }
}
```

But wait — some checks need CWD context (checkout-switch, reset-hard-workspace), and the redirect check iterates tokens within each segment. The checks aren't all the same shape. Forcing them into a uniform interface would require passing CWD state, which complicates the signatures.

The honest assessment: the current structure is **already clean enough**. Each check is 5-10 lines, clearly named in a comment, easy to scan. Extracting each into a function would add 10 function signatures but not improve readability — it would just move code around.

What I CAN do: make the structure more scannable by aligning the check comments:
