    60→// ============================================================
    61→// Tokenizer: single-pass POSIX-aligned shell command tokenizer
    62→// ============================================================
    63→
    64→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)]
    65→pub enum Token {
    66→    Word(String),
    67→    Operator(String),
    68→}
    69→
    70→/// Tokenize a shell command string into Words and Operators.
    71→/// Handles quotes, backslash escaping, operators, heredocs,
    72→/// command substitution ($() and backticks), and comments.
    73→///
    74→/// On any parse error (unmatched quotes, etc.), returns a fail-open
    75→/// fallback: the entire command whitespace-split into Word tokens.
    76→pub fn tokenize(cmd: &str) -> (Vec<Token>, Vec<Vec<Segment>>) {
    77→    tokenize_inner(cmd, 0)
    78→}
    79→
    80→/// Max recursion depth for command substitution parsing.
    81→const MAX_SUBST_DEPTH: usize = 3;
    82→
    83→/// Known command prefixes that wrap another command.
    84→/// `exec` replaces the process with the command — semantically a prefix.
    85→const COMMAND_PREFIXES: &[&str] = &["sudo", "env", "command", "nice", "nohup", "time", "exec"];
    86→
    87→/// Shell interpreters whose -c argument should be recursively parsed.
    88→const SHELL_COMMANDS: &[&str] = &["bash", "sh", "dash", "zsh", "ksh"];
    89→
    90→/// Non-shell interpreters and the flag that introduces inline code.
    91→/// (name, inline_flag) — e.g., python uses -c, node uses -e.
    92→const SCRIPT_INTERPRETERS: &[(&str, &str)] = &[
    93→    ("python", "-c"),
    94→    ("python3", "-c"),
    95→    ("node", "-e"),
    96→    ("ruby", "-e"),
    97→    ("perl", "-e"),
    98→];
    99→
   100→/// True if a token is a shell interpreter (handles full paths like /bin/bash).
   101→fn is_shell_command(token: &str) -> bool {
   102→    SHELL_COMMANDS.contains(&token)
   103→        || SHELL_COMMANDS
   104→            .iter()
   105→            .any(|s| token.ends_with(&format!("/{}", s)))
   106→}
   107→
   108→/// True if a token is a non-shell script interpreter. Returns the flag
   109→/// that introduces inline code (e.g., "-c" for python, "-e" for node).
   110→fn script_interpreter_flag(token: &str) -> Option<&'static str> {
   111→    let base = token.rsplit('/').next().unwrap_or(token);
   112→    SCRIPT_INTERPRETERS
   113→        .iter()
   114→        .find(|(name, _)| *name == base)
   115→        .map(|(_, flag)| *flag)
   116→}
   117→
   118→/// Substrings that indicate write or exec operations in interpreter code.
   119→/// Organized by category. If any pattern appears in the code string alongside
   120→/// a /workspace/ path, the command is blocked. Read-only operations (print,
   121→/// listdir, open for read) don't match any of these.
   122→const WRITE_INDICATORS: &[&str] = &[
   123→    // Shell-out patterns (any language)
   124→    "system(",
   125→    "exec(",
   126→    "popen(",
   127→    "subprocess",
   128→    "child_process",
   129→    "execSync",
   130→    "spawnSync",
   131→    // File mode patterns (Python open() mode argument)
   132→    "\"w\"",
   133→    "'w'",
   134→    "\"w+\"",
   135→    "'w+'",
   136→    "\"a\"",
   137→    "'a'",
   138→    "\"a+\"",
   139→    "'a+'",
   140→    // Python write/delete
   141→    "shutil.",
   142→    "rmtree",
   143→    "os.remove(",
   144→    "os.rename(",
   145→    // Node.js write/delete
   146→    "writeFile",
   147→    "appendFile",
   148→    "createWriteStream",
   149→    "mkdirSync",
   150→    "rmdirSync",
   151→    "unlinkSync",
   152→    "renameSync",
   153→    // Perl/Ruby/cross-language write/delete
   154→    "unlink(",
   155→    "rename(",
   156→    "File.write",
   157→    "File.delete",
   158→    "FileUtils",
   159→];
   160→
   161→/// True if a code string contains any write or exec indicator.
   162→fn has_write_indicator(code: &str) -> bool {
   163→    WRITE_INDICATORS
   164→        .iter()
   165→        .any(|pattern| code.contains(pattern))
   166→}
   167→
   168→/// Git global flags that consume the NEXT token as a value.
   169→const GIT_VALUE_FLAGS: &[&str] = &[
   170→    "-C",
   171→    "-c",
   172→    "--git-dir",
   173→    "--work-tree",
   174→    "--namespace",
   175→    "--super-prefix",
   176→];
   177→
   178→// ============================================================
   179→// Tokenizer struct: encapsulates all mutable state for tokenize_inner
   180→// ============================================================
   181→
   182→struct Tokenizer<'a> {
   183→    chars: &'a [char],
   184→    len: usize,
   185→    i: usize,
   186→    tokens: Vec<Token>,
   187→    word: String,
   188→    in_word: bool,
   189→    inner_segment_groups: Vec<Vec<Segment>>,
   190→    last_emitted_op: Option<&'static str>,
   191→    heredoc_delimiter: Option<String>,
   192→    heredoc_in_body: bool,
   193→    heredoc_strip_tabs: bool,
   194→    depth: usize,
   195→    cmd: &'a str, // for fail_open fallback
   196→}
   197→
   198→impl<'a> Tokenizer<'a> {
   199→    fn new(chars: &'a [char], cmd: &'a str, depth: usize) -> Self {
   200→        Tokenizer {
   201→            chars,
   202→            len: chars.len(),
   203→            i: 0,
   204→            tokens: Vec::new(),
   205→            word: String::new(),
   206→            in_word: false,
   207→            inner_segment_groups: Vec::new(),
   208→            last_emitted_op: None,
   209→            heredoc_delimiter: None,
   210→            heredoc_in_body: false,
   211→            heredoc_strip_tabs: false,
   212→            depth,
   213→            cmd,
   214→        }
   215→    }
   216→
   217→    /// Flush the current word if `in_word` is set.
   218→    fn emit_word(&mut self) {
   219→        if self.in_word {
   220→            self.tokens
   221→                .push(Token::Word(std::mem::take(&mut self.word)));
   222→            self.in_word = false;
   223→        }
   224→    }
   225→
   226→    /// Flush any pending word, then emit an operator token.
   227→    fn emit_op(&mut self, op: &'static str) {
   228→        self.emit_word();
   229→        self.last_emitted_op = Some(op);
   230→        self.tokens.push(Token::Operator(op.to_string()));
   231→    }
   232→
   233→    /// Handle `$()` command substitution at `self.i` (which points to `$`).
   234→    /// Always sets `self.in_word = true` (idempotent when already inside quotes).
   235→    /// Returns `false` if the substitution is unmatched (caller should fail-open).
   236→    fn parse_dollar_subst(&mut self) -> bool {
   237→        let start = self.i;
   238→        self.i += 2; // skip '$' and '('
   239→        match find_matching_close_paren(self.chars, self.i) {
   240→            Some((inner, new_i)) => {
   241→                self.i = new_i;
   242→                let subst_text: String = self.chars[start..self.i].iter().collect();
   243→                self.in_word = true;
   244→                self.word.push_str(&subst_text);
   245→                if self.depth < MAX_SUBST_DEPTH {
   246→                    let inner_segs = parse_segments_inner(&inner, self.depth + 1);
   247→                    if !inner_segs.is_empty() {
   248→                        self.inner_segment_groups.push(inner_segs);
   249→                    }
   250→                }
   251→                true
   252→            }
   253→            None => false,
   254→        }
   255→    }
   256→
   257→    /// Handle a backtick command substitution at `self.i` (which points to `` ` ``).
   258→    /// Always sets `self.in_word = true` (idempotent when already inside quotes).
   259→    /// Returns `false` if the closing backtick is missing (caller should fail-open).
   260→    fn parse_backtick(&mut self) -> bool {
   261→        let start = self.i;
   262→        self.i += 1; // skip opening `
   263→        let mut inner = String::new();
   264→        while self.i < self.len && self.chars[self.i] != '`' {
   265→            inner.push(self.chars[self.i]);
   266→            self.i += 1;
   267→        }
   268→        if self.i >= self.len {
   269→            return false;
   270→        }
   271→        self.i += 1; // skip closing `
   272→        let bt_text: String = self.chars[start..self.i].iter().collect();
   273→        self.in_word = true;
   274→        self.word.push_str(&bt_text);
   275→        if self.depth < MAX_SUBST_DEPTH {
   276→            let inner_segs = parse_segments_inner(&inner, self.depth + 1);
   277→            if !inner_segs.is_empty() {
   278→                self.inner_segment_groups.push(inner_segs);
   279→            }
   280→        }
   281→        true
   282→    }
   283→
   284→    /// Handle `<<` heredoc at `self.i` (which points to the first `<`).
   285→    /// Consumes `<<`, optional `-`, whitespace, and the delimiter.
   286→    fn parse_heredoc(&mut self) {
   287→        self.emit_word();
   288→        self.i += 2; // skip '<<'
   289→                     // Check for <<- (indented heredoc: strips leading tabs)
   290→        if self.i < self.len && self.chars[self.i] == '-' {
   291→            self.heredoc_strip_tabs = true;
   292→            self.i += 1;
   293→        }
   294→        // Skip whitespace before delimiter
   295→        while self.i < self.len && (self.chars[self.i] == ' ' || self.chars[self.i] == '\t') {
   296→            self.i += 1;
   297→        }
   298→        // Capture delimiter
   299→        if self.i < self.len {
   300→            let mut delim = String::new();
   301→            if self.chars[self.i] == '\'' || self.chars[self.i] == '"' {
   302→                let quote = self.chars[self.i];
   303→                self.i += 1;
   304→                while self.i < self.len && self.chars[self.i] != quote {
   305→                    delim.push(self.chars[self.i]);
   306→                    self.i += 1;
   307→                }
   308→                if self.i < self.len {
   309→                    self.i += 1; // skip closing quote
   310→                }
   311→            } else {
   312→                while self.i < self.len
   313→                    && self.chars[self.i] != ' '
   314→                    && self.chars[self.i] != '\t'
   315→                    && self.chars[self.i] != '\n'
   316→                    && self.chars[self.i] != ';'
   317→                    && self.chars[self.i] != '&'
   318→                    && self.chars[self.i] != '|'
   319→                {
   320→                    delim.push(self.chars[self.i]);
   321→                    self.i += 1;
   322→                }
   323→            }
   324→            if !delim.is_empty() {
   325→                // Set delimiter; the \n handler will enter body mode.
   326→                // Tokens on the rest of this line (e.g., `<< EOF && echo done`)
   327→                // are processed normally by the main loop.
   328→                self.heredoc_delimiter = Some(delim);
   329→            }
   330→        }
   331→    }
   332→
   333→    /// Try to match a simple (table-driven) operator at `self.i`.
   334→    /// Returns `true` and advances `self.i` if matched.
   335→    ///
   336→    /// Operator matching: greedy longest-first, table-driven.
   337→    /// Ordered longest-first so ">>" matches before ">", "&&" before "&", etc.
   338→    /// This is the same algorithm bash uses (shellmeta + peek-ahead).
   339→    ///
   340→    /// Includes `<<` redirect variants (`<>`, `<&`, `<`) AFTER heredoc has been
   341→    /// checked (heredoc check runs before this method is called, so `<<` is never
   342→    /// in the remaining input when we reach here for a `<` character).
   343→    fn match_simple_op(&mut self) -> bool {
   344→        const SIMPLE_OPS: &[&str] = &[
   345→            "&>>", "&&", "&>", ">>", ">&", ">|", "||", ">", "&", "|", ";", "(", ")", "<>", "<&",
   346→            "<",
   347→        ];
   348→        if let Some(&op) = SIMPLE_OPS.iter().find(|op| {
   349→            let ob = op.as_bytes();
   350→            self.i + ob.len() <= self.len
   351→                && ob
   352→                    .iter()
   353→                    .enumerate()
   354→                    .all(|(j, &b)| self.chars[self.i + j] == b as char)
   355→        }) {
   356→            self.emit_op(op);
   357→            self.i += op.len();
   358→            true
   359→        } else {
   360→            false
   361→        }
   362→    }
   363→
   364→    /// Main tokenizer loop. Returns `(tokens, inner_segment_groups)` on success,
   365→    /// or the fail-open result if an unmatched delimiter is encountered.
   366→    fn run(mut self) -> (Vec<Token>, Vec<Vec<Segment>>) {
   367→        while self.i < self.len {
   368→            // Heredoc body: skip until delimiter line
   369→            if self.heredoc_in_body {
   370→                let delim = self.heredoc_delimiter.as_ref().unwrap().clone();
   371→                let line_start = self.i;
   372→                while self.i < self.len && self.chars[self.i] != '\n' {
   373→                    self.i += 1;
   374→                }
   375→                let line: String = self.chars[line_start..self.i].iter().collect();
   376→                // <<- strips leading tabs; << requires exact match
   377→                let matches = if self.heredoc_strip_tabs {
   378→                    line.trim_start_matches('\t') == delim.as_str()
   379→                } else {
   380→                    line.as_str() == delim.as_str()
   381→                };
   382→                if matches {
   383→                    self.heredoc_delimiter = None;
   384→                    self.heredoc_in_body = false;
   385→                    self.heredoc_strip_tabs = false;
   386→                }
   387→                if self.i < self.len {
   388→                    self.i += 1; // skip the \n
   389→                }
   390→                continue;
   391→            }
   392→
   393→            let ch = self.chars[self.i];
   394→
   395→            // Skip \r (treat as whitespace, \r\n becomes just \n)
   396→            if ch == '\r' {
   397→                self.emit_word();
   398→                self.last_emitted_op = None;
   399→                self.i += 1;
   400→                continue;
   401→            }
   402→
   403→            // Whitespace: space, tab
   404→            if ch == ' ' || ch == '\t' {
   405→                self.emit_word();
   406→                self.last_emitted_op = None;
   407→                self.i += 1;
   408→                continue;
   409→            }
   410→
   411→            // Comment: # at start of token (not mid-word)
   412→            if ch == '#' && !self.in_word {
   413→                // Skip rest of line
   414→                while self.i < self.len && self.chars[self.i] != '\n' {
   415→                    self.i += 1;
   416→                }
   417→                // Don't consume the \n — let it be processed as an operator
   418→                continue;
   419→            }
   420→
   421→            // Newline: command separator, continuation, or heredoc body start
   422→            if ch == '\n' {
   423→                // Only reset last_emitted_op if we flush a word (mirrors original logic:
   424→                // the continuation check must see the operator that preceded the newline).
   425→                if self.in_word {
   426→                    self.emit_word();
   427→                    self.last_emitted_op = None;
   428→                }
   429→                // If heredoc delimiter is set but body hasn't started, enter body mode
   430→                if self.heredoc_delimiter.is_some() && !self.heredoc_in_body {
   431→                    self.heredoc_in_body = true;
   432→                    self.i += 1;
   433→                    continue;
   434→                }
   435→                // After |, &&, || → continuation (skip newline)
   436→                let is_continuation = matches!(self.last_emitted_op, Some("|" | "&&" | "||"));
   437→                if !is_continuation {
   438→                    self.last_emitted_op = Some("\n");
   439→                    self.tokens.push(Token::Operator("\n".to_string()));
   440→                }
   441→                self.i += 1;
   442→                continue;
   443→            }
   444→
   445→            // Single quote
   446→            if ch == '\'' {
   447→                self.in_word = true;
   448→                self.i += 1;
   449→                while self.i < self.len && self.chars[self.i] != '\'' {
   450→                    self.word.push(self.chars[self.i]);
   451→                    self.i += 1;
   452→                }
   453→                if self.i >= self.len {
   454→                    // Unmatched single quote — fail-open
   455→                    return fail_open(self.cmd);
   456→                }
   457→                self.i += 1; // skip closing '
   458→                continue;
   459→            }
   460→
   461→            // Double quote
   462→            if ch == '"' {
   463→                self.in_word = true;
   464→                self.i += 1;
   465→                while self.i < self.len && self.chars[self.i] != '"' {
   466→                    if self.chars[self.i] == '\\' && self.i + 1 < self.len {
   467→                        let next = self.chars[self.i + 1];
   468→                        match next {
   469→                            '"' | '\\' | '$' | '`' => {
   470→                                self.word.push(next);
   471→                                self.i += 2;
   472→                            }
   473→                            '\n' => {
   474→                                // Line continuation inside double quotes
   475→                                self.i += 2;
   476→                            }
   477→                            _ => {
   478→                                self.word.push('\\');
   479→                                self.word.push(next);
   480→                                self.i += 2;
   481→                            }
   482→                        }
   483→                        continue;
   484→                    }
   485→                    // $() inside double quotes — extract for recursive parsing
   486→                    if self.chars[self.i] == '$'
   487→                        && self.i + 1 < self.len
   488→                        && self.chars[self.i + 1] == '('
   489→                    {
   490→                        if !self.parse_dollar_subst() {
   491→                            return fail_open(self.cmd);
   492→                        }
   493→                        continue;
   494→                    }
   495→                    // Backtick inside double quotes
   496→                    if self.chars[self.i] == '`' {
   497→                        if !self.parse_backtick() {
   498→                            return fail_open(self.cmd);
   499→                        }
   500→                        continue;
   501→                    }
   502→                    self.word.push(self.chars[self.i]);
   503→                    self.i += 1;
   504→                }
   505→                if self.i >= self.len {
   506→                    return fail_open(self.cmd);
   507→                }
   508→                self.i += 1; // skip closing "
   509→                continue;
   510→            }
   511→
   512→            // Backslash outside quotes
   513→            if ch == '\\' {
   514→                if self.i + 1 >= self.len {
   515→                    // Dangling backslash — fail-open
   516→                    return fail_open(self.cmd);
   517→                }
   518→                let next = self.chars[self.i + 1];
   519→                if next == '\n' {
   520→                    // Line continuation
   521→                    self.i += 2;
   522→                    continue;
   523→                }
   524→                self.in_word = true;
   525→                self.word.push(next);
   526→                self.i += 2;
   527→                continue;
   528→            }
   529→
   530→            // $() command substitution outside quotes
   531→            if ch == '$' && self.i + 1 < self.len && self.chars[self.i + 1] == '(' {
   532→                if !self.parse_dollar_subst() {
   533→                    return fail_open(self.cmd);
   534→                }
   535→                continue;
   536→            }
   537→
   538→            // Backtick command substitution outside quotes
   539→            if ch == '`' {
   540→                if !self.parse_backtick() {
   541→                    return fail_open(self.cmd);
   542→                }
   543→                continue;
   544→            }
   545→
   546→            // Heredoc: << must be checked BEFORE the table-driven operator match
   547→            // so that `<<` is consumed here and `<`, `<&`, `<>` can live in SIMPLE_OPS.
   548→            if ch == '<' && self.i + 1 < self.len && self.chars[self.i + 1] == '<' {
   549→                self.parse_heredoc();
   550→                continue;
   551→            }
   552→
   553→            // Operator matching: greedy longest-first, table-driven.
   554→            // Includes <>, <&, < (safe now that << was caught above).
   555→            if self.match_simple_op() {
   556→                continue;
   557→            }
   558→
   559→            // Default: regular character, accumulate into word
   560→            self.in_word = true;
   561→            self.word.push(ch);
   562→            self.i += 1;
   563→        }
   564→
   565→        // Emit final word if any
   566→        self.emit_word();
   567→
   568→        (self.tokens, self.inner_segment_groups)
   569→    }
   570→}
   571→
   572→fn tokenize_inner(cmd: &str, depth: usize) -> (Vec<Token>, Vec<Vec<Segment>>) {
   573→    let chars: Vec<char> = cmd.chars().collect();
   574→    let tok = Tokenizer::new(&chars, cmd, depth);
   575→    tok.run()
   576→}
   577→
   578→/// Fail-open: return entire command as whitespace-split Word tokens, no inner segments.
   579→fn fail_open(cmd: &str) -> (Vec<Token>, Vec<Vec<Segment>>) {
   580→    let tokens: Vec<Token> = cmd
   581→        .split_whitespace()
   582→        .map(|w| Token::Word(w.to_string()))
   583→        .collect();
   584→    (tokens, Vec::new())
   585→}
   586→
   587→/// Find the matching `)` for a `$(` command substitution, tracking quote state
   588→/// so that `)` inside quotes doesn't prematurely close the substitution.
   589→/// Returns (inner_content, new_position) or None if unmatched.
   590→fn find_matching_close_paren(chars: &[char], start: usize) -> Option<(String, usize)> {
   591→    let len = chars.len();
   592→    let mut i = start;
   593→    let mut paren_depth = 1;
   594→    let mut inner = String::new();
   595→    let mut in_sq = false;
   596→    let mut in_dq = false;
   597→
   598→    while i < len && paren_depth > 0 {
   599→        let c = chars[i];
   600→        if in_sq {

[tool] Read({"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs","offset":600,"limit":400})

[result]    600→        if in_sq {
   601→            if c == '\'' {
   602→                in_sq = false;
   603→            }
   604→            inner.push(c);
   605→            i += 1;
   606→            continue;
   607→        }
   608→        if in_dq {
   609→            if c == '"' {
   610→                in_dq = false;
   611→            } else if c == '\\' && i + 1 < len {
   612→                inner.push(c);
   613→                inner.push(chars[i + 1]);
   614→                i += 2;
   615→                continue;
   616→            }
   617→            inner.push(c);
   618→            i += 1;
   619→            continue;
   620→        }
   621→        match c {
   622→            '\'' => {
   623→                in_sq = true;
   624→                inner.push(c);
   625→            }
   626→            '"' => {
   627→                in_dq = true;
   628→                inner.push(c);
   629→            }
   630→            '(' => {
   631→                paren_depth += 1;
   632→                inner.push(c);
   633→            }
   634→            ')' => {
   635→                paren_depth -= 1;
   636→                if paren_depth > 0 {
   637→                    inner.push(c);
   638→                }
   639→            }
   640→            '\\' if i + 1 < len => {
   641→                inner.push(c);
   642→                inner.push(chars[i + 1]);
   643→                i += 2;
   644→                continue;
   645→            }
   646→            _ => {
   647→                inner.push(c);
   648→            }
   649→        }
   650→        i += 1;
   651→    }
   652→
   653→    if paren_depth > 0 {
   654→        None
   655→    } else {
   656→        Some((inner, i))
   657→    }
   658→}
   659→
   660→// ============================================================
   661→// Segments: split tokens on operators, check each independently
   662→// ============================================================
   663→
   664→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
   665→pub struct Segment {
   666→    pub tokens: Vec<Token>,
   667→}
   668→
   669→/// True if a token is the `git` command (handles full paths like /usr/bin/git).
   670→fn is_git_command(token: &str) -> bool {
   671→    token == "git" || token.ends_with("/git")
   672→}
   673→
   674→/// True if a token looks like a variable assignment (VAR=val).
   675→fn is_var_assignment(token: &str) -> bool {
   676→    if let Some(eq_pos) = token.find('=') {
   677→        if eq_pos == 0 {
   678→            return false; // "=value" is not an assignment
   679→        }
   680→        let name = &token[..eq_pos];
   681→        let mut chars = name.chars();
   682→        match chars.next() {
   683→            Some(c) if c.is_ascii_alphabetic() || c == '_' => {}
   684→            _ => return false,
   685→        }
   686→        chars.all(|c| c.is_ascii_alphanumeric() || c == '_')
   687→    } else {
   688→        false
   689→    }
   690→}
   691→
   692→/// True if an operator separates commands (splits segments).
   693→/// Redirect operators (>, >>, <, etc.) are NOT control operators —
   694→/// they stay inside the segment with the command they modify.
   695→fn is_control_operator(op: &str) -> bool {
   696→    matches!(op, "&&" | "||" | "|" | ";" | "&" | "\n" | "(" | ")")
   697→}
   698→
   699→/// True if an operator can write to a file.
   700→/// Used by the redirect check to block writes to protected paths.
   701→fn is_write_redirect(op: &str) -> bool {
   702→    matches!(op, ">" | ">>" | ">|" | ">&" | "<>" | "&>" | "&>>")
   703→}
   704→
   705→impl Segment {
   706→    /// Find the effective command, skipping variable assignments, known prefixes,
   707→    /// and redirect operators.
   708→    pub fn effective_command(&self) -> Option<(usize, &str)> {
   709→        let mut i = 0;
   710→        while i < self.tokens.len() {
   711→            // Skip redirect operators — they're not commands
   712→            let tok = match &self.tokens[i] {
   713→                Token::Word(w) => w.as_str(),
   714→                Token::Operator(_) => {
   715→                    i += 1;
   716→                    continue;
   717→                }
   718→            };
   719→
   720→            // Skip variable assignments
   721→            if is_var_assignment(tok) {
   722→                i += 1;
   723→                continue;
   724→            }
   725→
   726→            // Skip brace group opener. In POSIX, { is a reserved word that
   727→            // groups commands but doesn't change what command executes.
   728→            // { git push; } → the effective command is git, not {.
   729→            if tok == "{" || tok == "}" {
   730→                i += 1;
   731→                continue;
   732→            }
   733→
   734→            // Skip known command prefixes and their flag-like arguments.
   735→            // After a prefix, skip tokens starting with - (flags) and assignments (=).
   736→            // The first non-flag, non-assignment token is the command.
   737→            //
   738→            // Known gap: prefix flags that take values (e.g., sudo -u root) cause the
   739→            // value ("root") to be returned as the command. This fails-open: "root" won't
   740→            // match any dangerous command pattern, so the guard allows the command.
   741→            // The alternative (skipping flag+value) is worse: sudo -n git push would skip
   742→            // -n AND git, returning "push" as the command — a more dangerous bypass.
   743→            if COMMAND_PREFIXES.contains(&tok) {
   744→                i += 1;
   745→                while i < self.tokens.len() {
   746→                    match &self.tokens[i] {
   747→                        Token::Operator(_) => {
   748→                            i += 1;
   749→                            continue;
   750→                        }
   751→                        Token::Word(t) => {
   752→                            if t.starts_with('-') || is_var_assignment(t) {
   753→                                i += 1;
   754→                            } else {
   755→                                break;
   756→                            }
   757→                        }
   758→                    }
   759→                }
   760→                continue;
   761→            }
   762→
   763→            return Some((i, tok));
   764→        }
   765→        None
   766→    }
   767→
   768→    /// Find the git subcommand, skipping global git flags and redirect operators.
   769→    pub fn git_subcmd(&self) -> Option<(usize, &str)> {
   770→        let (cmd_pos, cmd) = self.effective_command()?;
   771→        if !is_git_command(cmd) {
   772→            return None;
   773→        }
   774→
   775→        let mut i = cmd_pos + 1;
   776→        while i < self.tokens.len() {
   777→            let tok = match &self.tokens[i] {
   778→                Token::Word(w) => w.as_str(),
   779→                Token::Operator(_) => {
   780→                    i += 1;
   781→                    continue;
   782→                }
   783→            };
   784→            // --flag=value form
   785→            if tok.contains('=')
   786→                && GIT_VALUE_FLAGS
   787→                    .iter()
   788→                    .any(|f| tok.starts_with(&format!("{}=", f)))
   789→            {
   790→                i += 1;
   791→                continue;
   792→            }
   793→            // --flag value form (two tokens)
   794→            if GIT_VALUE_FLAGS.contains(&tok) {
   795→                i += 2; // skip flag + value
   796→                continue;
   797→            }
   798→            // Standalone flag (--no-pager, --bare, etc.)
   799→            if tok.starts_with('-') {
   800→                i += 1;
   801→                continue;
   802→            }
   803→            return Some((i, tok));
   804→        }
   805→        None
   806→    }
   807→
   808→    /// True if this segment runs git with the given subcommand.
   809→    pub fn is_git(&self, subcmd: &str) -> bool {
   810→        self.git_subcmd().map(|(_, s)| s == subcmd).unwrap_or(false)
   811→    }
   812→
   813→    /// True if any Word token AFTER the subcommand (for git) or command exactly equals `val`.
   814→    pub fn has_arg(&self, val: &str) -> bool {
   815→        let start = if let Some((pos, _)) = self.git_subcmd() {
   816→            pos + 1
   817→        } else if let Some((pos, _)) = self.effective_command() {
   818→            pos + 1
   819→        } else {
   820→            return false;
   821→        };
   822→        self.tokens[start..]
   823→            .iter()
   824→            .any(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w == val))
   825→    }
   826→
   827→    /// True if any Word token AFTER the subcommand/command is a short flag containing `ch`.
   828→    pub fn has_short_flag(&self, ch: char) -> bool {
   829→        let start = if let Some((pos, _)) = self.git_subcmd() {
   830→            pos + 1
   831→        } else if let Some((pos, _)) = self.effective_command() {
   832→            pos + 1
   833→        } else {
   834→            return false;
   835→        };
   836→        self.tokens[start..].iter().any(|t| {
   837→            matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w.starts_with('-') && !w.starts_with("--") && w.contains(ch))
   838→        })
   839→    }
   840→
   841→    /// True if any Word token starts with `prefix`.
   842→    pub fn has_token_starting_with(&self, prefix: &str) -> bool {
   843→        self.tokens
   844→            .iter()
   845→            .any(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w.starts_with(prefix)))
   846→    }
   847→
   848→    /// True if any Word token contains `substring`.
   849→    pub fn has_token_containing(&self, substring: &str) -> bool {
   850→        self.tokens
   851→            .iter()
   852→            .any(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w.contains(substring)))
   853→    }
   854→
   855→    /// True if any Word token resolves to a worktree ROOT directory path.
   856→    /// Uses resolve_path() to normalize ../ and relative paths.
   857→    pub fn targets_worktree_root(&self) -> bool {
   858→        let prefix = "/workspace/.claude/worktrees/";
   859→        self.tokens.iter().any(|t| {
   860→            if let Token::Word(w) = t {
   861→                let resolved = resolve_path(w);
   862→                let s = resolved
   863→                    .as_ref()
   864→                    .map(|p| p.to_string_lossy().into_owned())
   865→                    .unwrap_or_default();
   866→                if let Some(rest) = s.strip_prefix(prefix) {
   867→                    let trimmed = rest.trim_end_matches('/');
   868→                    !trimmed.is_empty() && !trimmed.contains('/')
   869→                } else {
   870→                    false
   871→                }
   872→            } else {
   873→                false
   874→            }
   875→        })
   876→    }
   877→
   878→    /// True if any Word token resolves to a protected workspace path.
   879→    /// Handles both absolute and relative paths via is_protected_path().
   880→    pub fn has_protected_path(&self) -> bool {
   881→        self.tokens
   882→            .iter()
   883→            .any(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if is_protected_path(w)))
   884→    }
   885→}
   886→
   887→/// Parse a command string into segments. Each segment is a separate command
   888→/// (split on operators like &&, ||, |, ;, &, newline, (, )).
   889→/// Also returns segments from command substitutions ($() and backticks).
   890→pub fn parse_segments(cmd: &str) -> Vec<Segment> {
   891→    parse_segments_inner(cmd, 0)
   892→}
   893→
   894→fn parse_segments_inner(cmd: &str, depth: usize) -> Vec<Segment> {
   895→    let (tokens, inner_segment_groups) = tokenize_inner(cmd, depth);
   896→
   897→    let mut segments: Vec<Segment> = Vec::new();
   898→    let mut current: Vec<Token> = Vec::new();
   899→
   900→    for tok in &tokens {
   901→        match tok {
   902→            Token::Operator(op) => {
   903→                if is_control_operator(op) {
   904→                    // Control operators split segments
   905→                    if !current.is_empty() {
   906→                        segments.push(Segment {
   907→                            tokens: std::mem::take(&mut current),
   908→                        });
   909→                    }
   910→                } else {
   911→                    // Redirect operators stay in the segment
   912→                    current.push(tok.clone());
   913→                }
   914→            }
   915→            Token::Word(_) => {
   916→                current.push(tok.clone());
   917→            }
   918→        }
   919→    }
   920→    if !current.is_empty() {
   921→        segments.push(Segment { tokens: current });
   922→    }
   923→
   924→    // Add segments from command substitutions
   925→    for group in inner_segment_groups {
   926→        segments.extend(group);
   927→    }
   928→
   929→    // Recursively parse shell -c arguments and eval arguments.
   930→    // bash -c "git push origin main" → the string arg is a shell command.
   931→    // eval "git push origin main" → the arg is a shell command.
   932→    // This extends the same recursive parsing used for $() and backticks.
   933→    if depth < MAX_SUBST_DEPTH {
   934→        let mut extra_segments: Vec<Segment> = Vec::new();
   935→        for seg in &segments {
   936→            if let Some((cmd_pos, cmd)) = seg.effective_command() {
   937→                if is_shell_command(cmd) {
   938→                    // Look for -c flag and its argument (Word tokens only)
   939→                    if let Some(c_pos) = seg.tokens[cmd_pos + 1..]
   940→                        .iter()
   941→                        .position(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w == "-c"))
   942→                    {
   943→                        let arg_pos = cmd_pos + 1 + c_pos + 1;
   944→                        if let Some(Token::Word(inner)) = seg.tokens.get(arg_pos) {
   945→                            let inner_segs = parse_segments_inner(inner, depth + 1);
   946→                            extra_segments.extend(inner_segs);
   947→                        }
   948→                    }
   949→                } else if cmd == "eval" {
   950→                    // eval concatenates all Word args and executes as a command
   951→                    let args: Vec<&str> = seg.tokens[cmd_pos + 1..]
   952→                        .iter()
   953→                        .filter_map(|t| match t {
   954→                            Token::Word(w) => Some(w.as_str()),
   955→                            Token::Operator(_) => None,
   956→                        })
   957→                        .collect();
   958→                    if !args.is_empty() {
   959→                        let inner = args.join(" ");
   960→                        let inner_segs = parse_segments_inner(&inner, depth + 1);
   961→                        extra_segments.extend(inner_segs);
   962→                    }
   963→                }
   964→            }
   965→        }
   966→        segments.extend(extra_segments);
   967→    }
   968→
   969→    segments
   970→}
   971→
   972→// ============================================================
   973→// Path resolution
   974→// ============================================================
   975→
   976→/// Normalize a path logically (without filesystem access).
   977→/// Resolves `.` and `..` components. Does NOT follow symlinks or
   978→/// check existence — the target may not exist yet (write operations).
   979→fn normalize_path(path: &std::path::Path) -> std::path::PathBuf {
   980→    use std::path::Component;
   981→    let mut components = Vec::new();
   982→    for component in path.components() {
   983→        match component {
   984→            Component::ParentDir => {
   985→                // Pop the last Normal component (go up one level).
   986→                // Never pop RootDir or Prefix — can't go above filesystem root.
   987→                if matches!(components.last(), Some(Component::Normal(_))) {
   988→                    components.pop();
   989→                }
   990→            }
   991→            Component::CurDir => {
   992→                // Skip . (current directory)
   993→            }
   994→            c => {
   995→                components.push(c);
   996→            }
   997→        }
   998→    }
   999→    components.iter().collect()

[tool] Read({"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs","offset":999,"limit":450})

[result]    999→    components.iter().collect()
  1000→}
  1001→
  1002→/// Resolve any path (absolute or relative) to a normalized absolute path.
  1003→/// Relative paths are joined against CWD. Returns None if CWD is unavailable
  1004→/// and the path is relative (fail-open).
  1005→fn resolve_path(path: &str) -> Option<std::path::PathBuf> {
  1006→    if path.is_empty() {
  1007→        return None;
  1008→    }
  1009→    if path.starts_with('/') {
  1010→        Some(normalize_path(std::path::Path::new(path)))
  1011→    } else {
  1012→        std::env::current_dir()
  1013→            .ok()
  1014→            .map(|cwd| normalize_path(&cwd.join(path)))
  1015→    }
  1016→}
  1017→
  1018→/// True if a path (absolute or relative) resolves to a protected location.
  1019→/// Protected = under /workspace/ but NOT under /workspace/.claude/worktrees/.
  1020→fn is_protected_path(path: &str) -> bool {
  1021→    match resolve_path(path) {
  1022→        Some(resolved) => {
  1023→            let s = resolved.to_string_lossy();
  1024→            s.starts_with("/workspace/") && !s.starts_with("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")
  1025→        }
  1026→        None => false, // fail-open
  1027→    }
  1028→}
  1029→
  1030→// ============================================================
  1031→// CWD helpers
  1032→// ============================================================
  1033→
  1034→fn is_workspace_cwd() -> bool {
  1035→    std::env::current_dir()
  1036→        .map(|p| {
  1037→            let s = p.to_string_lossy();
  1038→            s == "/workspace" || s == "/workspace/"
  1039→        })
  1040→        .unwrap_or(false) // fail-open: don't block CWD-dependent checks
  1041→}
  1042→
  1043→fn is_worktree_cwd() -> bool {
  1044→    std::env::current_dir()
  1045→        .map(|p| {
  1046→            p.to_string_lossy()
  1047→                .starts_with("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")
  1048→        })
  1049→        .unwrap_or(true) // fail-open: assume worktree, allow checkout/switch
  1050→}
  1051→
  1052→// ============================================================
  1053→// Check functions
  1054→// ============================================================
  1055→
  1056→/// Run all Edit/Write checks. Returns Some(reason) to block, None to allow.
  1057→pub fn check_edit(input: &serde_json::Value) -> Option<String> {
  1058→    let file_path = input["tool_input"]["file_path"].as_str().unwrap_or("");
  1059→
  1060→    // Protected files
  1061→    let basename = file_path.rsplit('/').next().unwrap_or(file_path);
  1062→    if file_path.contains("package-lock.json")
  1063→        || file_path.contains("src/wasm/scheduler/")
  1064→        || basename == ".env"
  1065→        || basename.starts_with(".env.")
  1066→    {
  1067→        return Some(format!(
  1068→            "Protected file — do not modify: {}. \
  1069→             package-lock.json is managed by npm, .env files contain secrets, \
  1070→             and src/wasm/scheduler/ is generated by the Rust build. \
  1071→             See CLAUDE.md for architecture constraints.",
  1072→            file_path
  1073→        ));
  1074→    }
  1075→
  1076→    // Workspace isolation
  1077→    if is_protected_path(file_path) {
  1078→        return Some(msg_workspace_edit_block());
  1079→    }
  1080→
  1081→    // CWD enforcement for worktree files
  1082→    if file_path.starts_with("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/") {
  1083→        if let Ok(cwd) = std::env::current_dir() {
  1084→            let cwd_str = cwd.to_string_lossy();
  1085→            if cwd_str == "/workspace" || cwd_str == "/workspace/" {
  1086→                return Some(
  1087→                    "You are editing a worktree file but your CWD is /workspace. \
  1088→                     Enter the worktree first (use the EnterWorktree tool or cd into it). \
  1089→                     Only the admin works from /workspace."
  1090→                        .to_string(),
  1091→                );
  1092→            }
  1093→        }
  1094→    }
  1095→
  1096→    None
  1097→}
  1098→
  1099→/// Run all Bash checks. Returns Some(reason) to block, None to allow.
  1100→pub fn check_bash(input: &serde_json::Value) -> Option<String> {
  1101→    let cmd = input["tool_input"]["command"].as_str().unwrap_or("");
  1102→
  1103→    let segments = parse_segments(cmd);
  1104→
  1105→    // push-to-main: block any push targeting the default branch.
  1106→    // Catches: git push origin main, git push origin HEAD:main,
  1107→    // git push origin feature:refs/heads/main, etc.
  1108→    for seg in &segments {
  1109→        if seg.is_git("push") {
  1110→            let targets_main = seg.tokens.iter().any(|t| {
  1111→                if let Token::Word(w) = t {
  1112→                    w == "main" || w.ends_with(":main") || w.ends_with(":refs/heads/main")
  1113→                } else {
  1114→                    false
  1115→                }
  1116→            });
  1117→            if targets_main {
  1118→                return Some(
  1119→                    "Cannot push directly to main. Use a feature branch and PR.".to_string(),
  1120→                );
  1121→            }
  1122→        }
  1123→    }
  1124→
  1125→    // checkout-switch (CWD-dependent)
  1126→    for seg in &segments {
  1127→        if (seg.is_git("checkout") || seg.is_git("switch"))
  1128→            && !seg.has_arg("--")
  1129→            && !seg.has_short_flag('b')
  1130→            && !seg.has_short_flag('B')
  1131→            && !seg.has_short_flag('c')
  1132→            && !seg.has_token_containing("worktree")
  1133→            && !is_worktree_cwd()
  1134→        {
  1135→            return Some(
  1136→                "Do not use git checkout/switch in /workspace. \
  1137→                 Use a worktree: git worktree add /workspace/.claude/worktrees/<name> -b <branch>"
  1138→                    .to_string(),
  1139→            );
  1140→        }
  1141→    }
  1142→
  1143→    // reset-hard: always block in /workspace (shared state).
  1144→    // In worktrees, only block without origin/ ref (likely mistake).
  1145→    // reset --hard origin/main is the documented squash-merge cleanup step.
  1146→    for seg in &segments {
  1147→        if seg.is_git("reset") && seg.has_arg("--hard") {
  1148→            if is_workspace_cwd() {
  1149→                return Some(
  1150→                    "Do not run git reset --hard in /workspace — it modifies shared state \
  1151→                     that other agents depend on. If you need to sync after a squash merge, \
  1152→                     run git reset --hard origin/<branch> in your own worktree instead. \
  1153→                     See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md."
  1154→                        .to_string(),
  1155→                );
  1156→            }
  1157→            if !seg.has_token_starting_with("origin/") {
  1158→                return Some(
  1159→                    "git reset --hard without an origin/ ref will discard uncommitted work. \
  1160→                     If syncing after a squash merge, use: git reset --hard origin/<branch>. \
  1161→                     If you intend to discard local changes, specify the target explicitly. \
  1162→                     See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md for the cleanup procedure."
  1163→                        .to_string(),
  1164→                );
  1165→            }
  1166→        }
  1167→    }
  1168→
  1169→    // clean-force: block in /workspace (shared state).
  1170→    // Allow in worktrees — agents legitimately clean build artifacts.
  1171→    for seg in &segments {
  1172→        if seg.is_git("clean")
  1173→            && (seg.has_short_flag('f') || seg.has_arg("--force"))
  1174→            && is_workspace_cwd()
  1175→        {
  1176→            return Some(
  1177→                "Do not run git clean -f in /workspace — it permanently deletes \
  1178→                 untracked files in shared state. Run this in your own worktree instead. \
  1179→                 Review files first with git clean -n."
  1180→                    .to_string(),
  1181→            );
  1182→        }
  1183→    }
  1184→
  1185→    // branch-force-delete: block in /workspace (could delete other agents' branches).
  1186→    // Allow in worktrees — needed for squash-merge cleanup where -d fails.
  1187→    for seg in &segments {
  1188→        if seg.is_git("branch") && seg.has_short_flag('D') && is_workspace_cwd() {
  1189→            return Some(
  1190→                "Do not run git branch -D in /workspace — it could delete another agent's \
  1191→                 branch. Use git branch -d (lowercase) which checks merge status, or run \
  1192→                 from your own worktree. See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md for cleanup procedure."
  1193→                    .to_string(),
  1194→            );
  1195→        }
  1196→    }
  1197→
  1198→    // worktree-remove: extract the target path from `git worktree remove <path>`.
  1199→    // Uses command structure (position after "remove") instead of scanning all tokens.
  1200→    for seg in &segments {
  1201→        if seg.is_git("worktree") && seg.has_arg("remove") {
  1202→            // Find the "remove" token position, then take the next Word as the path.
  1203→            let remove_pos = seg
  1204→                .tokens
  1205→                .iter()
  1206→                .position(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w == "remove"));
  1207→            let target_path = remove_pos.and_then(|pos| {
  1208→                // Skip any flags (--force, etc.) after "remove" to find the path argument
  1209→                seg.tokens[pos + 1..].iter().find_map(|t| match t {
  1210→                    Token::Word(w) if !w.starts_with('-') && w != "I_CREATED_THIS=1" => {
  1211→                        Some(w.as_str())
  1212→                    }
  1213→                    _ => None,
  1214→                })
  1215→            });
  1216→
  1217→            if let Some(target) = target_path {
  1218→                let resolved = resolve_path(target);
  1219→
  1220→                // Tier 1: target is own CWD — hard block (breaks Bash)
  1221→                let cwd_resolved = std::env::current_dir().ok().map(|cwd| normalize_path(&cwd));
  1222→                let is_own_cwd = match (&resolved, &cwd_resolved) {
  1223→                    (Some(r), Some(c)) => r == c,
  1224→                    (None, _) => false, // can't resolve target — fail-open
  1225→                    (_, None) => true,  // can't determine CWD — fail-safe
  1226→                };
  1227→
  1228→                if is_own_cwd {
  1229→                    return Some(msg_worktree_remove_cwd_block());
  1230→                }
  1231→
  1232→                // Tier 2: target is under agent worktrees dir — block with warning
  1233→                let is_agent_path = resolved
  1234→                    .as_ref()
  1235→                    .map(|r| {
  1236→                        r.to_string_lossy()
  1237→                            .starts_with("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")
  1238→                    })
  1239→                    .unwrap_or(false);
  1240→
  1241→                if is_agent_path {
  1242→                    // Allow if acknowledged with I_CREATED_THIS=1 prefix
  1243→                    let acknowledged = seg
  1244→                        .tokens
  1245→                        .first()
  1246→                        .is_some_and(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w == "I_CREATED_THIS=1"));
  1247→                    if !acknowledged {
  1248→                        return Some(
  1249→                            "⚠️  STOP — You are about to remove a worktree that may belong to \
  1250→                             another agent. Read this FULLY before proceeding.\n\n\
  1251→                             NEVER remove another agent's worktree. Other agents may be \
  1252→                             actively working in sibling worktrees even if they look idle.\n\n\
  1253→                             You may ONLY proceed if ALL of these are true:\n\
  1254→                             1. YOU created it (in this session or a previous one)\n\
  1255→                             2. Its PR is merged OR it was a test/scratch worktree\n\
  1256→                             3. You have verified no other agent is using it\n\n\
  1257→                             If all three are true, re-run with:\n\
  1258→                             I_CREATED_THIS=1 git worktree remove <path>\n\n\
  1259→                             If unsure, ask the user to remove it."
  1260→                                .to_string(),
  1261→                        );
  1262→                    }
  1263→                }
  1264→            }
  1265→
  1266→            // Tier 3: target is not an agent worktree (e.g. /tmp/wt) — allow.
  1267→        }
  1268→    }
  1269→
  1270→    // rm-worktree-root
  1271→    for seg in &segments {
  1272→        let cmd_name = seg.effective_command().map(|(_, c)| c);
  1273→        if cmd_name == Some("rm")
  1274→            && (seg.has_short_flag('r') || seg.has_short_flag('R') || seg.has_short_flag('f'))
  1275→            && seg.targets_worktree_root()
  1276→        {
  1277→            return Some(msg_rm_worktree_block());
  1278→        }
  1279→    }
  1280→
  1281→    // workspace-file-modification: sed -i
  1282→    for seg in &segments {
  1283→        let cmd_name = seg.effective_command().map(|(_, c)| c);
  1284→        if cmd_name == Some("sed")
  1285→            && (seg.has_arg("-i") || seg.has_token_starting_with("-i"))
  1286→            && seg.has_protected_path()
  1287→        {
  1288→            return Some(MSG_USE_WORKTREE.to_string());
  1289→        }
  1290→        // tee
  1291→        if cmd_name == Some("tee") && seg.has_protected_path() {
  1292→            return Some(MSG_USE_WORKTREE.to_string());
  1293→        }
  1294→    }
  1295→
  1296→    // interpreter-workspace: scan code arguments to python/node/perl/ruby
  1297→    // for workspace paths combined with write/exec indicators. We can't parse
  1298→    // these languages, but we can detect the combination of a /workspace/ path
  1299→    // and a write-like operation. This avoids false positives on read-only
  1300→    // operations (print, listdir, open for read) while catching writes and
  1301→    // shell-outs that bypass bash-level guards.
  1302→    for seg in &segments {
  1303→        if let Some((cmd_pos, cmd)) = seg.effective_command() {
  1304→            if let Some(flag) = script_interpreter_flag(cmd) {
  1305→                if let Some(f_pos) = seg.tokens[cmd_pos + 1..]
  1306→                    .iter()
  1307→                    .position(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w == flag))
  1308→                {
  1309→                    let arg_pos = cmd_pos + 1 + f_pos + 1;
  1310→                    if let Some(Token::Word(code)) = seg.tokens.get(arg_pos) {
  1311→                        if code.contains("/workspace/")
  1312→                            && !code.contains("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")
  1313→                            && has_write_indicator(code)
  1314→                        {
  1315→                            return Some(
  1316→                                "Do not use interpreter code to write to /workspace/ directly. \
  1317→                                 Use a worktree. Detected /workspace/ path with a write or exec \
  1318→                                 operation in inline code."
  1319→                                    .to_string(),
  1320→                            );
  1321→                        }
  1322→                    }
  1323→                }
  1324→            }
  1325→        }
  1326→    }
  1327→
  1328→    // Redirect check: type-safe via Token::Operator.
  1329→    // All write-capable redirect operators are blocked (per is_write_redirect):
  1330→    //   >  (truncate), >> (append), >| (clobber),
  1331→    //   >& (dup — with path target, acts as > + 2>&1),
  1332→    //   <> (read-write open), &> (bash: stdout+stderr), &>> (bash: append both).
  1333→    // Not blocked: < (input only), <& (fd dup, no path write).
  1334→    // Quoted/escaped > produces Word(">") and is naturally excluded.
  1335→    // No-space redirects (echo>/workspace/file) are properly tokenized as
  1336→    // [Word("echo"), Operator(">"), Word("/workspace/file")].
  1337→    for seg in &segments {
  1338→        for j in 0..seg.tokens.len() {
  1339→            if let Token::Operator(op) = &seg.tokens[j] {
  1340→                if is_write_redirect(op) {
  1341→                    if let Some(Token::Word(path)) = seg.tokens.get(j + 1) {
  1342→                        if is_protected_path(path) {
  1343→                            return Some(MSG_USE_WORKTREE.to_string());
  1344→                        }
  1345→                    }
  1346→                }
  1347→            }
  1348→        }
  1349→    }
  1350→
  1351→    None
  1352→}
  1353→
  1354→// ============================================================
  1355→// Tests
  1356→// ============================================================
  1357→
  1358→#[cfg(test)]
  1359→mod tests {
  1360→    use super::*;
  1361→    use serde_json::json;
  1362→
  1363→    /// True if tests are running from /workspace (vs a worktree).
  1364→    /// CWD-dependent checks (clean -f, branch -D) block in /workspace, allow in worktrees.
  1365→    fn in_workspace() -> bool {
  1366→        is_workspace_cwd()
  1367→    }
  1368→
  1369→    // Helper to extract word strings from tokens
  1370→    fn words(tokens: &[Token]) -> Vec<&str> {
  1371→        tokens
  1372→            .iter()
  1373→            .filter_map(|t| match t {
  1374→                Token::Word(w) => Some(w.as_str()),
  1375→                _ => None,
  1376→            })
  1377→            .collect()
  1378→    }
  1379→
  1380→    fn tok(cmd: &str) -> Vec<Token> {
  1381→        tokenize(cmd).0
  1382→    }
  1383→
  1384→    fn w(s: &str) -> Token {
  1385→        Token::Word(s.to_string())
  1386→    }
  1387→
  1388→    fn op(s: &str) -> Token {
  1389→        Token::Operator(s.to_string())
  1390→    }
  1391→
  1392→    // ================================================================
  1393→    // Test Organization
  1394→    // ================================================================
  1395→    //
  1396→    // Tests are organized by architecture layer, then by component.
  1397→    // Each L3 check covers these dimensions where applicable:
  1398→    //
  1399→    //   Dimension         | What it tests
  1400→    //   ------------------|----------------------------------------------
  1401→    //   basic trigger     | The check fires on the simplest form
  1402→    //   chained (&&, |)   | Check works across segment boundaries
  1403→    //   prefixed (sudo)   | Check works through command prefixes
  1404→    //   quoted (commit -m)| No false positive on quoted text
  1405→    //   heredoc body      | No false positive inside heredoc
  1406→    //   $() substitution  | Check works inside command substitution
  1407→    //   bash -c / eval    | Check works through recursive parsing
  1408→    //   CWD variant       | CWD-dependent checks assert via in_workspace()
  1409→    //   escape attempts   | ../  relative paths, case variants (-R vs -r)
  1410→    //
  1411→    // L1 Tokenizer: per-character-class + per-operator + quoting interactions
  1412→    // L2 Segments:  splitting + method coverage
  1413→    // L3 Checks:    per-check × dimensions above
  1414→    //
  1415→    // ================================================================
  1416→    // Layer 1: Tokenizer Tests
  1417→    // ================================================================
  1418→
  1419→    // ================================================================
  1420→    // Layer 1: Tokenizer Tests
  1421→    // ================================================================
  1422→

[assistant] Yes, these 32 commits tell the story of how the work *happened* (chronologically), not what the work *is* (logically). For a PR that will be squash-merged, the individual commits are the reviewer's guide. They should be organized by concern, not by conversation flow.

Current order mixes concerns:
- Commits 1-4: tokenizer refactor (from previous session)
- Commits 5-7: redirect operator recognition
- Commits 8-9: worktree remove (unrelated to redirect work)
- Commits 10-11: more redirect operators
- Commit 12: push refspec (unrelated)
- Commit 13: interpreter scanning (unrelated)
- Commits 14-15: path resolution (foundational — should come earlier)
- Commits 16-32: cleanup, reviews, tests, struct refactor (mixed)

A logical organization would be:

```
1. Foundation: tokenizer + Tokenizer struct + table-driven operators
2. Foundation: Segment with Vec<Token>, predicates, path resolution
3. Check: redirect operators (all types, type-safe)
4. Check: push-to-main (refspec detection)
5. Check: worktree-remove (three tiers, acknowledgment)
6. Check: CWD-aware (clean, branch-D)  
7. Check: interpreter scanning
8. Check: rm -R, .env precision, other fixes
9. Infrastructure: message constants, test organization, docs
```

Since this will be squash-merged, the individual commit history matters mainly for reviewability. But the user asked about logical organization with rich comments. The cleanest approach: interactive rebase to squash related commits together, reorder by concern, and write comprehensive commit messages.

However — 32 commits touching one file, with an interactive rebase, is risky (we saw the rebase issues earlier). The safer approach: squash everything into logical groups using `git reset --soft` to a specific point, then recommit.

But the user said this PR will be squash-merged (per `.claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md`). So the individual commits only matter for the reviewer reading the PR diff. The squash-merge commit message is what persists.

The pragmatic answer: the commits are fine for a squash-merge PR. What matters is the PR description (already written) and the squash commit message. Reorganizing 32 commits risks introducing bugs for no persistent benefit.

What do you think — do you want these reorganized for reviewer readability, or is the squash-merge approach sufficient given the PR description already documents the full scope?

[user] Provide a code review for the given pull request.

To do this, follow these steps precisely:

1. Use a Haiku agent to check if the pull request (a) is closed, (b) is a draft, (c) does not need a code review (eg. because it is an automated pull request, or is very simple and obviously ok), or (d) already has a code review from you from earlier. If so, do not proceed.
2. Use another Haiku agent to give you a list of file paths to (but not the contents of) any relevant CLAUDE.md files from the codebase: the root CLAUDE.md file (if one exists), as well as any CLAUDE.md files in the directories whose files the pull request modified
3. Use a Haiku agent to view the pull request, and ask the agent to return a summary of the change
4. Then, launch 5 parallel Sonnet agents to independently code review the change. The agents should do the following, then return a list of issues and the reason each issue was flagged (eg. CLAUDE.md adherence, bug, historical git context, etc.):
   a. Agent #1: Audit the changes to make sure they compily with the CLAUDE.md. Note that CLAUDE.md is guidance for Claude as it writes code, so not all instructions will be applicable during code review.
   b. Agent #2: Read the file changes in the pull request, then do a shallow scan for obvious bugs. Avoid reading extra context beyond the changes, focusing just on the changes themselves. Focus on large bugs, and avoid small issues and nitpicks. Ignore likely false positives.
   c. Agent #3: Read the git blame and history of the code modified, to identify any bugs in light of that historical context
   d. Agent #4: Read previous pull requests that touched these files, and check for any comments on those pull requests that may also apply to the current pull request.
   e. Agent #5: Read code comments in the modified files, and make sure the changes in the pull request comply with any guidance in the comments.
5. For each issue found in #4, launch a parallel Haiku agent that takes the PR, issue description, and list of CLAUDE.md files (from step 2), and returns a score to indicate the agent's level of confidence for whether the issue is real or false positive. To do that, the agent should score each issue on a scale from 0-100, indicating its level of confidence. For issues that were flagged due to CLAUDE.md instructions, the agent should double check that the CLAUDE.md actually calls out that issue specifically. The scale is (give this rubric to the agent verbatim):
   a. 0: Not confident at all. This is a false positive that doesn't stand up to light scrutiny, or is a pre-existing issue.
   b. 25: Somewhat confident. This might be a real issue, but may also be a false positive. The agent wasn't able to verify that it's a real issue. If the issue is stylistic, it is one that was not explicitly called out in the relevant CLAUDE.md.
   c. 50: Moderately confident. The agent was able to verify this is a real issue, but it might be a nitpick or not happen very often in practice. Relative to the rest of the PR, it's not very important.
   d. 75: Highly confident. The agent double checked the issue, and verified that it is very likely it is a real issue that will be hit in practice. The existing approach in the PR is insufficient. The issue is very important and will directly impact the code's functionality, or it is an issue that is directly mentioned in the relevant CLAUDE.md.
   e. 100: Absolutely certain. The agent double checked the issue, and confirmed that it is definitely a real issue, that will happen frequently in practice. The evidence directly confirms this.
6. Filter out any issues with a score less than 80. If there are no issues that meet this criteria, do not proceed.
7. Use a Haiku agent to repeat the eligibility check from #1, to make sure that the pull request is still eligible for code review.
8. Finally, use the gh bash command to comment back on the pull request with the result. When writing your comment, keep in mind to:
   a. Keep your output brief
   b. Avoid emojis
   c. Link and cite relevant code, files, and URLs

Examples of false positives, for steps 4 and 5:

- Pre-existing issues
- Something that looks like a bug but is not actually a bug
- Pedantic nitpicks that a senior engineer wouldn't call out
- Issues that a linter, typechecker, or compiler would catch (eg. missing or incorrect imports, type errors, broken tests, formatting issues, pedantic style issues like newlines). No need to run these build steps yourself -- it is safe to assume that they will be run separately as part of CI.
- General code quality issues (eg. lack of test coverage, general security issues, poor documentation), unless explicitly required in CLAUDE.md
- Issues that are called out in CLAUDE.md, but explicitly silenced in the code (eg. due to a lint ignore comment)
- Changes in functionality that are likely intentional or are directly related to the broader change
- Real issues, but on lines that the user did not modify in their pull request

Notes:

- Do not check build signal or attempt to build or typecheck the app. These will run separately, and are not relevant to your code review.
- Use `gh` to interact with Github (eg. to fetch a pull request, or to create inline comments), rather than web fetch
- Make a todo list first
- You must cite and link each bug (eg. if referring to a CLAUDE.md, you must link it)
- For your final comment, follow the following format precisely (assuming for this example that you found 3 issues):

---

### Code review

Found 3 issues:

1. <brief description of bug> (CLAUDE.md says "<...>")

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context, note that you MUST provide the full sha and not use bash here, eg. https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/1d54823877c4de72b2316a64032a54afc404e619/README.md#L13-L17>

2. <brief description of bug> (some/other/CLAUDE.md says "<...>")

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context>

3. <brief description of bug> (bug due to <file and code snippet>)

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context>

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

<sub>- If this code review was useful, please react with 👍. Otherwise, react with 👎.</sub>

---

- Or, if you found no issues:

---

### Code review

No issues found. Checked for bugs and CLAUDE.md compliance.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

- When linking to code, follow the following format precisely, otherwise the Markdown preview won't render correctly: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-cli-internal/blob/c21d3c10bc8e898b7ac1a2d745bdc9bc4e423afe/package.json#L10-L15
  - Requires full git sha
  - You must provide the full sha. Commands like `https://github.com/owner/repo/blob/$(git rev-parse HEAD)/foo/bar` will not work, since your comment will be directly rendered in Markdown.
  - Repo name must match the repo you're code reviewing
  - # sign after the file name
  - Line range format is L[start]-L[end]
  - Provide at least 1 line of context before and after, centered on the line you are commenting about (eg. if you are commenting about lines 5-6, you should link to `L4-7`)

[result] Launching skill: code-review:code-review

[user] Provide a code review for the given pull request.

To do this, follow these steps precisely:

1. Use a Haiku agent to check if the pull request (a) is closed, (b) is a draft, (c) does not need a code review (eg. because it is an automated pull request, or is very simple and obviously ok), or (d) already has a code review from you from earlier. If so, do not proceed.
2. Use another Haiku agent to give you a list of file paths to (but not the contents of) any relevant CLAUDE.md files from the codebase: the root CLAUDE.md file (if one exists), as well as any CLAUDE.md files in the directories whose files the pull request modified
3. Use a Haiku agent to view the pull request, and ask the agent to return a summary of the change
4. Then, launch 5 parallel Sonnet agents to independently code review the change. The agents should do the following, then return a list of issues and the reason each issue was flagged (eg. CLAUDE.md adherence, bug, historical git context, etc.):
   a. Agent #1: Audit the changes to make sure they compily with the CLAUDE.md. Note that CLAUDE.md is guidance for Claude as it writes code, so not all instructions will be applicable during code review.
   b. Agent #2: Read the file changes in the pull request, then do a shallow scan for obvious bugs. Avoid reading extra context beyond the changes, focusing just on the changes themselves. Focus on large bugs, and avoid small issues and nitpicks. Ignore likely false positives.
   c. Agent #3: Read the git blame and history of the code modified, to identify any bugs in light of that historical context
   d. Agent #4: Read previous pull requests that touched these files, and check for any comments on those pull requests that may also apply to the current pull request.
   e. Agent #5: Read code comments in the modified files, and make sure the changes in the pull request comply with any guidance in the comments.
5. For each issue found in #4, launch a parallel Haiku agent that takes the PR, issue description, and list of CLAUDE.md files (from step 2), and returns a score to indicate the agent's level of confidence for whether the issue is real or false positive. To do that, the agent should score each issue on a scale from 0-100, indicating its level of confidence. For issues that were flagged due to CLAUDE.md instructions, the agent should double check that the CLAUDE.md actually calls out that issue specifically. The scale is (give this rubric to the agent verbatim):
   a. 0: Not confident at all. This is a false positive that doesn't stand up to light scrutiny, or is a pre-existing issue.
   b. 25: Somewhat confident. This might be a real issue, but may also be a false positive. The agent wasn't able to verify that it's a real issue. If the issue is stylistic, it is one that was not explicitly called out in the relevant CLAUDE.md.
   c. 50: Moderately confident. The agent was able to verify this is a real issue, but it might be a nitpick or not happen very often in practice. Relative to the rest of the PR, it's not very important.
   d. 75: Highly confident. The agent double checked the issue, and verified that it is very likely it is a real issue that will be hit in practice. The existing approach in the PR is insufficient. The issue is very important and will directly impact the code's functionality, or it is an issue that is directly mentioned in the relevant CLAUDE.md.
   e. 100: Absolutely certain. The agent double checked the issue, and confirmed that it is definitely a real issue, that will happen frequently in practice. The evidence directly confirms this.
6. Filter out any issues with a score less than 80. If there are no issues that meet this criteria, do not proceed.
7. Use a Haiku agent to repeat the eligibility check from #1, to make sure that the pull request is still eligible for code review.
8. Finally, use the gh bash command to comment back on the pull request with the result. When writing your comment, keep in mind to:
   a. Keep your output brief
   b. Avoid emojis
   c. Link and cite relevant code, files, and URLs

Examples of false positives, for steps 4 and 5:

- Pre-existing issues
- Something that looks like a bug but is not actually a bug
- Pedantic nitpicks that a senior engineer wouldn't call out
- Issues that a linter, typechecker, or compiler would catch (eg. missing or incorrect imports, type errors, broken tests, formatting issues, pedantic style issues like newlines). No need to run these build steps yourself -- it is safe to assume that they will be run separately as part of CI.
- General code quality issues (eg. lack of test coverage, general security issues, poor documentation), unless explicitly required in CLAUDE.md
- Issues that are called out in CLAUDE.md, but explicitly silenced in the code (eg. due to a lint ignore comment)
- Changes in functionality that are likely intentional or are directly related to the broader change
- Real issues, but on lines that the user did not modify in their pull request

Notes:

- Do not check build signal or attempt to build or typecheck the app. These will run separately, and are not relevant to your code review.
- Use `gh` to interact with Github (eg. to fetch a pull request, or to create inline comments), rather than web fetch
- Make a todo list first
- You must cite and link each bug (eg. if referring to a CLAUDE.md, you must link it)
- For your final comment, follow the following format precisely (assuming for this example that you found 3 issues):

---

### Code review

Found 3 issues:

1. <brief description of bug> (CLAUDE.md says "<...>")

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context, note that you MUST provide the full sha and not use bash here, eg. https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/1d54823877c4de72b2316a64032a54afc404e619/README.md#L13-L17>

2. <brief description of bug> (some/other/CLAUDE.md says "<...>")

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context>

3. <brief description of bug> (bug due to <file and code snippet>)

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context>

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

<sub>- If this code review was useful, please react with 👍. Otherwise, react with 👎.</sub>

---

- Or, if you found no issues:

---

### Code review

No issues found. Checked for bugs and CLAUDE.md compliance.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

- When linking to code, follow the following format precisely, otherwise the Markdown preview won't render correctly: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-cli-internal/blob/c21d3c10bc8e898b7ac1a2d745bdc9bc4e423afe/package.json#L10-L15
  - Requires full git sha
  - You must provide the full sha. Commands like `https://github.com/owner/repo/blob/$(git rev-parse HEAD)/foo/bar` will not work, since your comment will be directly rendered in Markdown.
  - Repo name must match the repo you're code reviewing
  - # sign after the file name
  - Line range format is L[start]-L[end]
  - Provide at least 1 line of context before and after, centered on the line you are commenting about (eg. if you are commenting about lines 5-6, you should link to `L4-7`)
