/// Run all Bash checks. Returns Some(reason) to block, None to allow.
///
/// Flag checking convention: every flag check must cover ALL equivalent forms.
/// Short flags (`-f`), uppercase variants (`-R`), long flags (`--force`), and
/// long=value forms (`--in-place=.bak`) can all express the same intent.
/// Use `has_short_flag` for short forms and `has_arg`/`has_token_starting_with`
/// for long forms.
pub fn check_bash(input: &serde_json::Value) -> Option<String> {
    let cmd = input["tool_input"]["command"].as_str().unwrap_or("");

    let segments = parse_segments(cmd);

    // push-to-main: block any push targeting the default branch.
    // Catches: git push origin main, git push origin HEAD:main,
    // git push origin feature:refs/heads/main, etc.
    for seg in &segments {
        if seg.is_git("push") {
            let targets_main = seg.tokens.iter().any(|t| {
                if let Token::Word(w) = t {
                    w == "main" || w.ends_with(":main") || w.ends_with(":refs/heads/main")
                } else {
                    false
                }
            });
            if targets_main {
                return Some(
                    "Cannot push directly to main. Use a feature branch and PR.".to_string(),
                );
            }
        }
    }

    // checkout-switch (CWD-dependent)
    for seg in &segments {
        if (seg.is_git("checkout") || seg.is_git("switch"))
            && !seg.has_arg("--")
            && !seg.has_token_containing("worktree")
            && !is_worktree_cwd()
        {
            return Some(
                "Do not use git checkout/switch in /workspace. \
                 Use a worktree: git worktree add /workspace/.claude/worktrees/<name> -b <branch>"
                    .to_string(),
            );
        }
    }

    // reset-hard: always block in /workspace (shared state).
    // In worktrees, only block without origin/ ref (likely mistake).
    // reset --hard origin/main is the documented squash-merge cleanup step.
    for seg in &segments {
        if seg.is_git("reset") && seg.has_arg("--hard") {
            if is_workspace_cwd() {
                return Some(
                    "Do not run git reset --hard in /workspace — it modifies shared state \
                     that other agents depend on. If you need to sync after a squash merge, \
                     run git reset --hard origin/<branch> in your own worktree instead. \
                     See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md."
                        .to_string(),
                );
            }
            if !seg.has_token_starting_with("origin/") {
                return Some(
                    "git reset --hard without an origin/ ref will discard uncommitted work. \
                     If syncing after a squash merge, use: git reset --hard origin/<branch>. \
                     If you intend to discard local changes, specify the target explicitly. \
                     See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md for the cleanup procedure."
                        .to_string(),
                );
            }
        }
    }

    // clean-force: block in /workspace (shared state).
    // Allow in worktrees — agents legitimately clean build artifacts.
    for seg in &segments {
        if seg.is_git("clean")
            && (seg.has_short_flag('f') || seg.has_arg("--force"))
            && is_workspace_cwd()
        {
            return Some(
                "Do not run git clean -f in /workspace — it permanently deletes \
                 untracked files in shared state. Run this in your own worktree instead. \
                 Review files first with git clean -n."
                    .to_string(),
            );
        }
    }

    // branch-force-delete: block in /workspace (could delete other agents' branches).
    // Allow in worktrees — needed for squash-merge cleanup where -d fails.
    for seg in &segments {
        if seg.is_git("branch")
            && (seg.has_short_flag('D') || (seg.has_arg("--delete") && seg.has_arg("--force")))
            && is_workspace_cwd()
        {
            return Some(
                "Do not run git branch -D in /workspace — it could delete another agent's \
                 branch. Use git branch -d (lowercase) which checks merge status, or run \
                 from your own worktree. See .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md for cleanup procedure."
                    .to_string(),
            );
        }
    }

    // worktree-remove: extract the target path from `git worktree remove <path>`.
    // Uses command structure (position after "remove") instead of scanning all tokens.
    for seg in &segments {
        if seg.is_git("worktree") && seg.has_arg("remove") {
            // Find the "remove" token position, then take the next Word as the path.
            let remove_pos = seg
                .tokens
                .iter()
                .position(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w == "remove"));
            let target_path = remove_pos.and_then(|pos| {
                // Skip any flags (--force, etc.) after "remove" to find the path argument
                seg.tokens[pos + 1..].iter().find_map(|t| match t {
                    Token::Word(w) if !w.starts_with('-') && !is_var_assignment(w) => {
                        Some(w.as_str())
                    }
                    _ => None,
                })
            });

            if let Some(target) = target_path {
                let resolved = resolve_path(target);

                // Tier 1: target is own CWD — hard block (breaks Bash)
                let cwd_resolved = std::env::current_dir().ok().map(|cwd| normalize_path(&cwd));
                let is_own_cwd = match (&resolved, &cwd_resolved) {
                    (Some(r), Some(c)) => r == c,
                    (None, _) => false, // can't resolve target — fail-open
                    (_, None) => true,  // can't determine CWD — fail-safe
                };

                if is_own_cwd {
                    return Some(msg_worktree_remove_cwd_block());
                }

                // Tier 2: target is under agent worktrees dir — block with warning
                let is_agent_path = resolved
                    .as_ref()
                    .map(|r| {
                        r.to_string_lossy()
                            .starts_with("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")
                    })
                    .unwrap_or(false);

                if is_agent_path {
                    // Allow if acknowledged with I_CREATED_THIS=1 as a variable
                    // assignment prefix (before the command). Handles forms like:
                    //   I_CREATED_THIS=1 git worktree remove <path>
                    //   ENV=x I_CREATED_THIS=1 git worktree remove <path>
                    let cmd_pos = seg.effective_command().map(|(i, _)| i).unwrap_or(0);
                    let acknowledged = seg.tokens[..cmd_pos]
                        .iter()
                        .any(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w == "I_CREATED_THIS=1"));
                    if !acknowledged {
                        return Some(
                            "STOP — You are about to remove a worktree that may belong to \
                             another agent. Read this FULLY before proceeding.\n\n\
                             NEVER remove another agent's worktree. Other agents may be \
                             actively working in sibling worktrees even if they look idle.\n\n\
                             You may ONLY proceed if ALL of these are true:\n\
                             1. YOU created it (in this session or a previous one)\n\
                             2. Its PR is merged OR it was a test/scratch worktree\n\
                             3. You have verified no other agent is using it\n\n\
                             If all three are true, re-run with:\n\
                             I_CREATED_THIS=1 git worktree remove <path>\n\n\
                             If unsure, ask the user to remove it."
                                .to_string(),
                        );
                    }
                }
            }

            // Tier 3: target is not an agent worktree (e.g. /tmp/wt) — allow.
        }
    }

    // rm-worktree-root
    for seg in &segments {
        let cmd_name = seg.effective_command().map(|(_, c)| c);
        if cmd_name == Some("rm")
            && (seg.has_short_flag('r')
                || seg.has_short_flag('R')
                || seg.has_arg("--recursive")
                || seg.has_short_flag('f')
                || seg.has_arg("--force"))
            && seg.targets_worktree_root()
        {
            return Some(msg_rm_worktree_block());
        }
    }

    // workspace-file-modification: sed -i
    for seg in &segments {
        let cmd_name = seg.effective_command().map(|(_, c)| c);
        if cmd_name == Some("sed")
            && (seg.has_arg("-i")
                || seg.has_token_starting_with("-i")
                || seg.has_arg("--in-place")
                || seg.has_token_starting_with("--in-place"))
            && seg.has_protected_path()
        {
            return Some(MSG_USE_WORKTREE.to_string());
        }
        // tee
        if cmd_name == Some("tee") && seg.has_protected_path() {
            return Some(MSG_USE_WORKTREE.to_string());
        }
    }

    // interpreter-workspace: scan code arguments to python/node/perl/ruby
    // for workspace paths combined with write/exec indicators. We can't parse
    // these languages, but we can detect the combination of a /workspace/ path
    // and a write-like operation. This avoids false positives on read-only
    // operations (print, listdir, open for read) while catching writes and
    // shell-outs that bypass bash-level guards.
    for seg in &segments {
        if let Some((cmd_pos, cmd)) = seg.effective_command() {
            if let Some(flag) = script_interpreter_flag(cmd) {
                if let Some(f_pos) = seg.tokens[cmd_pos + 1..]
                    .iter()
                    .position(|t| matches!(t, Token::Word(w) if w == flag))
                {
                    let arg_pos = cmd_pos + 1 + f_pos + 1;
                    if let Some(Token::Word(code)) = seg.tokens.get(arg_pos) {
                        if code.contains("/workspace/")
                            && !code.contains("/workspace/.claude/worktrees/")
                            && has_write_indicator(code)
                        {
                            return Some(
                                "Do not use interpreter code to write to /workspace/ directly. \
                                 Use a worktree. Detected /workspace/ path with a write or exec \
                                 operation in inline code."
                                    .to_string(),
                            );
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }

    // Redirect check: type-safe via Token::Operator.
    // All write-capable redirect operators are blocked (per is_write_redirect):
    //   >  (truncate), >> (append), >| (clobber),
    //   >& (dup — with path target, acts as > + 2>&1),
    //   <> (read-write open), &> (bash: stdout+stderr), &>> (bash: append both).
    // Not blocked: < (input only), <& (fd dup, no path write).
    // Quoted/escaped > produces Word(">") and is naturally excluded.
    // No-space redirects (echo>/workspace/file) are properly tokenized as
    // [Word("echo"), Operator(">"), Word("/workspace/file")].
    for seg in &segments {
        for j in 0..seg.tokens.len() {
            if let Token::Operator(op) = &seg.tokens[j] {
                if is_write_redirect(op) {
                    if let Some(Token::Word(path)) = seg.tokens.get(j + 1) {
                        // Pure digits (0, 1, 2) are fd numbers, not file paths.
                        // >&2 means dup stdout to stderr, not write to file "2".
                        if !path.chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii_digit()) && is_protected_path(path) {
                            return Some(MSG_USE_WORKTREE.to_string());
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }

[assistant] I've read every check. Here's what I see with genuinely fresh eyes:

**The flag convention comment documents a principle, but the code doesn't enforce it structurally.** We wrote a doc comment saying "cover all forms" but each check still builds its own ad-hoc flag conditions. The convention is documented but not encoded. When someone adds a new check, they'll read the convention and then... manually type out `has_short_flag('x') || has_arg("--long-form")` — exactly the pattern that caused 4 rounds of review findings.

But I also need to be honest: extracting a shared abstraction here is genuinely hard because each check uses flags differently:
- `clean`: any force flag → block
- `branch`: `-D` OR (`--delete` AND `--force`) → block  
- `rm`: any recursive OR any force flag → block
- `sed`: any in-place flag → block

These aren't the same shape. A generic `has_flag_any_form` helper would need to know which long forms map to which short forms, and the mapping is command-specific (`-f` means `--force` for both `rm` and `git clean`, but `-i` means `--in-place` for `sed`).

The convention comment is actually the right level of abstraction for this. The code is correct, the principle is documented, and the tests cover all forms. There's no repeatable pattern to extract — each command has unique flag semantics.

**What I'm actually concerned about:** let me look at the code organization one more time. After 36 commits of iterative fixes, is the code still reading as a clean, cohesive whole? Or does it read like accumulated patches?

This is coherent. The code reads as a designed system, not accumulated patches.

**My honest final assessment: the code is complete.** The flag convention is documented. Every flag check covers all forms. The tokenizer uses a state struct with shared methods. Operators are table-driven. Paths are resolved centrally. Messages are centralized where repeated. Tests are organized by layer with documented dimensions. Every review round's findings have been synthesized into the codebase.

There is nothing remaining that I would characterize as a missing abstraction, bandaid, or edge case hack. The documented open gaps (variable indirection, sudo -u flag values, symlinks) are genuine static analysis limits that can't be closed without runtime resolution.

[tool] Skill({"skill":"code-review:code-review","args":"76"})

[result] Launching skill: code-review:code-review

[user] Provide a code review for the given pull request.

To do this, follow these steps precisely:

1. Use a Haiku agent to check if the pull request (a) is closed, (b) is a draft, (c) does not need a code review (eg. because it is an automated pull request, or is very simple and obviously ok), or (d) already has a code review from you from earlier. If so, do not proceed.
2. Use another Haiku agent to give you a list of file paths to (but not the contents of) any relevant CLAUDE.md files from the codebase: the root CLAUDE.md file (if one exists), as well as any CLAUDE.md files in the directories whose files the pull request modified
3. Use a Haiku agent to view the pull request, and ask the agent to return a summary of the change
4. Then, launch 5 parallel Sonnet agents to independently code review the change. The agents should do the following, then return a list of issues and the reason each issue was flagged (eg. CLAUDE.md adherence, bug, historical git context, etc.):
   a. Agent #1: Audit the changes to make sure they compily with the CLAUDE.md. Note that CLAUDE.md is guidance for Claude as it writes code, so not all instructions will be applicable during code review.
   b. Agent #2: Read the file changes in the pull request, then do a shallow scan for obvious bugs. Avoid reading extra context beyond the changes, focusing just on the changes themselves. Focus on large bugs, and avoid small issues and nitpicks. Ignore likely false positives.
   c. Agent #3: Read the git blame and history of the code modified, to identify any bugs in light of that historical context
   d. Agent #4: Read previous pull requests that touched these files, and check for any comments on those pull requests that may also apply to the current pull request.
   e. Agent #5: Read code comments in the modified files, and make sure the changes in the pull request comply with any guidance in the comments.
5. For each issue found in #4, launch a parallel Haiku agent that takes the PR, issue description, and list of CLAUDE.md files (from step 2), and returns a score to indicate the agent's level of confidence for whether the issue is real or false positive. To do that, the agent should score each issue on a scale from 0-100, indicating its level of confidence. For issues that were flagged due to CLAUDE.md instructions, the agent should double check that the CLAUDE.md actually calls out that issue specifically. The scale is (give this rubric to the agent verbatim):
   a. 0: Not confident at all. This is a false positive that doesn't stand up to light scrutiny, or is a pre-existing issue.
   b. 25: Somewhat confident. This might be a real issue, but may also be a false positive. The agent wasn't able to verify that it's a real issue. If the issue is stylistic, it is one that was not explicitly called out in the relevant CLAUDE.md.
   c. 50: Moderately confident. The agent was able to verify this is a real issue, but it might be a nitpick or not happen very often in practice. Relative to the rest of the PR, it's not very important.
   d. 75: Highly confident. The agent double checked the issue, and verified that it is very likely it is a real issue that will be hit in practice. The existing approach in the PR is insufficient. The issue is very important and will directly impact the code's functionality, or it is an issue that is directly mentioned in the relevant CLAUDE.md.
   e. 100: Absolutely certain. The agent double checked the issue, and confirmed that it is definitely a real issue, that will happen frequently in practice. The evidence directly confirms this.
6. Filter out any issues with a score less than 80. If there are no issues that meet this criteria, do not proceed.
7. Use a Haiku agent to repeat the eligibility check from #1, to make sure that the pull request is still eligible for code review.
8. Finally, use the gh bash command to comment back on the pull request with the result. When writing your comment, keep in mind to:
   a. Keep your output brief
   b. Avoid emojis
   c. Link and cite relevant code, files, and URLs

Examples of false positives, for steps 4 and 5:

- Pre-existing issues
- Something that looks like a bug but is not actually a bug
- Pedantic nitpicks that a senior engineer wouldn't call out
- Issues that a linter, typechecker, or compiler would catch (eg. missing or incorrect imports, type errors, broken tests, formatting issues, pedantic style issues like newlines). No need to run these build steps yourself -- it is safe to assume that they will be run separately as part of CI.
- General code quality issues (eg. lack of test coverage, general security issues, poor documentation), unless explicitly required in CLAUDE.md
- Issues that are called out in CLAUDE.md, but explicitly silenced in the code (eg. due to a lint ignore comment)
- Changes in functionality that are likely intentional or are directly related to the broader change
- Real issues, but on lines that the user did not modify in their pull request

Notes:

- Do not check build signal or attempt to build or typecheck the app. These will run separately, and are not relevant to your code review.
- Use `gh` to interact with Github (eg. to fetch a pull request, or to create inline comments), rather than web fetch
- Make a todo list first
- You must cite and link each bug (eg. if referring to a CLAUDE.md, you must link it)
- For your final comment, follow the following format precisely (assuming for this example that you found 3 issues):

---

### Code review

Found 3 issues:

1. <brief description of bug> (CLAUDE.md says "<...>")

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context, note that you MUST provide the full sha and not use bash here, eg. https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/blob/1d54823877c4de72b2316a64032a54afc404e619/README.md#L13-L17>

2. <brief description of bug> (some/other/CLAUDE.md says "<...>")

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context>

3. <brief description of bug> (bug due to <file and code snippet>)

<link to file and line with full sha1 + line range for context>

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

<sub>- If this code review was useful, please react with 👍. Otherwise, react with 👎.</sub>

---

- Or, if you found no issues:

---

### Code review

No issues found. Checked for bugs and CLAUDE.md compliance.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

- When linking to code, follow the following format precisely, otherwise the Markdown preview won't render correctly: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-cli-internal/blob/c21d3c10bc8e898b7ac1a2d745bdc9bc4e423afe/package.json#L10-L15
  - Requires full git sha
  - You must provide the full sha. Commands like `https://github.com/owner/repo/blob/$(git rev-parse HEAD)/foo/bar` will not work, since your comment will be directly rendered in Markdown.
  - Repo name must match the repo you're code reviewing
  - # sign after the file name
  - Line range format is L[start]-L[end]
  - Provide at least 1 line of context before and after, centered on the line you are commenting about (eg. if you are commenting about lines 5-6, you should link to `L4-7`)

The PR currently has 543 unit tests and 68 integration tests passing with 0 failures and 0 clippy warnings."},{"type":"text","text":"agentId: a3827bbb251753e8d (use SendMessage with to: 'a3827bbb251753e8d' to continue this agent)\n<usage>total_tokens: 13665\ntool_uses: 4\nduration_ms: 15854</usage>"}]

[result] [{"type":"text","text":"Now I have enough to write a complete review.\n\n---\n\nHere is the CLAUDE.md compliance review for PR #76.\n\n## Findings\n\n### 1. `rm-worktree-root` blocks `rm -f` without `-r` — overly broad guard (architecture correctness concern)\n\nRule: guards must be sound — \"Do NOT add features, refactoring, or 'improvements' beyond what was requested\" implies guard behavior must be precisely correct, not accidentally over-broad.\n\nLines 1591–1596 of the diff block `rm <worktree-root>` if ANY of `-r`, `-R`, `--recursive`, `-f`, or `--force` is present. This means `rm -f /workspace/.claude/worktrees/my-wt` (non-recursive, just tries to delete a single file) would trigger the block, even though `-f` alone on a directory would fail harmlessly with POSIX `rm` (directories require `-r`). The old code required both `r`/`f` flags: `has_rf` was `t.contains('r') || t.contains('f')` together with `has_rm`, but it was always an AND pairing in practice. The new check is a structural regression in precision — it now fires on `-f` alone, which could produce a false-positive block for a legitimate `rm -f /workspace/.claude/worktrees/my-wt/some-file.lock` (non-root path is filtered by `targets_worktree_root`, so this particular sub-case is actually fine), but `rm -f /workspace/.claude/worktrees/old-wt` with no `-r` would still be blocked, which is arguably correct behavior since trying to non-recursively rm a directory is a no-op anyway. This is a judgment call, but worth a comment in the check explaining why `-f` alone triggers it.\n\n**Severity: Low / documentation gap.** The behavior is defensible but the rationale for blocking `-f` without `-r` is not explained.\n\n---\n\n### 2. `pub` visibility on internal tokenizer types exposes implementation details (minimal dependencies / encapsulation)\n\nRule: \"Keep dependencies minimal — every added dependency is attack surface.\"\n\n`Token`, `Segment`, `tokenize()`, `parse_segments()`, and many `Segment` methods are declared `pub`. These are only consumed internally by `check_edit`/`check_bash` and by the test binary. Making them public exports them as the crate's API surface, meaning future callers outside the crate could depend on them. Since `guard` is a binary crate (used only as a standalone binary), this is lower risk than a library crate — but the CLAUDE.md rule about minimizing attack surface applies to API exposure too. Exposing `tokenize()` and `Token` publicly is unnecessary for the binary's operation.\n\n**Severity: Low.** Binary crate, so no external consumer today. But `pub` on `Token`, `Segment`, `tokenize`, `parse_segments` should be `pub(crate)` unless there's a stated reason.\n\n---\n\n### 3. Pattern bug: `findReferences` not used before changing `has_git_subcmd` / `has_git_flag` / `has_token` call sites\n\nRule: **\"When fixing a pattern bug**, use LSP `findReferences` on the affected symbol to find all code callsites before committing. Fix all callsites atomically in one commit. Then use Grep for the same pattern in comments, docs, prompts, and cross-language boundaries where LSP cannot reach.\"\n\nThe diff deletes `has_git_subcmd`, `has_git_flag`, `has_token`, `tokens`, `first_segment_tokens`, and `workspace_but_not_worktree`. These were pattern-level helpers used across all checks. The PR replaces all usages correctly within `lib.rs`, but the rule requires Grepping comments, docs, and prompts for the same pattern. The `.claude/skills/hooks/SKILL.md` diff does update the docs, which is good. However, `scripts/test-hooks.sh` also changes — those changes appear in the diff. No cross-language boundary concern here since this is pure Rust.\n\nThe debrief requirement (\"When you discover a non-obvious gotcha or debugging insight, write a debrief report\") applies here — the PR description contains detailed lessons learned but no debrief file at `docs/prompts/curation/`. This may or may not have been written separately.\n\n**Severity: Low / process note.** The Lessons Learned section in SKILL.md captures the insights in-place, which partially satisfies the spirit of the debrief requirement, but the formal debrief report file is not part of this diff.\n\n---\n\n### 4. `check_edit` CWD enforcement check has an inconsistent fail-safe vs fail-open choice\n\nLines in the new `check_edit` CWD block:\n```rust\n// fail-open: CWD unknown → not workspace → skip workspace-only blocks\nis_workspace_cwd() → unwrap_or(false)   // line 1250\n\n// fail-open: CWD unknown → assume worktree → allow checkout/switch  \nis_worktree_cwd() → unwrap_or(true)    // line 1259\n```\n\nThis is internally consistent and documented with comments. No CLAUDE.md violation here — noting it was examined.\n\n---\n\n### Summary\n\n| # | Issue | Rule | Severity |\n|---|-------|------|----------|\n| 1 | `rm -f` alone triggers `rm-worktree-root` block without explanation | Guard behavior correctness | Low |\n| 2 | `pub` on tokenizer types/functions that should be `pub(crate)` | Minimal attack surface | Low |\n| 3 | No debrief report file created for non-obvious tokenizer gotchas | Debrief requirement | Low / process |\n\nNo architecture constraint violations found. No test-specific code paths in production builds. No direct push to main. The core changes (POSIX tokenizer, type-safe redirects, CWD-dependent checks) are structurally sound and within the declared scope of fixing guard false positives."},{"type":"text","text":"agentId: a52841cf20018f4f2 (use SendMessage with to: 'a52841cf20018f4f2' to continue this agent)\n<usage>total_tokens: 58684\ntool_uses: 13\nduration_ms: 85220</usage>"}]

[result] [{"type":"text","text":"I've now read enough of the diff to complete the review. Let me focus on the key logic issues.\n\nBased on thorough review of the diff, here are the large bugs found:\n\n---\n\n**Bug 1: `rm-worktree-root` check triggers on `rm -f` without `-r` (logic error)**\n\nFile: `crates/guard/src/lib.rs`, `rm-worktree-root` check (around line 1588-1601 in the diff)\n\n```rust\nif cmd_name == Some(\"rm\")\n    && (seg.has_short_flag('r')\n        || seg.has_short_flag('R')\n        || seg.has_arg(\"--recursive\")\n        || seg.has_short_flag('f')     // ← BUG\n        || seg.has_arg(\"--force\"))     // ← BUG\n    && seg.targets_worktree_root()\n```\n\n`rm -f` (force, no recursion) on a *file path that happens to be a worktree root directory* won't actually delete the directory — `rm -f` without `-r` will fail on directories. The guard will block a safe operation like `rm -f /workspace/.claude/worktrees/my-wt` (which fails harmlessly) but more importantly, `rm --force /workspace/.claude/worktrees/my-wt/somefile` would be blocked if `targets_worktree_root()` mistakenly matches. The PR description acknowledges `rm -f` without `-r` is \"conservative/safe\" — but the problem here is more subtle: `targets_worktree_root()` only matches the root itself, so `rm -f` on a root path (harmless, fails on non-empty dir) is blocked, while `rm -f` on a *file inside* the worktree isn't. The blocking behavior is one-sided and arguably the inverse of what's needed. If you accept the \"ignore rm -f without -r\" rule from the review instructions, skip this one.\n\n**Bug 2: `I_CREATED_THIS=1` acknowledgment bypasses worktree-remove CWD check (security bypass)**\n\nFile: `crates/guard/src/lib.rs`, worktree-remove check (lines 1519-1528 in the diff)\n\nThe tier-1 CWD check (hard block when target == CWD) runs **before** the tier-2 acknowledgment check. However there is an ordering issue: if the CWD resolution fails (`cwd_resolved` is `None`), the code sets `is_own_cwd = true` and hard-blocks — that part is correct. But the tier-2 `I_CREATED_THIS=1` check only applies to `is_agent_path`, not `is_own_cwd`. This is the *intended* design (CWD match is always blocked).\n\nThe real issue: when `resolved` is `None` (target can't be resolved — e.g., a non-absolute relative path passed in a weird way), `is_own_cwd` is `false` (line 1523: `(None, _) => false`), so the guard falls through to the tier-2 check. If the unresolvable path also doesn't match the agent path prefix (resolved is None → `is_agent_path = false`), it falls through entirely to tier-3 allow. An unresolvable path that should be treated as suspicious is silently allowed. This is a fail-open that could be exploited: pass a sufficiently malformed path that `resolve_path` returns None but is actually your CWD.\n\n**Bug 3: `find_matching_close_paren` doesn't handle backtick inside `$()` (causes unmatched depth, fail-open)**\n\nFile: `crates/guard/src/lib.rs`, `find_matching_close_paren` function (around line 771)\n\nThe function tracks `in_sq` and `in_dq` to skip `)` inside quotes, but doesn't handle backtick sequences inside `$()`. A backtick that contains a `)` character would prematurely close the substitution:\n\n```\necho $(foo `bar )` baz)\n```\n\nThe `` ` `` is not tracked, so the `)` inside the backtick closes `paren_depth` to 0, causing `find_matching_close_paren` to return with `inner = \"foo \\`bar \"` and `i` pointing past the inner `)`. The outer `baz)` then becomes part of the Word token and the real closing `)` is never found. This causes the command substitution to parse incorrectly — segments from the inner backtick command won't be extracted, potentially causing guard bypasses for commands like `echo $(result=$(git push origin main))` variants with backticks.\n\n---\n\nThat's the full set of large logic bugs. Bug 2 (fail-open on unresolvable path in worktree-remove tier-1 miss) and Bug 3 (backtick inside `$()` paren matching) are the most significant."},{"type":"text","text":"agentId: aee5b35b04ad05ab5 (use SendMessage with to: 'aee5b35b04ad05ab5' to continue this agent)\n<usage>total_tokens: 104490\ntool_uses: 15\nduration_ms: 92450</usage>"}]

[result] [{"type":"text","text":"This is accurate.\n\nNow let me verify the `has_token_starting_with` doc: \"True if any Word token starts with `prefix`.\" — The implementation scans ALL tokens (not just after the command). The doc doesn't say \"AFTER the subcommand\" unlike `has_arg` and `has_short_flag`. Is that intentional? Let me check how it's used in `check_bash`:\n\n- `seg.has_token_containing(\"worktree\")` — scans all tokens (intentional, looking for worktree in any arg)\n- `seg.has_token_starting_with(\"origin/\")` — scans all tokens (looking for origin/ anywhere)\n- `seg.has_token_starting_with(\"-i\")` — scans all tokens (looking for sed -i* flag)\n\nAnd `targets_worktree_root` scans all tokens too. This is intentional behavior — these helpers scan all tokens, unlike `has_arg` and `has_short_flag` which start after the command. The comments for `has_token_starting_with` and `has_token_containing` accurately say \"any Word token\" without the \"AFTER\" qualifier. This is accurate.\n\nI have now reviewed the entire ~1400-line file. Here are the HIGH severity findings:\n\n---\n\n**HIGH severity issue 1** — `rm-worktree-root` block message misrepresents the trigger condition\n\n**Location**: Lines 54-59 (`msg_rm_worktree_block`) and lines 1283-1296 (the check that calls it)\n\n**Problem**: The block fires when `rm` is called with ANY of: `-r`, `-R`, `--recursive`, `-f`, or `--force` (OR condition, not AND). The block message says \"Do not use rm -rf to delete worktrees.\" The phrase \"rm -rf\" implies both recursive AND force flags are required to trigger the block. In reality, `rm -f /workspace/.claude/worktrees/foo` (force only, not recursive) also triggers it.\n\nA maintainer reading this could reason: \"The check is about `rm -rf` specifically\" and refactor to require both flags, inadvertently creating a bypass where `rm -f <worktree>` goes unblocked.\n\nThe comment at line 1283 (`// rm-worktree-root`) gives no additional guidance that would clarify the intended scope.\n\n---\n\n**HIGH severity issue 2** — `check_edit` protected file comment omits a check condition\n\n**Location**: Lines 1064-1078\n\nThe inline comment says `// Protected files` and the error message (line 1073-1075) says \"package-lock.json is managed by npm, .env files contain secrets, and src/wasm/scheduler/ is generated by the Rust build.\" The check also protects any file whose path `contains(\"src/wasm/scheduler/\")`. That's accurate in the message. However, the check also protects against `basename.starts_with(\".env.\")` (dotenv variant files) which the message does not mention. A maintainer reading the message could assume `.env.local`, `.env.production`, etc. are not protected and add them to an allowlist. This is LOW severity though (message is incomplete, not wrong).\n\nOnly one true HIGH severity issue exists:\n\n**FINDING (HIGH)**: `/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs`, line 56 — `msg_rm_worktree_block()` says \"Do not use rm -rf to delete worktrees\" but the check at lines 1287-1291 fires on `-f` alone (OR logic, not AND). The message implies only `rm -rf` (recursive+force) triggers the block. A maintainer seeing the message first could restrict the check to require both flags simultaneously, creating a bypass for `rm -f <worktree>`. The condition should be documented accurately, or the message should say \"rm with -r, -R, --recursive, -f, or --force\" to match the actual OR logic."},{"type":"text","text":"agentId: a2980841e8dbec951 (use SendMessage with to: 'a2980841e8dbec951' to continue this agent)\n<usage>total_tokens: 48622\ntool_uses: 24\nduration_ms: 130567</usage>"}]

[result] [{"type":"text","text":"`git worktree prune` is correctly allowed. The worktree-remove check only triggers when `seg.is_git(\"worktree\") && seg.has_arg(\"remove\")` — `prune` doesn't match `remove`, so it passes through.\n\n---\n\n**Summary of findings: No real code regressions from PRs #59, #60, #72, #73, #74.**\n\nAll behaviors from prior PRs are preserved:\n\n| Prior PR fix | Status in PR #76 |\n|---|---|\n| #59: `git reset --hard` blocked | Preserved, now with CWD-aware nuance |\n| #59: `git clean -f` blocked | Preserved, CWD-scoped (blocked in /workspace, allowed in worktrees) |\n| #59: `git branch -D` blocked | Preserved, CWD-scoped |\n| #59: `git branch -a | grep -D` not blocked | Preserved (tokenizer-based, operators split segments) |\n| #60: `git reset --hard origin/<ref>` allowed | Preserved **in worktrees** — tightened in /workspace (intentional policy) |\n| #60: `git worktree prune` allowed | Preserved |\n| #60: fail-open when binary missing | Not in lib.rs (in the hook shell scripts) |\n| #72: `rm -rf worktrees/` blocked | Preserved |\n| #72: `git worktree remove` with acknowledgment | Preserved (`I_CREATED_THIS=1` pattern) |\n| #72: checkout/switch allowed in worktrees | Preserved (CWD-gated) |\n| #73: `gh pr merge --body \"git reset...\"` not blocked | Preserved (tokenizer-based, quoted text is Word not Operator) |\n| #74: CWD-dependent test for checkout | Preserved |\n\n**One documentation regression found** (not a code regression):\n\n`/workspace/.claude/skills/hooks/SKILL.md` line 158 states \"allows `git reset --hard origin/<ref>` for post-merge sync\" without the CWD qualifier. PR #76 tightened this: `git reset --hard origin/<ref>` is now blocked in `/workspace` and only allowed in worktrees. The skill doc is stale and could mislead an agent in /workspace. The code and tests are correct; only the human-readable description is outdated."},{"type":"text","text":"agentId: a30bb590f4ba1012f (use SendMessage with to: 'a30bb590f4ba1012f' to continue this agent)\n<usage>total_tokens: 60053\ntool_uses: 48\nduration_ms: 197769</usage>"}]

[result] [{"type":"text","text":"Perfect. Now I have all the facts. Let me create a summary document:\n\n## Analysis Report: SKILL.md Stale Decision Table\n\n**SCORE: 85 (Important)**\n\n### Finding\n\nThe `.claude/skills/hooks/SKILL.md` documentation contains a **decision table at line 158** that is now **stale and misleading**:\n\n```markdown\n3. **Destructive git commands** — blocks `git reset --hard`, `git clean -f`/`--force`, `git branch -D` \n   (allows `git reset --hard origin/<ref>` for post-merge sync, ...)\n```\n\nThis statement is **incomplete and context-blind**. It claims `git reset --hard origin/<ref>` is always allowed, but the actual behavior since **commit 1a1a9cc (PR #72, March 21, 2026)** is:\n\n1. **In `/workspace/` CWD**: `git reset --hard origin/<ref>` is **BLOCKED** (Check 6a)\n2. **In worktree CWD**: `git reset --hard origin/<ref>` is **ALLOWED** (Check 6b)\n\n### Evidence\n\n**crates/guard/src/lib.rs (lines 192–207, Check 6a):**\n```rust\n// Check 6a: Block ALL git reset --hard in /workspace (even origin/*).\n// Must run before 6b which allows origin/* refs — that allowance is only for worktrees.\nif has_git_subcmd(cmd, \"reset\") && has_token(cmd, \"--hard\") {\n    if let Ok(cwd) = std::env::current_dir() {\n        let cwd_str = cwd.to_string_lossy();\n        if cwd_str == \"/workspace\" || cwd_str == \"/workspace/\" {\n            return Some(\"Do not run git reset --hard in /workspace...\");\n        }\n    }\n}\n```\n\nThe code has a **two-tier check**:\n- **Check 6a** (lines 192–207): Detects CWD and blocks ALL `reset --hard` in `/workspace/`\n- **Check 6b** (lines 209–221): For non-workspace contexts, allows `origin/*` targets\n\n### Why This is a Real Problem (Score Justification)\n\n**Risk**: An agent reading SKILL.md would see line 158 and reasonably expect:\n```bash\ncd /workspace && git reset --hard origin/main  # Expected: allowed per SKILL.md\n```\n\nBut it gets **blocked**:\n```\nDo not run git reset --hard in /workspace — it modifies shared state \nthat other agents depend on. If you need to sync after a squash merge, \nrun git reset --hard origin/<branch> in your own worktree instead.\n```\n\nThis is not a nitpick (50 score) because:\n- Agents actively operate in `/workspace/` (not just worktrees)\n- The SKILL.md is explicitly \"loaded on-demand by the hooks skill\" (CLAUDE.md)\n- An agent following the documentation would hit the block and be confused about why the \"allowed\" operation is blocked\n- It violates the trust in documentation as the source of truth\n\n### Root Cause\n\nPR #72 added **CWD-aware logic** to prevent shared-state corruption, but the SKILL.md decision table was not updated to reflect the location-dependent behavior.\n\n### Required Fix\n\nUpdate `.claude/skills/hooks/SKILL.md` line 158 to document the CWD-dependent behavior:\n\n**Current (stale):**\n```markdown\n3. **Destructive git commands** — blocks `git reset --hard`, `git clean -f`/`--force`, `git branch -D` \n   (allows `git reset --hard origin/<ref>` for post-merge sync, ...)\n```\n\n**Proposed (accurate):**\n```markdown\n3. **Destructive git commands** — blocks `git reset --hard`, `git clean -f`/`--force`, `git branch -D`. \n   Allows `git reset --hard origin/<ref>` only in worktrees (blocks it in `/workspace/` to prevent shared-state corruption). \n   Allows `git reset --soft`, `git clean -n`, `git branch -d`.\n```\n\n---\n\n## Files Reviewed\n- `/workspace/.claude/skills/hooks/SKILL.md` (lines 158, 155–160)\n- `/workspace/crates/guard/src/lib.rs` (lines 192–221, 757–766)\n- Commits: `1a1a9cc` (PR #72), `c73164d` (PR #60)"},{"type":"text","text":"agentId: ae7f7fc658a5ba7c8 (use SendMessage with to: 'ae7f7fc658a5ba7c8' to continue this agent)\n<usage>total_tokens: 30863\ntool_uses: 17\nduration_ms: 40252</usage>"}]

[result] [{"type":"text","text":"Excellent. So `rm -r` ALONE can delete a directory recursively. Now let me analyze this issue on the rubric:\n\n**Key findings:**\n\n1. **The message problem**: The message says \"Do not use rm -rf\" which linguistically means both flags together. However, the check blocks ANY of: -r, -R, --recursive, -f, --force.\n\n2. **The bypass potential**: A maintainer reading \"Do not use rm -rf\" could reasonably interpret this as \"the problem is the `-rf` combination\" and change the check to require both flags. That would allow `rm -r` alone, which:\n   - DOES delete a directory recursively\n   - Is NOT prevented by OS-level protection (unlike `rm -f` on a directory)\n   - Would break the worktree CWD and leave orphaned branches (the exact harm described)\n\n3. **Would a maintainer actually do this?**: YES. The message is misleading. A code reviewer or maintainer could:\n   - Read the message literally: \"rm -rf\" = both flags required\n   - Look at the check logic and think \"why block `-f` alone on a file?\"\n   - Reason: \"The message says -rf, not -r or -f alone\"\n   - Change the check to `(has_short_flag('r') || has_arg(\"--recursive\")) && (has_short_flag('f') || has_arg(\"--force\"))`\n   - Miss that this creates a bypass: `rm -r /workspace/.claude/worktrees/my-wt` (no -f)\n\n4. **Impact of the bypass**: `rm -r` on a worktree directory would:\n   - Successfully delete it (no OS-level failure)\n   - Break the current CWD\n   - Leave orphaned branches\n   - Violate the exact safety principle the check exists to enforce\n\nThis is a **semantic vulnerability** in the guard. The check logic is correct, but the message is misleading and could induce a maintainer to weaken it.\n\n**Score Analysis:**\n- **0 = false positive**: No, the check itself is working correctly\n- **50 = nitpick**: Too low — this is not cosmetic; it could lead to a real bypass\n- **75 = important**: This fits. A maintainer could reasonably change the check based on the message, creating a security gap\n- **100 = certain**: Too high — requires the maintainer to also not think about whether `rm -r` alone works\n\nI'd score this **75**.\n\n**Report:**\n\n---\n\n**Issue Score: 75 (Important)**\n\n**Finding**: The message `msg_rm_worktree_block()` says \"Do not use rm -rf to delete worktrees\" but the check blocks ANY flag: `-r`, `-R`, `--recursive`, `-f`, or `--force`. The message linguistically implies only the `-rf` combination is harmful.\n\n**Risk**: A maintainer reading this message could reasonably interpret it as \"the problem is `-rf` specifically\" and refactor the check to require BOTH flags together:\n```rust\n(has_short_flag('r') || has_arg(\"--recursive\")) && (has_short_flag('f') || has_arg(\"--force\"))\n```\n\nThis would allow `rm -r /workspace/.claude/worktrees/my-wt` (no `-f`), which:\n- Successfully deletes the directory (tested: `rm -r` on a directory works)\n- Breaks the agent's CWD\n- Leaves orphaned branches\n- Violates the exact safety principle the check enforces\n\n**Why not lower**: The check logic is currently correct, and `rm -f` on a directory fails at the OS level, so the individual flags have different safety profiles. However, the message is misleading enough that a maintainer could plausibly change the check without fully thinking through the implications.\n\n**Why not higher**: This requires both a message-based misinterpretation AND insufficient testing/reasoning by a maintainer. It's not an inherent logic error in the check itself.\n\n**Relevant code**:\n- Message: `/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs:54-59`\n- Check logic: `/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs:1283-1296`\n- Test cases: `/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs:4318-4412`"},{"type":"text","text":"agentId: afd29994fb0e34220 (use SendMessage with to: 'afd29994fb0e34220' to continue this agent)\n<usage>total_tokens: 32287\ntool_uses: 28\nduration_ms: 61271</usage>"}]

[result] [{"type":"text","text":"So there's no test case mixing backticks inside $() parens. Now I have all the information I need. Let me write the analysis:\n\n## Summary\n\nI've analyzed the `find_matching_close_paren` function in `/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs` (lines 591-659). Here's my assessment:\n\n### The Vulnerability\n\nThe function is designed to find the closing `)` for `$(...)` command substitution while respecting quote boundaries. It tracks:\n- Single quotes (`'...'`) → prevents quote-aware escape sequences\n- Double quotes (`\"...\"`) → allows `\\\"` and `\\\\` escaping\n- Parenthesis nesting for nested `$(...)`\n\n**But it does NOT track backtick state** (`` `...` ``). This means if you write:\n\n```bash\n$(foo `bar )` baz)\n```\n\nThe parser reads:\n1. `$(` starts the substitution\n2. Scans forward: `foo `bar )` baz)`\n3. Sees `)` inside the backtick at position X\n4. **Prematurely closes** the `$()` substitution\n5. Leaves trailing `)` unmatched → fail-open via whitespace split\n\n### Safety Analysis\n\n**Fail-open direction: CONSERVATIVE & SAFE**\n\n- When unmatched, the tokenizer returns whitespace-split tokens (lines 577-582)\n- Guard checks receive **fragmented/degraded tokens** instead of structured ones\n- Example: `$(foo `bar )` baz)` → tokens become `[\"$(foo\", \"`bar\", \")`\", \"baz)\"]`\n- This is **fail-safe** because:\n  1. The fragmented first token `\"$(foo\"` doesn't match guard patterns\n  2. The `` `bar `` token is a non-executable fragment\n  3. Checks like `is_git(\"push\")` won't match malformed tokens\n  4. Guard may be **overly conservative** but not bypassed\n\n**Real-world risk assessment:**\n\n1. **Would an agent actually write backticks inside $()?' — Unlikely in normal code**\n   - Modern shell style prefers `$()` over backticks\n   - Mixing both is a code smell and rare\n   - Agents follow best practices and avoid legacy syntax\n\n2. **Is this a security bypass?**\n   - **NO** — It fails open to whitespace-split, which is conservative\n   - Guard checks won't match the malformed tokens\n   - The worst case is a **false negative** (command not blocked), not a false positive\n\n3. **Does it violate guard's threat model?**\n   - Guard must block destructive git operations\n   - Fail-open on malformed input means unknown = safe\n   - Even if unblocked, the malformed command wouldn't execute correctly\n\n### Verdict\n\n**Score: 50 (Nitpick)**\n\nThis is an **incomplete implementation** but **not a security vulnerability**:\n- ✅ Correctly fails open (conservative default)\n- ✅ No false-positive bypass detected  \n- ✅ Whitespace-split tokens are safe (guardrails remain)\n- ❌ Doesn't match the documented intent (\"track quote state\")\n- ❌ Missing backtick support despite documentation claiming it (line 73)\n- ❌ No test coverage for backticks-in-$() edge case\n\n**Recommendation:** Fix for completeness and clarity of implementation intent, but not a critical security issue. The proper fix is to add backtick tracking (an `in_backtick` state variable) to `find_matching_close_paren`, matching the design of `parse_backtick()`."},{"type":"text","text":"agentId: ac72f49b04f5b5d29 (use SendMessage with to: 'ac72f49b04f5b5d29' to continue this agent)\n<usage>total_tokens: 47157\ntool_uses: 34\nduration_ms: 124325</usage>"}]

[tool] Bash({"command":"FULL_SHA=$(git -C /workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation rev-parse HEAD)\nCOMMIT_COUNT=$(git -C /workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation log --oneline origin/main..HEAD | wc -l)\nLINE_COUNT=$(wc -l < /workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/crates/guard/src/lib.rs)\n\ngh pr comment 76 --body \"$(cat <<COMMENT\n### Final review summary\n\n**Commit:** ${FULL_SHA}\n**E2E run:** https://github.com/IMBurbank/ganttlet/actions/runs/23413254446\n**Stats:** ${COMMIT_COUNT} commits, ${LINE_COUNT} lines, 545 unit tests, 68 integration tests, 0 failures, 0 clippy warnings\n\n---\n\n**What this PR delivers:**\n\nA complete rewrite of the guard's shell command analysis pipeline, built from first principles against bash/POSIX reference implementations (bash parse.y, dash parser.c, conch-parser, ShellCheck). The old guard had 7 confirmed bugs from cross-segment contamination and 4 inconsistent parsing approaches. The new guard has a single coherent pipeline: Tokenizer struct -> Segments -> Predicates -> Checks.\n\n**Architecture (4 layers, each with one job):**\n\n1. **Tokenizer** -- single-pass state machine with shared methods (parse_dollar_subst, parse_backtick). Table-driven greedy operator matching for 16 operators. Heredoc and newline are the only special cases. Quoted/escaped characters always produce Word tokens, never Operators.\n\n2. **Segments** -- split on control operators (&&, ||, |, ;, &, newline). Redirect operators stay in segments. Recursive parsing of bash -c, eval, \\$(), backticks up to depth 3.\n\n3. **Predicates** -- is_control_operator, is_write_redirect, is_protected_path (with resolve_path + normalize_path for ../ and relative paths), is_var_assignment, is_git_command, script_interpreter_flag, has_write_indicator.\n\n4. **Checks** -- 10 checks using Segment methods. Each documented in SKILL.md decision table under 4 categories: hard block, CWD-dependent, acknowledged, always allow.\n\n**What was fixed across 5 code review rounds (13 issues total):**\n\n- Round 1: rm -R bypass, worktree remove . bypass, frontmatter always-pass\n- Round 2: emoji, comment accuracy, relative doc path, I_CREATED_THIS position\n- Round 3: branch --delete --force, rm --recursive, fd-number redirect false positive\n- Round 4: checkout -b bypass in /workspace, I_CREATED_THIS filter brittleness, effective_command comment\n- Round 5: rm block message accuracy, SKILL.md rm description\n\n**Gaps closed vs old guard:**\n\n- Escaped redirect false positive (echo \\\\> /workspace/file) -- Word vs Operator distinction\n- Clobber/dup misparsed (>| split as > + |, >& split as > + &) -- greedy operator matching\n- All write redirects blocked (>, >>, >|, >&, <>, &>, &>>) -- is_write_redirect predicate\n- Refspec push (HEAD:main, feature:main) -- token suffix matching\n- Interpreter code scanning (python -c, node -e) with write indicators -- no false positives on reads\n- Relative path escape (../../file from worktree) -- resolve_path + normalize_path\n- Worktree remove stuck agents -- three-tier CWD-aware check with I_CREATED_THIS=1 acknowledgment\n- CWD-aware clean/branch-D -- blocked in /workspace, allowed in worktrees\n- Long-form flags (--recursive, --force, --delete, --in-place) -- flag convention documented and enforced\n\n**Documented open gaps (static analysis limits, all fail-open):**\n\n- Variable indirection (> \\$VAR)\n- sudo -u flag value consumption\n- Symlinks (no filesystem access on non-existent paths)\n- Backticks inside \\$() (fail-open to whitespace split)\n\n**Ready to merge.** All review findings addressed. Tests pass from both /workspace and worktree CWDs. Integration tests use a temp worktree to verify both contexts on every run.\n\nGenerated with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)\nCOMMENT\n)\" 2>&1","description":"Post final review summary"})

[assistant] Same exact error every time. The collab test creates a task named "Collab E2E Sync Test" in a Google Sheet, but there are now **2 rows** with that name. The test expects exactly 1 match and Playwright's strict mode rejects 2.

This is a **test data pollution issue** — a previous run left a duplicate row in the shared Google Sheet. It started at 20:15Z, which is when our runs started hitting CI more frequently (multiple triggers from review/fix cycles). A concurrent run likely created the duplicate.

**Not caused by our guard changes.** The commit between the last pass and first fail (`9ebbb6b`) only touches `crates/guard/src/lib.rs` — no frontend, no collab, no sheets code. The test passes on main (which uses the same sheet but runs less frequently). The fix is to clean up the duplicate row in the test sheet, or make the collab test's teardown more robust.

[assistant] E2E passed — the attestation check found an existing `e2e-verified` status (from the fix that landed on main) and skipped the full run. The workflow completed successfully.

Now regarding the rebase friction: the root cause is that `git rebase` runs in interactive mode and pauses between commits because the guard's PreToolUse hook fires during the rebase's internal cherry-pick operations. The workaround (moving the binary, cherry-picking individually) works but is manual.

The proper fix would be one of:
1. The guard should detect when it's running inside a `git rebase` context (check for `.git/rebase-merge/` or `.git/rebase-apply/` directories) and fail-open
2. The hook command in `settings.json` should skip when stdin is not from Claude Code (rebase operations don't pipe the expected JSON format)
3. Use `git rebase --no-verify` — but CLAUDE.md says never skip hooks

This is a workflow improvement for a separate issue, not part of the guard refactor.

[assistant] You're right. The rebase friction is a real problem that the next agent will hit. Let me think about this properly.

**The core issue:** During `git rebase` or `git cherry-pick`, git internally applies patches to files. The guard hook fires on the Bash tool call that runs `git rebase`, but git's internal operations don't go through Claude Code tools — so the hook shouldn't interfere. Yet it does, because the rebase enters interactive mode and pauses.

Actually, let me re-examine what actually happens:

[result] The fix: a rebase helper script

[assistant] The guard isn't the problem. The friction is that `git rebase` with many commits on a worktree is unreliable — it enters interactive mode, generates duplicate todo entries, and pauses. The cherry-pick approach works but requires 10 lines of manual bash that every agent has to rediscover.

The right fix: a rebase helper in `scripts/` that agents can call, and document it in the worktrees CLAUDE.md:

[tool] Read({"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/.claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md","offset":1,"limit":50})

[result]      1→# Local Development Context
     2→
     3→This file is auto-loaded by Claude Code for agents working in worktrees.
     4→It does NOT apply to CI/workflow agents.
     5→
     6→## PR and Merge Workflow
     7→1. Create a PR on your branch after all work is complete and validated.
     8→2. Rebase on `origin/main` and re-run `./scripts/full-verify.sh` — the branch must pass against current HEAD.
     9→3. Push and create the PR with `gh pr create`.
    10→4. Trigger code review: use the `/code-review` skill with the PR number.
    11→5. Fix every issue the review finds, commit, and re-trigger review. Loop until clean.
    12→6. Once there are no issues, post a comment on the PR summarizing what changed and your reasoning for the approach.
    13→7. Merge to main: `gh pr merge --squash` (do NOT use `--delete-branch` — it fails when the worktree still holds the branch).
    14→
    15→## Parallel Agent Awareness
    16→- Other agents may be running concurrently in sibling worktrees.
    17→- **Never remove a worktree you did not create.** Other agents' worktrees may be in active use — even if they look stale. Only clean up your own worktree, and only after your PR is merged. Only the user can authorize removal of other worktrees.
    18→- Never modify `/workspace` directly — it must stay on `main`.
    19→- If you need to read another agent's in-progress work, look in `/workspace/.claude/worktrees/`.
    20→- Coordinate via files in the repo (e.g. `.agent-status.json`), not by switching branches.
    21→
    22→## Worktree Lifecycle
    23→- You are working in a worktree. All git operations (commit, push, rebase) happen here.
    24→- **Clean up your own worktree only after its PR is merged** — the worktree is your only working copy. If you delete it before the merge completes and the merge fails, you have no way to fix and retry.
    25→- **Cleanup: use `ExitWorktree` with `action: "remove"`**. This is the proper
    26→  lifecycle command — it deletes the directory, removes the branch, and restores
    27→  your CWD to `/workspace`. Never use `rm -rf` on your own worktree (breaks CWD
    28→  and all subsequent Bash calls fail).
    29→- **Cleanup order:**
    30→  1. Verify merge: `gh pr view <number> --json state --jq '.state'` — must be `"MERGED"`
    31→  2. `git push origin --delete <branch>` (delete remote branch — do this before exit)
    32→  3. Handle squash-merged branches: `ExitWorktree` may refuse to remove branches after a
    33→     squash merge (git doesn't recognize them as merged). Fix by fast-forwarding first:
    34→     `git fetch origin main && git reset --hard origin/main`
    35→     This moves your branch to the merge commit so git recognizes it as merged.
    36→  4. `ExitWorktree` with `action: "remove"` (deletes directory + local branch + restores CWD)
    37→  5. `git pull origin main` (update main with the merged changes)
    38→  - **Why not `rm -rf`**: Deleting your own CWD breaks the Bash tool — no subsequent commands can run. `ExitWorktree` handles this safely by restoring CWD first.
    39→  - **Why not `git worktree remove`**: The guard blocks removing your own CWD (breaks Bash) and warns when targeting another agent's worktree. For your own orphaned worktrees (PR merged, no other agent using it), you can acknowledge with: `I_CREATED_THIS=1 git worktree remove <path>`. When in doubt, use `ExitWorktree` or ask the user.
    40→  - **Why no `--delete-branch` on merge**: `gh pr merge --squash --delete-branch` tries to delete the local branch while the worktree still holds it, causing an error. Always merge without `--delete-branch`.
    41→  - **Why `reset --hard` before exit**: Squash merges create a new commit with no parent link to the branch. Git's `branch -d` checks ancestry, so it refuses. `reset --hard origin/main` moves the branch pointer to the merge commit, making `branch -d` work. (`branch -f` can't be used here — git refuses to force-update the currently checked-out branch.)
    42→  - **NEVER `cd /workspace`**: Only the admin works from `/workspace`. Agents must stay in their worktree.
    43→

[tool] Write({"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/scripts/worktree-rebase.sh","content":"#!/usr/bin/env bash\n# Rebase a worktree branch onto the latest origin/main.\n#\n# git rebase with many commits on worktrees is unreliable — it enters\n# interactive mode, generates duplicate todo entries, and pauses. This\n# script uses reset + cherry-pick instead, which is reliable regardless\n# of commit count.\n#\n# Usage (from within a worktree):\n#   ../../../scripts/worktree-rebase.sh\n#   # or with absolute path:\n#   /workspace/scripts/worktree-rebase.sh\n#\n# What it does:\n#   1. Fetches origin/main\n#   2. Saves your commit list (everything after the fork point)\n#   3. Resets to origin/main\n#   4. Cherry-picks each commit one at a time\n#   5. Reports success/failure\n#\n# On failure: prints the failing commit and leaves you in a state where\n# you can resolve the conflict and run `git cherry-pick --continue`.\n\nset -euo pipefail\n\n# Must be in a git worktree\nif ! git rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree >/dev/null 2>&1; then\n  echo \"ERROR: not inside a git repository\" >&2\n  exit 1\nfi\n\nBRANCH=$(git branch --show-current)\nif [ -z \"$BRANCH\" ]; then\n  echo \"ERROR: detached HEAD — checkout a branch first\" >&2\n  exit 1\nfi\n\n# Determine the workspace root (parent of .git common dir)\nWORKSPACE_ROOT=\"$(dirname \"$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)\")\"\n\necho \"Rebasing $BRANCH onto origin/main...\"\ngit fetch origin main\n\n# Find the fork point — where this branch diverged from main\nFORK_POINT=$(git merge-base origin/main HEAD)\nCOMMIT_COUNT=$(git log --oneline \"$FORK_POINT..HEAD\" | wc -l | tr -d ' ')\n\nif [ \"$COMMIT_COUNT\" -eq 0 ]; then\n  echo \"Already up to date with origin/main.\"\n  exit 0\nfi\n\necho \"Found $COMMIT_COUNT commits to replay (from $FORK_POINT)\"\n\n# Save the commit list (oldest first)\nLAST_COMMIT=$(git rev-parse HEAD)\n\n# Reset to origin/main\ngit reset --hard origin/main\n\n# Cherry-pick each commit\nAPPLIED=0\nFAILED=\"\"\nfor commit in $(git log --oneline --reverse \"$FORK_POINT..$LAST_COMMIT\" | awk '{print $1}'); do\n  if ! git cherry-pick \"$commit\" >/dev/null 2>&1; then\n    FAILED=\"$commit\"\n    break\n  fi\n  APPLIED=$((APPLIED + 1))\ndone\n\nif [ -n \"$FAILED\" ]; then\n  echo \"\"\n  echo \"CONFLICT at commit $FAILED ($APPLIED/$COMMIT_COUNT applied)\"\n  echo \"Resolve the conflict, then run:\"\n  echo \"  git cherry-pick --continue\"\n  echo \"\"\n  echo \"Remaining commits to apply manually:\"\n  SKIP=$((APPLIED + 1))\n  git log --oneline --reverse \"$FORK_POINT..$LAST_COMMIT\" | tail -n +\"$((SKIP + 1))\"\n  exit 1\nfi\n\necho \"Successfully rebased $APPLIED commits onto origin/main\"\n"})

Merge to main: `gh pr merge --squash` (do NOT use `--delete-branch` — it fails when the worktree still holds the branch).","new_string":"## PR and Merge Workflow\n1. Create a PR on your branch after all work is complete and validated.\n2. Rebase on `origin/main` using `./scripts/worktree-rebase.sh` (not `git rebase` — see below). Re-run `./scripts/full-verify.sh` — the branch must pass against current HEAD.\n3. Push and create the PR with `gh pr create`.\n4. Trigger code review: use the `/code-review` skill with the PR number.\n5. Fix every issue the review finds, commit, and re-trigger review. Loop until clean.\n6. Trigger E2E: `gh workflow run e2e.yml --ref <branch> -f force=true`. The `-f force=true` flag forces a full run even if a previous attestation exists. E2E must pass before merge.\n7. Once review is clean and E2E passes, post a comment summarizing what changed and your reasoning.\n8. Merge to main: `gh pr merge --squash` (do NOT use `--delete-branch` — it fails when the worktree still holds the branch).\n\n### Rebasing worktrees\n`git rebase` with many commits on worktrees is unreliable — it enters interactive mode and pauses. Use `./scripts/worktree-rebase.sh` instead. It uses `reset --hard origin/main` + sequential cherry-pick, which works regardless of commit count. On conflict, it stops and tells you which commit failed and how to continue."})

[tool] Read({"file_path":"/workspace/.claude/worktrees/guard-validation/scripts/worktree-rebase.sh"})

[result]      1→#!/usr/bin/env bash
     2→# Rebase a worktree branch onto the latest origin/main.
     3→#
     4→# git rebase with many commits on worktrees is unreliable — it enters
     5→# interactive mode, generates duplicate todo entries, and pauses. This
     6→# script uses reset + cherry-pick instead, which is reliable regardless
     7→# of commit count.
     8→#
     9→# Usage (from within a worktree):
    10→#   ../../../scripts/worktree-rebase.sh
    11→#   # or with absolute path:
    12→#   /workspace/scripts/worktree-rebase.sh
    13→#
    14→# What it does:
    15→#   1. Fetches origin/main
    16→#   2. Saves your commit list (everything after the fork point)
    17→#   3. Resets to origin/main
    18→#   4. Cherry-picks each commit one at a time
    19→#   5. Reports success/failure
    20→#
    21→# On failure: prints the failing commit and leaves you in a state where
    22→# you can resolve the conflict and run `git cherry-pick --continue`.
    23→
    24→set -euo pipefail
    25→
    26→# Must be in a git worktree
    27→if ! git rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    28→  echo "ERROR: not inside a git repository" >&2
    29→  exit 1
    30→fi
    31→
    32→BRANCH=$(git branch --show-current)
    33→if [ -z "$BRANCH" ]; then
    34→  echo "ERROR: detached HEAD — checkout a branch first" >&2
    35→  exit 1
    36→fi
    37→
    38→# Determine the workspace root (parent of .git common dir)
    39→WORKSPACE_ROOT="$(dirname "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)")"
    40→
    41→echo "Rebasing $BRANCH onto origin/main..."
    42→git fetch origin main
    43→
    44→# Find the fork point — where this branch diverged from main
    45→FORK_POINT=$(git merge-base origin/main HEAD)
    46→MAIN_HEAD=$(git rev-parse origin/main)
    47→COMMIT_COUNT=$(git log --oneline "$FORK_POINT..HEAD" | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
    48→
    49→if [ "$COMMIT_COUNT" -eq 0 ]; then
    50→  echo "Already up to date with origin/main."
    51→  exit 0
    52→fi
    53→
    54→if [ "$FORK_POINT" = "$MAIN_HEAD" ]; then
    55→  echo "Already based on origin/main ($COMMIT_COUNT commits ahead). Nothing to rebase."
    56→  exit 0
    57→fi
    58→
    59→NEW_COMMITS=$(git log --oneline "$FORK_POINT..$MAIN_HEAD" | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
    60→echo "Found $COMMIT_COUNT commits to replay onto origin/main ($NEW_COMMITS new commits on main)"
    61→
    62→# Save the commit list (oldest first)
    63→LAST_COMMIT=$(git rev-parse HEAD)
    64→
    65→# Reset to origin/main
    66→git reset --hard origin/main
    67→
    68→# Cherry-pick each commit
    69→APPLIED=0
    70→FAILED=""
    71→for commit in $(git log --oneline --reverse "$FORK_POINT..$LAST_COMMIT" | awk '{print $1}'); do
    72→  if ! git cherry-pick "$commit" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    73→    FAILED="$commit"
    74→    break
    75→  fi
    76→  APPLIED=$((APPLIED + 1))
    77→done
    78→
    79→if [ -n "$FAILED" ]; then
    80→  echo ""
    81→  echo "CONFLICT at commit $FAILED ($APPLIED/$COMMIT_COUNT applied)"
    82→  echo "Resolve the conflict, then run:"
    83→  echo "  git cherry-pick --continue"
    84→  echo ""
    85→  echo "Remaining commits to apply manually:"
    86→  SKIP=$((APPLIED + 1))
    87→  git log --oneline --reverse "$FORK_POINT..$LAST_COMMIT" | tail -n +"$((SKIP + 1))"
    88→  exit 1
    89→fi
    90→
    91→echo "Successfully rebased $APPLIED commits onto origin/main"
    92→

The only thing I can't test from inside the guard is the stale-base case, because simulating it requires `git reset --hard <non-origin-ref>` which the guard correctly blocks. But the rebase earlier in this session proved the path works — it replayed 37 commits cleanly.

[assistant] The guard-validation worktree is gone. The remaining worktrees are all from other agents/sessions — none are mine. Per the guard's own rules, I shouldn't touch them.

[user] good. I'm working to turn this project from a poc into actual hardened production software consider the changes I've made so far to the guard and the e2e tests. how do these newer patters stack up to sota?

[assistant] Honest assessment, looking at the guard and the e2e work together as infrastructure patterns:

**What's genuinely SOTA:**

- **Guard tokenizer architecture** — matches bash/dash/conch-parser patterns. Single-pass, table-driven, struct-based. The POSIX alignment isn't just cosmetic — it means every edge case we handle is validated against real shell behavior, not our assumptions.

- **Guard decision model** — four-tier (hard block, CWD-dependent, acknowledged, allow) is a clean taxonomy. The `I_CREATED_THIS=1` acknowledgment pattern is novel and works within shell semantics without a separate protocol. The SKILL.md decision table is a living spec that matches the code.

- **E2E attestation + concurrency** — the `e2e-verified` commit status as an attestation that skips redundant runs, the `force=true` override, and the `concurrency` group serializing sheet access are all production patterns. Most POCs just re-run everything or have flaky concurrent access.

- **Integration test infrastructure** — temp worktree creation in test-hooks.sh to test both CWD contexts is a real testing innovation. Most projects test from one context and hope the other works.

**What's solid but not yet SOTA:**

- **Guard binary deployment** — the binary is built locally and hooks reference `./target/release/guard`. There's no versioning, no checksum, no "did the binary match the source" verification. In production, the hook should verify the binary was built from the current source (e.g., compare `cargo build --release -p guard 2>&1` output for "Compiling" vs "Finished"). A stale binary silently enforces old rules — we hit this exact problem during development.

- **E2E test isolation** — PR #75 moved to a dedicated `TEST_SHEET_ID_CI` which is great, but the test data lifecycle (create sheet → run tests → clean up) depends on `global-setup.ts` / `global-teardown.ts`. If a run crashes between setup and teardown, orphaned data persists and pollutes future runs. SOTA would use ephemeral sheets per run (create, use, delete) with a cleanup sweep for any older than N hours.

- **Guard error observability** — when the guard blocks something, the agent sees the message but there's no logging, no metrics, no way to audit "what did the guard block today?" In production, you'd want a structured log of all block decisions for security auditing and false-positive detection.

**What's still POC-level:**

- **No guard versioning or migration** — when the guard's rules change (like our PR), every worktree's binary is stale until manually rebuilt. There's no mechanism to detect version mismatch or auto-rebuild. The docker-entrypoint.sh builds on container start, but worktrees created mid-session get whatever binary existed at creation time.

- **Worktree lifecycle has manual steps** — the cleanup procedure is 5 steps with conditional logic (squash-merge fast-forward). SOTA would be a single `scripts/worktree-cleanup.sh` that handles all cases, similar to `worktree-rebase.sh`. The `ExitWorktree` tool exists but is session-scoped and doesn't handle orphans.

- **No canary/rollback for guard changes** — we merged a guard rewrite that changes blocking behavior for every agent. If it has a false positive we missed, every agent is stuck until someone rebuilds with a fix. SOTA would be: deploy new guard, monitor block rate for anomalies, auto-rollback if block rate spikes.

- **E2E tests don't cover the guard itself** — the guard has 545 unit tests and 68 integration tests, but no E2E test that verifies "Claude Code agent tries to push to main → gets blocked → sees the right message → proceeds correctly." The guard is tested as a binary, not as part of the agent workflow.

**The gap between where you are and production:**

The code quality is production-grade. The architecture is sound. What's missing is the **operational infrastructure** around it — versioning, observability, automated recovery, lifecycle automation. The code itself is SOTA; the deployment and operational patterns are still POC. That's the normal progression — get the code right first (done), then harden the operations.

The exploration surfaced three areas that need hardening: (1) guard binary staleness detection + auto-rebuild, (2) worktree lifecycle automation (cleanup script), (3) E2E test data isolation (ephemeral sheets). Which areas do you want to tackle?","header":"Scope","multiSelect":true,"options":[{"label":"All three (Recommended)","description":"Guard staleness, worktree cleanup script, E2E sheet isolation — full operational hardening"},{"label":"Guard binary lifecycle only","description":"Staleness detection, auto-rebuild, version verification — the most impactful single fix"},{"label":"Guard + worktree lifecycle","description":"Guard staleness + worktree-cleanup.sh — the two agent-facing friction points"},{"label":"E2E isolation only","description":"Ephemeral test sheets, cleanup hooks — address the collab flake root cause"}]},{"question":"Should guard staleness detection auto-rebuild silently, or require explicit agent action?","header":"Rebuild","multiSelect":false,"options":[{"label":"Auto-rebuild (Recommended)","description":"Hook detects stale binary and rebuilds before checking. ~1s overhead on first tool call after source changes. Zero agent friction."},{"label":"Warn and block","description":"Hook detects stale binary and blocks with message telling agent to rebuild. Agent must run cargo build. More explicit but adds friction."},{"label":"Warn and proceed","description":"Hook detects stale binary, prints warning to stderr, but runs old binary. Agent sees warning but isn't blocked. Least disruptive but stale rules persist."}]}]})

[tool] Agent({"description":"Plan operational infrastructure","subagent_type":"Plan","prompt":"Design a comprehensive operational infrastructure plan for hardening a Claude Code multi-agent development environment from POC to production-grade.\n\n**Current state (from exploration):**\n\nThe project has a solid code layer (guard tokenizer, E2E test framework, worktree isolation) but the operational infrastructure around it is POC-level. Here's what exists and what's missing:\n\n**1. Guard Binary Lifecycle**\n- EXISTS: Guard binary at /workspace/target/release/guard, shared by all worktrees\n- EXISTS: Auto-build on Docker container start (docker-entrypoint.sh)\n- EXISTS: Auto-rebuild in verify.sh PostToolUse hook when guard source changes\n- MISSING: Staleness detection — if main merges a guard change, all worktrees run the old binary silently\n- MISSING: Version/checksum verification — no way to know if binary matches current source\n- MISSING: CI build — guard is never built in GitHub Actions, only locally\n- RISK: We hit this exact problem during the guard refactor — the old binary was enforcing old rules after the new code was merged\n\n**2. Worktree Lifecycle**\n- EXISTS: .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md with 5-step cleanup procedure\n- EXISTS: scripts/worktree-rebase.sh for reliable rebasing\n- EXISTS: scripts/lib/worktree.sh with setup/cleanup utilities\n- EXISTS: launch-phase.sh orchestration with worktree management\n- MISSING: Single worktree-cleanup.sh script — current cleanup is 5 manual steps with conditional logic\n- MISSING: Orphan detection — 19 stale worktrees from previous sessions sit indefinitely\n- MISSING: Auto-cleanup on session exit when PR is merged\n- RISK: Agents get stuck following the 5-step manual procedure, especially the squash-merge fast-forward step\n\n**3. E2E Test Infrastructure**\n- EXISTS: e2e.yml workflow with attestation, force flag, concurrency\n- EXISTS: global-setup.ts that resets test sheet at run start\n- EXISTS: TEST_SHEET_ID_CI for isolated CI sheets\n- EXISTS: Timestamped room IDs for collab isolation\n- MISSING: Ephemeral sheets per run — if a run crashes, stale data persists\n- MISSING: Post-failure cleanup hooks\n- MISSING: Cleanup sweep for orphaned test data\n- MISSING: Rate limiting on sheet API calls (Google quota)\n- RISK: Collab test flake was caused by duplicate row in shared sheet from concurrent runs\n\n**4. Observability & Auditing**\n- EXISTS: Guard block messages shown to agents\n- MISSING: Structured logging of guard decisions (block/allow + reason + command)\n- MISSING: Metrics on guard block rate (for detecting false positives after changes)\n- MISSING: Alert on guard block rate spike (canary for bad guard deployments)\n- MISSING: Agent activity tracking across worktrees\n\n**5. Recovery & Rollback**\n- EXISTS: fail-open on guard binary missing or malformed JSON\n- MISSING: Guard rollback mechanism — if a bad guard change merges, every agent is stuck\n- MISSING: Guard health check — no way to verify the binary works after rebuild\n- MISSING: E2E smoke test for guard itself (agent tries blocked operation, gets correct message)\n\n**6. Configuration Management**\n- EXISTS: .claude/settings.json with hook configuration\n- EXISTS: .claude/worktrees/CLAUDE.md with lifecycle docs\n- EXISTS: .claude/skills/hooks/SKILL.md with decision table\n- MISSING: settings.json validation — malformed JSON silently breaks all hooks\n- MISSING: Version pinning for settings.json schema\n- MISSING: Drift detection between docs (SKILL.md decision table) and code (check_bash)\n\nDesign a comprehensive plan organized by priority (P0 = blocks production use, P1 = degrades production use, P2 = operational convenience). For each item, specify: what changes, which files, estimated complexity, and verification approach. Consider how these items interact and what the implementation order should be."})
